topics.title: Basic Engine Parameters, Measurement of BHP and IHP
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Engine Parameters, Measurement of BHP and IHP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Basic Engine Parameters"?
In the context of Internal Combustion (IC) engines, **basic engine parameters** are the standardized geometric, thermodynamic, and performance quantities that completely describe an engine's size, capacity, working cycle, and output. Without these parameters, two engines cannot be compared, scaled, or rated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Basic Engine Parameters* refers to the geometric sizing of the cylinder (Bore, Stroke), its volumetric capacity (Swept Volume, Clearance Volume, Compression Ratio), the cyclic pressure work transfer (Mean Effective Pressure), and the resulting power outputs (IHP, BHP, FHP) along with associated efficiencies (Mechanical, Volumetric, Thermal).

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart of the Car"
Think of an engine cylinder as the **human heart**:
- **Bore (d)** is the *width* of the artery — wider bore means more blood (air-fuel mixture) per pump.
- **Stroke (L)** is the *length* of the heartbeat — how far the piston travels.
- **Swept Volume (Vs)** is the *volume of blood* ejected per heartbeat (the "engine displacement").
- **Compression Ratio (r)** is the *squeeze* given to the blood before it is expelled — a tighter squeeze releases more energy.
- **IHP** is the *total chemical energy converted* by the heart (true work done by gas).
- **BHP** is the *energy that actually reaches the wheels* — the useful output after the heart wastes some energy on its own internal friction.
- **FHP (Frictional Horse Power)** is the energy lost to the heart's own internal friction (bearings, pistons rings, etc.).

### 1.3 Core Terms — Quick Reference Box

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Parameter List (Module 1):**
> 1. **Bore (d)** — Cylinder internal diameter (*mm or m*)
> 2. **Stroke (L)** — Distance between TDC and BDC (*mm or m*)
> 3. **Swept Volume (Vs)** — Volume displaced by the piston
> 4. **Clearance Volume (Vc)** — Volume above piston at TDC
> 5. **Compression Ratio (r)** — Ratio of max to min cylinder volume
> 6. **Mean Effective Pressure (pm)** — Average pressure during the power stroke
> 7. **Indicated Power (IP / IHP)** — Power developed inside the cylinder
> 8. **Brake Power (BP / BHP)** — Power available at the crankshaft
> 9. **Friction Power (FP / FHP)** — Power lost to internal friction
> 10. **Mechanical Efficiency (ηm)** — Ratio BHP/IHP

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Parameters — The "Size" of the Engine

**a) Bore (d):**
The internal diameter of the engine cylinder, measured in **mm** or **m**. It determines the cross-sectional area through which the piston sweeps.

**b) Stroke Length (L):**
The linear distance traveled by the piston from **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** to **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)**. For a given crank radius **R**, we have:
$$L = 2R$$

**c) Swept Volume (Vs) — Displacement Volume:**
The volume swept by the piston as it moves from TDC to BDC. For one cylinder:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, L$$

For a multi-cylinder engine with **n** cylinders, total displacement:
$$V_{s,\text{total}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, L \, n$$

**d) Clearance Volume (Vc):**
The volume of the combustion chamber above the piston when the piston is at TDC. It includes the head gasket volume, piston crown cavity, and any pre-chamber volume.

**e) Cylinder Volume / Total Volume (V₁):**
$$V_1 = V_s + V_c$$

**f) Compression Ratio (r):**
A dimensionless number that quantifies how much the air-fuel mixture is compressed before ignition:
$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typical compression ratios: **Petrol (SI) engine → 8 to 12**, **Diesel (CI) engine → 14 to 22**. Higher r gives higher thermal efficiency (Otto cycle: η = 1 - 1/r^(γ-1)).

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Parameter — Mean Effective Pressure (pm)

**pm** is a *hypothetical* constant pressure that, if acted on the piston throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual variable-pressure cycle.

$$\text{Net Work per cycle} = p_m \times V_s$$

The concept of pm allows engineers to compare engines of *different sizes* using a single normalized parameter — bigger engine, bigger Vs → pm remains comparable.

### 2.3 Power Output Parameters — IHP, BHP, FHP

| Power | Where Measured | What it Represents |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| **IHP** | Inside the cylinder (via indicator diagram) | Gross power developed by gas pressure |
| **BHP** | At the crankshaft (via dynamometer) | Net useful power delivered |
| **FHP** | Difference (IHP − BHP) | Power lost to friction, pumping, auxiliaries |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Swept Volume (one cyl.) | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$ | m³ | d, L in meters |
| 2 | Compression Ratio | $r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | Stroke Length | $L = 2R$ | m | R = crank radius |
| 4 | Net Work / cycle | $W = p_m \, V_s$ | J | pm in N/m² |
| 5 | IHP (4-stroke) | $IHP = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{2 \times 60}$ | W (or HP) | divide by 746 for HP |
| 6 | IHP (2-stroke) | $IHP = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{60}$ | W | every rev = power stroke |
| 7 | BHP (Prony Brake) | $BHP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 746}$ | HP | T = torque in N·m |
| 8 | BHP (Rope Brake) | $BHP = \frac{(W - S) \, R \, N}{g \times 60 \times 746}$ | HP | W, S in N; R in m |
| 9 | FHP | $FHP = IHP - BHP$ | HP | by subtraction |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \frac{BHP}{IHP}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 11 | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{U}_p = \frac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s | N in rpm |
| 12 | Specific Fuel Cons. | $sfc = \frac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | lower = better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for k factor:**
> - 4-stroke engine → **k = 1/2** (power stroke once every **2** revolutions).
> - 2-stroke engine → **k = 1** (power stroke every **1** revolution).
> General: $IHP = p_m L A N \times n \times k / 60$ where k = ½ (4S) or 1 (2S).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Engine Selection:** pm is used by engine designers to compare two engines of different sizes — the one with higher pm is more "thermally loaded."
- **Emission Norms:** BS-VI, Euro 6, etc., all specify specific fuel consumption limits, which depend directly on BHP and engine displacement (Vs).
- **Vehicle Homologation:** ARAI (India) tests measure BHP at the wheel using chassis dynamometers to certify power ratings that appear in vehicle brochures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Indicated Horse Power (IHP)

**Given:**
- Number of cylinders = n
- Bore = d, Stroke = L, Area A = πd²/4
- Mean effective pressure = pm (N/m²)
- Engine speed = N (rpm)
- Working cycle = 4-stroke or 2-stroke

**Step 1 — Work done per cylinder per power stroke:**
Work done by gas pressure on the piston during one power stroke = mean pressure × swept volume.
$$W_{\text{per stroke}} = p_m \times V_s = p_m \times A \times L$$

**Step 2 — Number of power strokes per minute per cylinder:**
In a **4-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **2** revolutions → per minute: N/2.
In a **2-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **1** revolution → per minute: N.

**Step 3 — Work done per minute per cylinder:**
For 4-stroke:
$$W_{\text{4S}} = p_m \times A \times L \times \frac{N}{2}$$

For 2-stroke:
$$W_{\text{2S}} = p_m \times A \times L \times N$$

**Step 4 — Total work done per minute for n cylinders:**
$$W_{\text{total}} = p_m \times A \times L \times \frac{N}{2} \times n \quad (\text{4-stroke})$$
$$W_{\text{total}} = p_m \times A \times L \times N \times n \quad (\text{2-stroke})$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Power (Work/sec) by dividing by 60:**
$$\boxed{IHP_{(4S)} = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$
$$\boxed{IHP_{(2S)} = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert Watts to Metric Horsepower (1 HP = 735.5 W) or Mechanical HP (1 HP = 746 W):**
$$IHP_{(HP)} = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{2 \times 60 \times 746} \quad (4\text{-stroke})$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Brake Horse Power (BHP) — Prony Brake Method

A **Prony brake** is a friction-type dynamometer that absorbs engine power by applying a friction force on a rotating drum.

**Setup:**
- Drum rotates at N rpm
- A friction arm of length R (m) presses on the drum
- Weights W (N) applied at one end, balancing spring balance reading S (N)

**Step 1 — Net tangential force on the drum:**
The friction force creates a moment that the weights balance. Net effective force:
$$F_{net} = W - S$$

**Step 2 — Torque developed:**
$$T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \times R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

**Step 3 — Power = Torque × Angular Velocity:**
Angular velocity $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.

$$BP_{(\text{Watts})} = T \times \omega = (W - S) \times R \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to HP:**
$$\boxed{BHP = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 746} \quad [\text{HP}]}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Mechanical Efficiency (ηm)

By definition, mechanical efficiency is the ratio of useful output power to the total indicated power developed by the gas:
$$\eta_m = \frac{BHP}{IHP}$$

Since some power is always lost to friction:
$$FHP = IHP - BHP$$
$$\eta_m = \frac{IHP - FHP}{IHP} = 1 - \frac{FHP}{IHP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typical values: **ηm = 70% to 90%** for modern engines (higher for large marine engines, lower for small 2-stroke SI engines).

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Problem

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore = 80 mm, stroke = 100 mm, runs at 3000 rpm with a mean effective pressure of 8 bar. Mechanical efficiency is 80%. Find (a) IHP, (b) BHP, (c) FHP.

**Given:**
- n = 4, 4-stroke, d = 0.08 m, L = 0.1 m, N = 3000 rpm
- pm = 8 bar = 8 × 10⁵ N/m²
- ηm = 0.80

**Step 1 — Area A:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — IHP (Watts), 4-stroke:**
$$IHP = \frac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{2 \times 60}$$

$$IHP = \frac{(8 \times 10^5)(0.1)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3})(3000)(4)}{2 \times 60}$$

$$IHP = \frac{(8 \times 10^5)(0.1)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3})(12000)}{120}$$

Let us evaluate numerator:
$$8 \times 10^5 \times 0.1 = 8 \times 10^4$$
$$8 \times 10^4 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 402.12$$
$$402.12 \times 12000 = 4{,}825{,}440$$
$$4{,}825{,}440 / 120 = 40{,}212 \text{ W} \approx 40.21 \text{ kW}$$

In HP: $40{,}212 / 746 = 53.9 \text{ HP}$

**Step 3 — BHP:**
$$BHP = \eta_m \times IHP = 0.80 \times 40.21 = 32.17 \text{ kW}$$
$$BHP = 32{,}170 / 746 = 43.1 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 4 — FHP:**
$$FHP = IHP - BHP = 40.21 - 32.17 = 8.04 \text{ kW}$$

| Quantity | Value (kW) | Value (HP) |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| IHP | 40.21 | 53.9 |
| BHP | 32.17 | 43.1 |
| FHP | 8.04 | 10.8 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Engine Parameter Relationships — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Cylinder Geometry] --> B[Bore d]
    A --> C[Stroke L]
    B --> D[Swept Volume Vs = pi by 4 d squared L]
    C --> D
    E[Clearance Volume Vc] --> F[Compression Ratio r]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Thermal Efficiency]
    H[Mean Effective Pressure pm] --> I[Net Work per Cycle W = pm times Vs]
    D --> I
    I --> J[Indicated Power IP]
    K[Engine Speed N rpm] --> J
    L[Number of Cylinders n] --> J
    J --> M[Mechanical Efficiency nm]
    N[Dynamometer Reading] --> O[Brake Power BP]
    J --> P[Friction Power FP = IP minus BP]
    O --> P
    M --> Q[BHP = nm times IHP]
```

### 4.2 Prony Brake Dynamometer — Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineOutput[Engine Crankshaft Side]
        ENG[Engine Output Shaft] --> DRUM[Rotor Drum]
    end
    subgraph BrakeMechanism[Prony Brake Assembly]
        DRUM --> FRIC[Friction Lining]
        FRIC --> ARM[Brake Arm of length R]
        ARM --> PIVOT[Pivot Point]
    end
    subgraph LoadingSystem[Loading and Measurement]
        ARM --> WT[Dead Weight W in N]
        ARM --> SB[Spring Balance S in N]
    end
    subgraph Output[Computed Result]
        WT --> CALC[BHP Calculation]
        SB --> CALC
        CALC --> BHPVAL[Brake Horse Power BHP]
    end
```

### 4.3 IHP vs BHP vs FHP — Energy Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CHEM[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> IHPBOX[Indicated Power IHP]
    IHPBOX -->|Lost as FHP| FHPBOX[Friction Power FHP]
    IHPBOX -->|Available at Crankshaft| BHPBOX[Brake Power BHP]
    BHPBOX --> MECH[Mechanical Work at Wheels]
    style IHPBOX fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    style FHPBOX fill:#e06666,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style BHPBOX fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Indicator Diagram — p-V Curve Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ID[Ideal Indicator Diagram]
        P1[Point 1: BDC Start of Compression] --> P2[Point 2: TDC End of Compression]
        P2 --> P3[Point 3: TDC End of Combustion]
        P3 --> P4[Point 4: BDC End of Expansion]
        P4 --> P5[Point 1 again]
    end
    AREA[Area enclosed by loop] --> NETW[Net Work per Cycle]
    NETW --> PM[Mean Effective Pressure pm = Area divided by Vs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a real **indicator diagram**, the closed loop is traced on a p-V plot. The *area inside* the loop = net work per cycle. The pm is found by drawing a horizontal line such that the rectangle (pm × Vs) has the same area as the loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
Define the term **Mean Effective Pressure (pm)**. How is it related to the work done per cycle?

> **Model Answer:**
> Mean Effective Pressure (pm) is defined as a hypothetical constant pressure which, if acted on the piston throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual variable pressure during the cycle.
> Relation to work: $W_{\text{net per cycle}} = p_m \times V_s$ where Vs is the swept volume.
> Unit of pm is **N/m² (Pa)** or **bar**. (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). [3 marks]

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
Distinguish between **IHP**, **BHP**, and **FHP** with their physical significance.

> **Model Answer:**
> - **IHP (Indicated Horse Power):** Power actually developed by the working gas inside the cylinder; measured using an **engine indicator** (e.g., Maihak, Schaffer).
> - **BHP (Brake Horse Power):** Power available at the engine crankshaft for useful work; measured using a **dynamometer** (Prony brake, rope brake, hydraulic, eddy current).
> - **FHP (Friction Horse Power):** Power lost to overcome mechanical friction of pistons, bearings, valves, etc.; obtained by difference: **FHP = IHP − BHP**. [3 marks]

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**a)** Derive an expression for the Indicated Horse Power (IHP) of a multi-cylinder 4-stroke IC engine in terms of mean effective pressure. [7 marks]
**b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and stroke of 90 mm. It runs at 2800 rpm with a mean effective pressure of 7.5 bar. If mechanical efficiency is 82%, calculate the BHP and the indicated specific fuel consumption if fuel consumption is 12 kg/hr. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

> **Step 1:** Define work done per power stroke for one cylinder: $W = p_m \times A \times L$ where $A = \pi d^2/4$.
> **Step 2:** For a 4-stroke engine, number of power strokes per minute per cylinder = N/2.
> **Step 3:** Total work per minute for n cylinders: $W_{\text{total}} = p_m \times A \times L \times (N/2) \times n$.
> **Step 4:** Power in Watts: $IP = W_{\text{total}}/60 = \dfrac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{120}$.
> **Step 5:** Convert to HP: $\boxed{IHP = \dfrac{p_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{120 \times 746}}$ HP. (Award marks for each step.)

**Model Solution (b):**

> **Given:** n = 6, 4S, d = 0.075 m, L = 0.090 m, N = 2800 rpm, pm = 7.5 × 10⁵ N/m², ηm = 0.82, mf = 12 kg/hr.
>
> **Area A:** $A = \pi/4 \times (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}$ m².
>
> **IHP (Watts):**
> $IHP = \dfrac{(7.5 \times 10^5)(0.090)(4.418 \times 10^{-3})(2800)(6)}{120}$
>
> Numerator step-by-step:
> - $7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.090 = 67500$
> - $67500 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} = 298.215$
> - $298.215 \times 2800 = 8.35 \times 10^5$
> - $8.35 \times 10^5 \times 6 = 5.01 \times 10^6$
> - $5.01 \times 10^6 / 120 = 41{,}750$ W ≈ **41.75 kW**
>
> **BHP:**
> $BHP = \eta_m \times IHP = 0.82 \times 41.75 = 34.235$ kW ≈ **34.24 kW**
>
> **ISFC (Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption):**
> $ISFC = \dfrac{m_f}{IP \text{ (kW)}} = \dfrac{12 \text{ kg/hr}}{41.75 \text{ kW}} = 0.287 \text{ kg/kWh}$

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Prony brake dynamometer** and derive the expression for BHP. [7 marks]
**b)** A 4-cylinder, 2-stroke diesel engine has bore = 100 mm, stroke = 120 mm, runs at 1500 rpm, and develops a torque of 350 N·m. If the brake thermal efficiency is 30% and calorific value of fuel is 42000 kJ/kg, find the fuel consumption in kg/hr. [7 marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

> **Prony Brake Setup:** A rotating drum fixed on engine shaft, a friction block on a lever arm of length R clamped against the drum, dead weight W applied at one end, spring balance S supports the other end.
>
> **Derivation:**
> - Net force at drum: F = W − S
> - Torque: T = (W − S) × R
> - Angular velocity: ω = 2πN/60
> - Power (W): BP = Tω = (W − S)R × 2πN/60
> - In HP: $\boxed{BHP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 746}}$ (full 7 marks with sketch + derivation)

**Model Solution (b):**

> **Given:** n = 4, 2S, d = 0.1 m, L = 0.12 m, N = 1500 rpm, T = 350 N·m, ηbth = 0.30, CV = 42000 kJ/kg.
>
> **BHP (Watts):**
> $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 350}{60}$
>
> $= \dfrac{3{,}298{,}672.6}{60} = 54{,}977.9$ W ≈ **54.98 kW**
>
> **Heat input rate:**
> $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{BP}{\eta_{bth}} = \dfrac{54.98}{0.30} = 183.27$ kW
>
> **Fuel consumption:**
> $m_f = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{CV} = \dfrac{183.27 \text{ kJ/s}}{42000 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 4.364 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s
>
> In kg/hr: $m_f = 4.364 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = \boxed{15.71 \text{ kg/hr}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing k-factor:** Many students write IHP formula without the ½ for 4-stroke engines. **Always check the stroke type first.**
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing SI (Watts) with HP (746 W). State conversions explicitly.
> 3. **pm in bar vs N/m²:** pm in formulas MUST be in N/m². Convert bar → N/m² by multiplying by 10⁵.
> 4. **Prony brake formula sign:** Forgetting that if W is suspended (upward) and S is upward spring, net force = W − S. Get the sign wrong and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not drawing the indicator diagram** in IHP-related questions → loses at least 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mechanical efficiency definition:** Many students write BHP/IHP as ηm — correct. But several mistakenly write IHP/BHP. Examiners deduct full marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Bore (d)** = cylinder diameter; **Stroke (L)** = TDC to BDC distance; both in **mm** in vehicle specs.
- ✅ **Swept Volume $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$** — for **one** cylinder; multiply by **n** for total displacement.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio $r = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$** — petrol ≈ 10, diesel ≈ 18 (modern CRDi).
- ✅ **Mean Effective Pressure (pm)** — constant imaginary pressure giving same work as the cycle; enables cross-engine comparison.
- ✅ **IHP formula:** Multiply pm × L × A × N × n → divide by 120 (4S) or 60 (2S) → divide by 746 for HP.
- ✅ **BHP** is measured by **dynamometer** (Prony, rope, hydraulic, eddy current). IHP via **indicator diagram**.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency ηm = BHP / IHP**; **FHP = IHP − BHP**.
- ✅ **BSFC = mf / BP**; **ISFC = mf / IP** (lower is better; modern diesels ≈ 0.20 kg/kWh).
- ✅ **Prony Brake BHP = 2πN(W−S)R / (60 × 746)** — sign convention matters.
- ✅ **Higher ηm → better-designed engine** (typically 75–90% in modern automotive engines).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Testing on Dynamometers (Hydraulic, Eddy Current, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Testing on Dynamometers (Hydraulic, Eddy Current, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **dynamometer** (commonly abbreviated as *dyno*) is a precision mechanical instrument used to measure the **brake torque** and **rotational speed** simultaneously produced by a prime-mover (engine, motor, turbine, gearbox) under controlled loading conditions. The combination of torque $T$ (in N·m) and angular speed $N$ (in rpm) yields the **brake power** developed by the engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A dynamometer is the *heart* of any engine test cell. Every parameter required for engine performance assessment — brake power, brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC), brake thermal efficiency, volumetric efficiency, and mechanical efficiency — is either directly measured or derived from dynamometer readings.

The fundamental governing equation for any absorption dynamometer is:

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where $T$ is in N·m and $N$ is in rpm.

## 1.2 Classification of Dynamometers

Dynamometers are broadly classified into two families based on their function:

| Family | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Absorption Dynamometers** | Absorb (dissipate as heat) the power delivered by the engine | Hydraulic, Eddy-Current, Electrical (DC/AC), Mechanical Friction (Prony / Rope brake) |
| **Transmission (Drive) Dynamometers** | Measure transmitted torque without absorbing it | Strain-gauge torque meters, in-line rotary transducers |

Within the **absorption** family, the syllabus of OEAUT831 emphasises four prime types:

1. **Hydraulic (Water-Brake) Dynamometer** — uses fluid shear.
2. **Eddy-Current (Electromagnetic) Dynamometer** — uses induced eddy currents.
3. **Electrical (DC & AC) Dynamometer** — uses generator loading.
4. **Mechanical Friction Dynamometer** — Prony brake and Rope brake.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Resistance Training of an Engine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Think of a dynamometer exactly like the **treadmill at a gym**. When you run on a treadmill, the belt resists your motion. By adjusting the incline (load), the trainer can precisely measure how much *mechanical effort* you put out. Now imagine the engine is the runner, and the dynamometer is the treadmill.
> 
> - If there is **no load**, the engine just free-revs like a sprinter on flat ground — we get almost no useful measurement.
> - If there is **too much load**, the engine stalls — like a runner tripping on a wall.
> - The sweet spot is **variable, controlled resistance** — this is what a dynamometer provides.
> 
> The *brake torque* is the force the engine must push against, and the *rpm* is how fast it spins against that force. Multiply them, and we get the **power** the engine is delivering.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine Speed | $N$ | **rpm** (revolutions per minute) |
| Brake Torque | $T$ | **N·m** |
| Brake Power | $P_b$ | **kW** |
| Indicated Power | $P_i$ | **kW** |
| Frictional Power | $P_f$ | **kW** |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | BSFC | **kg/kWh** or **g/kWh** |
| Brake Mean Effective Pressure | BMEP | **bar** or **kPa** |
| Calorific Value of Fuel (Diesel) | CV | **42.5 MJ/kg** |
| Calorific Value of Fuel (Petrol) | CV | **44.0 MJ/kg** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote power in **kW**, torque in **N·m**, and fuel flow in **kg/h** when reporting results. Mismatched units are the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical problems.

---

## 1.5 Why Dynamometer Testing is Essential

Without a dynamometer, an engine bolted to a vehicle or machine is a "black box" — we cannot directly measure how much useful work it produces. Dynamometer testing enables:

- **Performance mapping** — power, torque, BSFC curves vs. speed.
- **Emission compliance testing** — under controlled load cycles.
- **Endurance / durability tests** — sustained full-throttle runs.
- **Calibration of ECU maps** on modern electronic engines.
- **Comparison of competing engine designs** under identical lab conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Engine Power Equation

Every engine, regardless of type (SI/CI, 2-stroke/4-stroke), is evaluated using the **indicated–brake–frictional** power triangle:

$$\boxed{P_i = P_b + P_f}$$

- $P_i$ = Indicated Power (power developed inside the cylinder from gas pressure)
- $P_b$ = Brake Power (power available at the crankshaft / flywheel)
- $P_f$ = Frictional Power (power lost to mechanical friction, pumping, bearings)

The **mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{mech}$ is therefore:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_b}{P_i} = 1 - \frac{P_f}{P_i}$$

For modern multi-cylinder engines, $\eta_{mech}$ typically lies in the range **0.80 – 0.92**.

---

## 2.2 Type-by-Type Working Principle

### 2.2.1 Hydraulic (Water-Brake) Dynamometer — Froude Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle:** A multi-bladed **rotor** is mounted on the engine shaft and rotates inside a stator housing filled with water. The rotor creates a swirling fluid film; the stator is restrained by a torque arm connected to a weighing scale. Mechanical power is dissipated as heat in water.

**Working Logic (step-by-step):**

- Engine rotates the rotor inside the water-filled casing.
- Water gains kinetic energy and forms a vortex (eddy).
- The reaction torque on the stator tries to rotate the casing.
- A spring balance / load cell reads the tangential pull on the torque arm.
- Water flow rate + temperature rise are measured to cross-check heat balance.

**Key Formula:**

$$T = (W_1 - W_2) \times R \times g \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

where $W_1$ = loaded scale reading (kg), $W_2$ = tare reading (kg), $R$ = effective arm radius (m), $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**.

**Heat-absorption check:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T = P_b \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where $\dot{m}_w$ is water mass flow rate in kg/s, $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, and $\Delta T$ is the water temperature rise.

**Advantages:** rugged, cheap, very high absorption capacity, smooth loading.
**Disadvantages:** non-linear curve, water carry-over, requires cooling tower.

---

### 2.2.2 Eddy-Current Dynamometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle:** A conductive metal disc (rotor) rotates between the poles of an electromagnet (stator). The magnetic flux induces **eddy currents** in the disc; by Lenz's law, these currents oppose the motion and produce a braking torque proportional to the excitation current.

**Working Logic:**

- Engine drives the rotor disc.
- A variable DC current is fed into the field coil of the stator.
- Stronger current → stronger flux → larger eddy currents → higher braking torque.
- Torque is sensed by a strain-gauge load cell on the stator arm.

**Key Formula for Induced Drag Force:**

$$F \;\propto\; \sigma \, B^2 \, v \, t^2$$

where $\sigma$ = disc conductivity, $B$ = magnetic flux density, $v$ = velocity, $t$ = disc thickness.

**Practical loading formula:**

$$T = k \cdot \phi^2 \cdot N \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

where $\phi$ = field excitation flux, $N$ = speed, $k$ = machine constant.

**Advantages:** wide speed range (down to nearly zero rpm), precise electronic control, no water.
**Disadvantages:** heat dissipated into the rotor → needs water cooling of the stator; rotor discs can warp at sustained full load.

---

### 2.2.3 Electrical Dynamometers (DC & AC)

> [!NOTE]
> These are essentially **motor-generators**. They can *absorb* power (operate as a generator load) **or** *drive* the engine as a starter/motor during motoring tests (used to find $P_f$).

**DC Dynamometer:** A separately-excited or shunt DC machine acts as a generator. Loading is controlled by field rheostat or load bank. DC dynos are excellent for variable-speed testing of ICEs.

**AC Dynamometer (Asynchronous/Induction):** Robust, lower maintenance, but harder to control at low speeds; usually paired with a VFD (variable-frequency drive) for full-range operation.

**Formula:**

$$P_b = V \cdot I \cdot \eta_{gen} \quad \text{(electrical side)}$$

where $V$ is terminal voltage, $I$ is load current, $\eta_{gen}$ is generator efficiency (typically 0.92–0.96).

**Use in KTU problems:** electrical dynos are frequently used in *motoring test* derivations to find frictional power $P_f$.

---

### 2.2.4 Mechanical Friction Dynamometers

**Prony Brake:**
- Wooden block (or cast-iron shoe) grips a pulley mounted on the crankshaft.
- Two arms with spring balances measure reaction torque.
- Water drips onto the pulley to prevent overheating.

$$T = (W_1 - W_2) \times R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

**Rope Brake:**
- Hemp/steel rope wrapped around the flywheel rim, with spring balances at each end.
- Difference in tensions times wheel radius gives torque.

$$T = (W - S) \times R \times g \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

| Feature | Prony Brake | Rope Brake |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity | Small (≤ 25 kW) | Small-Medium |
| Cooling | Poor | Moderate |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Moderate |
| Modern Use | Lab demos | Field testing |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorise** formula bank for OEAUT831 Module 1. Memorise every row — questions worth up to 14 marks are framed using these equations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power | $P_b = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Indicated Power | $P_i = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_{mech}}$ | kW | $\eta_{mech} \in [0.8, 0.92]$ |
| 3 | Frictional Power | $P_f = P_i - P_b$ | kW | Also $= P_b\dfrac{1-\eta_{mech}}{\eta_{mech}}$ |
| 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_i}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 5 | Brake Torque (Prony / Froude) | $T = (W_1 - W_2)\, R\, g$ | N·m | $W$ in kg, $R$ in m |
| 6 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | kg/kWh | Lower = more efficient |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{3600\, P_b}{\dot{m}_f\, \text{CV}}$ | — | CV in kJ/kg |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{3600\, P_i}{\dot{m}_f\, \text{CV}}$ | — | Always $> \eta_{bt}$ |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{actual mass}}{\text{theoretical mass}}$ | — | measured at inlet |
| 10 | BMEP | $\text{BMEP} = \dfrac{2 P_b}{V_d N / 60}$ | kPa | $V_d$ in m³/s for 4-stroke |
| 11 | Heat-balance check (Hydraulic) | $Q = \dot{m}_w c_p \Delta T$ | kW | must equal $P_b$ within 5% |
| 12 | Air-standard Otto efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| 13 | Air-standard Diesel efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | — | $\rho = r_c$ cut-off ratio |
| 14 | Mean Effective Pressure (general) | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_d}$ | Pa | $W_{cycle}$ in J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Hydraulic dynos dominate heavy-duty diesel engine test cells (truck, marine, genset). Eddy-current dynos dominate passenger-car OEM test cells and racing. Electrical dynos dominate research labs because they can *both* absorb and *motoring-drive* the engine for $P_f$ measurement.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Torque and Speed

Let the engine rotate at a steady $N$ revolutions per minute.

**Step 1 — Angular displacement per second.**
Each revolution corresponds to $2\pi$ radians. Therefore, angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \left[\frac{\text{rad}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Torque definition.**
Torque $T$ is the tangential force $F$ multiplied by the radius $r$ of action:

$$T = F \times r \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

**Step 3 — Power definition.**
Power is the rate of doing work:

$$P = F \times v = F \times r \times \omega = T \times \omega$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\omega$:**

$$P = T \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**Step 5 — Convert W to kW:**

$$\boxed{P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad [\text{kW}]}$$

*Conversion logic:* W → kW divides by 1000, so $\dfrac{60 \times 1000}{2\pi}$ inverts correctly. This is the most-used equation in the entire syllabus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mechanical Efficiency

By definition:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{useful output}}{\text{input to mechanism}}$$

**Step 1.** The input to the rotating assembly is the indicated power $P_i$.

**Step 2.** The useful output at the crankshaft is the brake power $P_b$.

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_b}{P_i}$$

**Step 4.** Substituting $P_i = P_b + P_f$:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_b}{P_b + P_f}$$

**Step 5.** Solving for $P_f$:

$$P_f = P_b \left(\frac{1}{\eta_{mech}} - 1\right) = P_b \cdot \frac{1 - \eta_{mech}}{\eta_{mech}}$$

*Conversion logic:* This rearrangement is frequently tested — KTU board papers expect students to *start* from $P_i = P_b + P_f$ and reach $P_f$ in terms of $P_b$ and $\eta_{mech}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of BMEP for a 4-Stroke Engine

**Step 1.** Work done per cycle per cylinder:

$$W_{cycle} = \text{IMEP} \times V_{s} \quad [\text{J}]$$

where $V_s$ is the swept volume of one cylinder.

**Step 2.** Number of power strokes per second for a 4-stroke engine with $n_c$ cylinders and crank speed $N$ rpm:

$$\text{power strokes/sec} = \frac{n_c \cdot N}{2 \times 60}$$

**Step 3.** Total indicated power:

$$P_i = \text{IMEP} \times V_s \times \frac{n_c \cdot N}{2 \times 60}$$

**Step 4.** Replace $n_c \cdot V_s$ with total swept volume per cycle $V_d$. For brake power, replace IMEP with BMEP:

$$P_b = \text{BMEP} \times V_d \times \frac{N}{2 \times 60}$$

**Step 5.** Solve for BMEP:

$$\boxed{\text{BMEP} = \frac{120{,}000 \cdot P_b}{V_d \cdot N} \quad [\text{kPa}]}$$

with $P_b$ in kW, $V_d$ in m³, $N$ in rpm.

*Conversion logic:* The factor 120,000 arises from $60 \times 1000 \times 2$ — multiply seconds-to-minutes, Watts-to-kW, and the 4-stroke factor of 2.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Eddy-Current Dynamo Problem

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU 14-mark problem: a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine is tested on an eddy-current dynamometer.

**Given Data:**
- Speed $N = 3000$ rpm
- Brake torque $T = 110$ N·m
- Fuel consumption $\dot{m}_f = 8.0$ kg/h
- Calorific value $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$

**Find:** (i) Brake Power, (ii) Indicated Power, (iii) BSFC, (iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency, (v) Indicated Thermal Efficiency.

---

**Step (i) — Brake Power:**

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$$

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi \times 3000 \times 110}{60{,}000}$$

Let me compute the numerator first:

$$2\pi \times 3000 \times 110 = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 3000 \times 110$$

$$= 6.28318 \times 330{,}000 = 2{,}073{,}449.4$$

Dividing by 60,000:

$$P_b = \frac{2{,}073{,}449.4}{60{,}000} \approx 34.56 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Step (ii) — Indicated Power:**

$$P_i = \frac{P_b}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{34.56}{0.85} \approx 40.66 \text{ kW}$$

**Frictional Power (extra info):**

$$P_f = P_i - P_b = 40.66 - 34.56 = 6.10 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Step (iii) — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \frac{8.0}{34.56}$$

$$\text{BSFC} \approx 0.2315 \text{ kg/kWh} = 231.5 \text{ g/kWh}$$

*Conversion logic:* A well-tuned modern petrol engine achieves BSFC in the range 230–260 g/kWh at best torque; values above 300 g/kWh indicate poor combustion or high mechanical loss.

---

**Step (iv) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{3600 \cdot P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$$

Energy input per hour:

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} = 8.0 \times 44{,}000 = 352{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Energy input per second:

$$\frac{352{,}000}{3600} = 97.78 \text{ kJ/s} = 97.78 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{34.56}{97.78} \approx 0.3535 = 35.35\%$$

---

**Step (v) — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{it} = \frac{3600 \cdot P_i}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{40.66}{97.78} \approx 0.4158 = 41.58\%$$

*Sanity check:* $\eta_{it} > \eta_{bt}$ ✓, and $\eta_{bt} / \eta_{it} = 0.3535 / 0.4158 = 0.8502 \approx \eta_{mech}$ ✓.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Dynamometer

| Test Requirement | Best Dyno Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy-duty diesel truck engine (peak 600 kW) | **Hydraulic (Froude)** | Absorbs huge power smoothly |
| Racing engine, transient throttle response | **Eddy-Current** | Fast electronic loading, low inertia |
| Frictional power $P_f$ measurement | **Electrical DC (motoring mode)** | Can drive the unloaded engine |
| Field test of a small farm pump | **Rope Brake** | Portable, no infrastructure needed |
| Research lab, multi-mode operation | **AC dynamometer + VFD** | Reversible, four-quadrant operation |
| Student lab demonstration | **Prony Brake** | Visible torque arm, simple to grasp |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Dynamometers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DYNAMOMETERS] --> B[ABSORPTION TYPE]
    A --> C[TRANSMISSION TYPE]
    B --> D[HYDRAULIC WATER BRAKE]
    B --> E[EDDY CURRENT]
    B --> F[ELECTRICAL]
    B --> G[MECHANICAL FRICTION]
    D --> D1[FROUDE]
    D --> D2[MANN]
    E --> E1[DC EXCITED STATOR]
    F --> F1[DC SHUNT GENERATOR]
    F --> F2[AC INDUCTION]
    F --> F3[AC SYNCHRONOUS]
    G --> G1[PRONY BRAKE]
    G --> G2[ROPE BRAKE]
    C --> H[STRAIN GAUGE TORQUE METER]
```

*Reading the diagram:* All absorption types dissipate engine power as heat; transmission types only sense torque and pass it on.

---

## 4.2 Hydraulic Froude Dynamometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENG[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> SHF[DYNOS SHAFT]
    SHF --> ROT[ROTORS WITH MULTIPLE BLADES]
    ROT -->|fluid shear| WTR[WATER FILLED CASING]
    WTR -->|reaction torque| STR[STATOR RESTRAINED BY ARM]
    STR --> LC[LOAD CELL OR SPRING BALANCE]
    LC -->|W1 minus W2 reading| DSP[DISPLAY CONSOLE]
    WTR -->|heated water| CWT[COOLING TOWER HEAT EXCHANGER]
    CWT -->|cooled water recirculated| WTR
    SHF -->|speed pickup| SPD[TACHOMETER]
    SPD --> DSP
```

*Reading the diagram:* Two closed loops exist — the **mechanical/torque loop** (engine → shaft → rotor → water → stator → load cell) and the **thermal loop** (water heated → cooling tower → water cooled back).

---

## 4.3 Eddy-Current Dynamometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENG[ENGINE] --> SFT[INPUT SHAFT]
    SFT --> DSC[CONDUCTIVE STEEL ROTOR DISC]
    DSC -->|cuts flux| EMG[ELECTROMAGNET STATOR]
    EMG -->|excitation current| EXC[DC EXCITATION SUPPLY UNIT]
    EMG -->|torque reaction| ARM[TORQUE ARM WITH STRAIN GAUGE]
    ARM --> AMP[AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> DAQ[DATA ACQUISITION]
    SFT --> TAC[TACHOGENERATOR]
    TAC --> DAQ
    DSC -->|heat to be removed| CLG[STATOR WATER JACKET COOLING]
    CLG --> CHR[COOLING WATER CIRCUIT]
```

*Reading the diagram:* The *electrical control loop* (excitation supply → electromagnet → rotor disc → torque reaction) and the *thermal management loop* (stator water jacket) are critical sub-systems of every eddy-current dyno.

---

## 4.4 Indicating–Brake–Frictional Power Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY] -->|CV times mass flow| B[INDICATED POWER PI]
    B --> C[BRAKE POWER PB USEFUL OUTPUT]
    B --> D[FRICTIONAL POWER PF LOST]
    D --> D1[BEARING FRICTION]
    D --> D2[PISTON RING FRICTION]
    D --> D3[PUMPING LOSS]
    D --> D4[AUXILIARY DRIVE LOSS]
    C --> E[OUTPUT SHAFT TO LOAD]
```

*Reading the diagram:* This Sankey-style chart visualises how the chemical energy in fuel is partitioned — only the brake power reaches the dyno; everything above the dotted line is internal.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a dynamometer. List any two types of absorption dynamometers with one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A dynamometer is a device used to measure the **brake torque** and **rotational speed** of a prime-mover, from which **brake power** is calculated using $P_b = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000$ kW.

Two types of absorption dynamometers:

1. **Hydraulic (Froude) dynamometer** — used in heavy-duty diesel engine test cells (truck, marine, genset) due to its very high power-absorption capacity and smooth loading.
2. **Eddy-current dynamometer** — used in passenger-car OEM test cells and motorsport applications due to its fast electronic loading response and broad speed range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Valuation Note (3-mark split):** 1 mark for the definition + formula, 1 mark each for the two types with application. Do not exceed 3–4 lines.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)? State its typical range for a petrol engine.**

**Model Answer:**
BSFC is defined as the **mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power per unit time**:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$$

Typical range for a petrol (SI) engine: **0.23 – 0.30 kg/kWh** (i.e., 230 – 300 g/kWh) at the point of best efficiency.

For a diesel (CI) engine, the typical range is **0.20 – 0.26 kg/kWh**, which is lower because of higher compression ratio and leaner combustion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Froude hydraulic dynamometer**. Derive the expression for brake power.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine with swept volume 1200 cc develops a brake torque of 90 N·m at 2800 rpm when tested on an eddy-current dynamometer. If the mechanical efficiency is 82% and fuel consumption is 7.2 kg/h with CV = 44 MJ/kg, calculate BP, IP, BSFC, BMEP, $\eta_{bt}$ and $\eta_{it}$.

---

### Solution to Question A (a) — Froude Hydraulic Dynamometer

**Sketch description (verbal):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │   WATER INLET                         │
   │     ╲                                  │
   │      ╲   ┌────┐     ┌───────┐         │
   │       ●──┤ROT ├─────┤STATOR ├─●───W₁  │
   │       ●──┤OR  │     │       ├─●───W₂  │
   │      ╱   └────┘     └───────┘         │
   │     ╱    (shaft)                       │
   │   WATER OUTLET (heated)                │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
              Effective arm radius R
```

**Working Principle:**

- The **rotor** is keyed to the engine shaft and rotates inside the **stator** casing.
- The casing is partially filled with water; the rotor blades agitate the water creating a fluid vortex.
- By Newton's third law, the reaction torque on the stator tries to rotate it.
- The stator is restrained by a torque arm; the tangential force is measured by a spring balance / load cell.
- Water temperature rises as it absorbs engine power — it is recirculated through a cooling tower.

**Brake Power Derivation:**

Let the spring balance reading under load be $W_1$ (kg) and tare (no load) be $W_2$ (kg).

**Step 1 — Net tangential force on the arm:**

$$F = (W_1 - W_2)\, g \quad [\text{N}]$$

**Step 2 — Effective arm length = $R$ m:**

$$T = F \times R = (W_1 - W_2)\, R\, g \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

**Step 3 — Power:**

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi N (W_1 - W_2) R g}{60{,}000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Heat-balance cross-check:**

$$P_b = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where $\dot{m}_w$ = water flow rate in kg/s and $\Delta T$ = temperature rise across the dyno.

---

### Solution to Question A (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $n_c = 4$, $V_d = 1200$ cc $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $N = 2800$ rpm, $T = 90$ N·m, $\eta_{mech} = 0.82$, $\dot{m}_f = 7.2$ kg/h, $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Brake Power:**

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 2800 \times 90}{60{,}000}$$

Numerator:

$$2\pi \times 2800 \times 90 = 6.2832 \times 252{,}000 = 1{,}583{,}366$$

$$P_b = \frac{1{,}583{,}366}{60{,}000} \approx 26.39 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power:**

$$P_i = \frac{P_b}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{26.39}{0.82} \approx 32.18 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — BSFC:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \frac{7.2}{26.39} \approx 0.2728 \text{ kg/kWh} = 272.8 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — BMEP (4-stroke):**

$$\text{BMEP} = \frac{120{,}000 \cdot P_b}{V_d \cdot N}$$

$$\text{BMEP} = \frac{120{,}000 \times 26.39}{1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2800}$$

Numerator:

$$120{,}000 \times 26.39 = 3{,}166{,}800$$

Denominator:

$$1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2800 = 3.36$$

$$\text{BMEP} = \frac{3{,}166{,}800}{3.36} \approx 942{,}500 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.43 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5 — $\eta_{bt}$:**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{3600 \cdot P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{3600 \times 26.39}{7.2 \times 44{,}000}$$

Numerator:

$$3600 \times 26.39 = 95{,}004$$

Denominator:

$$7.2 \times 44{,}000 = 316{,}800$$

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{95{,}004}{316{,}800} \approx 0.2999 \approx 30.0\%$$

**Step 6 — $\eta_{it}$:**

$$\eta_{it} = \frac{3600 \cdot P_i}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{3600 \times 32.18}{316{,}800} = \frac{115{,}848}{316{,}800} \approx 0.3657 \approx 36.6\%$$

**Final Answers Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Brake Power $P_b$ | **26.39 kW** |
| Indicated Power $P_i$ | **32.18 kW** |
| BSFC | **272.8 g/kWh** |
| BMEP | **9.43 bar** |
| $\eta_{bt}$ | **30.0 %** |
| $\eta_{it}$ | **36.6 %** |

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of an **eddy-current dynamometer** with a block diagram. Discuss its advantages over the Prony brake.

**(b) [7 marks]** A single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine tested on a rope brake has a flywheel diameter of 1.0 m. The spring balance readings are $W = 25$ kg and $S = 4$ kg. The engine runs at 360 rpm and consumes 1.6 kg/h of fuel with CV = 42.5 MJ/kg. Calculate (i) Brake Power, (ii) Brake Torque, (iii) BSFC, (iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency.

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — Eddy-Current Dynamometer

**Construction:**

- **Rotor:** A high-grade, hardened, magnetic-steel disc keyed to the input shaft.
- **Stator:** A multi-pole electromagnet (usually 4 or 6 poles) with field windings energised by a controlled DC excitation supply.
- **Torque arm:** The stator assembly is mounted on trunnion bearings and restrained by a torque arm fitted with a strain-gauge load cell.
- **Cooling:** The stator poles contain internal water jackets to dissipate eddy-current heat.
- **Tachogenerator / encoder:** Provides speed feedback to the controller.

**Working Principle:**

- As the rotor disc spins through the magnetic field of the stator, eddy currents are induced in the disc by **Faraday's law**.
- By **Lenz's law**, these eddy currents produce a magnetic field that **opposes** the inducing flux — this opposition manifests as a braking torque on the disc.
- The torque is proportional to the square of the field flux $\phi^2$ and the speed $N$.
- The stator receives an equal and opposite reaction torque, which is sensed by the load cell.

**Block Diagram (verbal):**

```
Engine → Shaft → Rotor Disc ⇄ Electromagnet (excitation)
                          ↓
                    Reaction Torque
                          ↓
                   Strain-gauge Arm
                          ↓
                    Load Cell Reading
                          ↓
                    Display / Recorder
```

**Advantages over Prony Brake:**

| Feature | Eddy-Current Dyno | Prony Brake |
|---|---|---|
| Loading method | Smooth, electronic | Jerky, manual |
| Speed range | Near zero to max | Narrow |
| Heat dissipation | Internal stator water jacket | Poor, block burns |
| Accuracy | ± 0.5 % FS | ± 2–3 % |
| Automation | Fully automated | Manual |
| Wear | No mechanical contact | Continuous block wear |

---

### Solution to Question B (b) — Rope Brake Numerical

**Given:** $D = 1.0$ m, $R = 0.5$ m, $W = 25$ kg, $S = 4$ kg, $N = 360$ rpm, $\dot{m}_f = 1.6$ kg/h, $CV = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg.

**Step (i) — Brake Torque:**

$$T = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g = (25 - 4) \times 0.5 \times 9.81$$

$$T = 21 \times 0.5 \times 9.81 = 10.5 \times 9.81 = 103.005 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step (ii) — Brake Power:**

$$P_b = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2\pi \times 360 \times 103.005}{60{,}000}$$

Numerator:

$$2\pi \times 360 \times 103.005 = 6.2832 \times 37{,}081.8 = 232{,}987.6$$

$$P_b = \frac{232{,}987.6}{60{,}000} \approx 3.883 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iii) — BSFC:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \frac{1.6}{3.883} \approx 0.412 \text{ kg/kWh} = 412 \text{ g/kWh}$$

*Conversion logic:* A small single-cylinder diesel typically shows BSFC around 380–450 g/kWh — slightly high due to low mechanical efficiency (~75%) and high heat loss.

**Step (iv) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

Energy input rate:

$$\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} = 1.6 \times 42{,}500 = 68{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW:

$$\frac{68{,}000}{3600} = 18.889 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{3.883}{18.889} \approx 0.2056 \approx 20.6\%$$

**Final Answers Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Brake Torque $T$ | **103.0 N·m** |
| Brake Power $P_b$ | **3.88 kW** |
| BSFC | **412 g/kWh** |
| $\eta_{bt}$ | **20.6 %** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit mismatch in BSFC:** Forgetting to convert fuel flow from **kg/h** to **kg/s** before dividing by $P_b$ in kW. Always keep $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/h and $P_b$ in kW so that BSFC automatically comes out in kg/kWh.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $\eta_{bt}$ with $\eta_{it}$:** $\eta_{bt}$ uses $P_b$; $\eta_{it}$ uses $P_i$. Many students swap these and lose 2 marks per sub-question.
> 
> 3. **Prony-brake torque formula:** Forgetting to multiply by $g = 9.81$. If $W$ is in kg and $R$ is in metres, you **must** multiply by $g$ to obtain N·m.
> 
> 4. **Rope brake sign convention:** Using $W - S$ in the wrong order. By definition, the rope side with greater pull is $W$ and the lighter side is $S$.
> 
> 5. **BMEP formula factor:** Using 60,000 instead of **120,000** for a 4-stroke engine. The factor of 2 difference is a frequent slip.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Stick to Your Wall:**

- ✅ Dynamometer measures **brake torque + speed → brake power**. Master equation: $P_b = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000$ (kW).
- ✅ Power triangle: $P_i = P_b + P_f$. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = P_b / P_i \in [0.80, 0.92]$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic (Froude)** — fluid shear; best for heavy-duty diesel; needs cooling tower.
- ✅ **Eddy-current** — induced eddy currents; best for fast electronic loading; rotor + stator electromagnet.
- ✅ **Electrical (DC/AC)** — generator mode absorbs; motor mode drives (used for $P_f$ measurement).
- ✅ **Prony brake** — wooden block on pulley; small lab demos only.
- ✅ **Rope brake** — rope wrapped on flywheel; field tests.
- ✅ Prony/Rope torque formula: $T = (W_1 - W_2) R g$ — **always multiply by 9.81**.
- ✅ BSFC lower = more efficient. Petrol: 230–300 g/kWh; Diesel: 200–260 g/kWh.
- ✅ $\eta_{bt} < \eta_{it}$ always. $\eta_{bt} / \eta_{it} = \eta_{mech}$.
- ✅ BMEP for 4-stroke: $\text{BMEP} = 120{,}000 P_b / (V_d N)$; for 2-stroke: $60{,}000 P_b / (V_d N)$.
- ✅ Heat balance on hydraulic dyno: $P_b = \dot{m}_w c_p \Delta T$ (cross-check within 5%).
- ✅ KTU often asks: choice of dynamometer for a given engine → memorise the **selection matrix** in Section 3.5.
- ✅ Always quote **units** in final answers — marks are reserved explicitly for unit correctness.
- ✅ Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless otherwise specified; never approximate as 10 in board exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Testing on Chassis Dynamometers (Two-Wheel and Four-Wheel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Testing on Chassis Dynamometers (Two-Wheel and Four-Wheel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Chassis Dynamometer** is a stationary mechanical test apparatus used to measure the tractive force, power output, emissions, and fuel economy of a complete road vehicle by placing its drive wheels (or all wheels) on rotating rollers. The device applies a controllable absorbing torque to simulate the road-load resistance that the vehicle would experience under actual driving conditions, thereby enabling repeatable, instrumented, and emission-safe laboratory testing of a vehicle without requiring road movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"A chassis dynamometer is a vehicle-mounted-on-rollers test rig in which the rotary motion of the drive wheel(s) is opposed by a power-absorption unit that replicates the in-use road load (rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, gradient, and inertia) so that the engine/transmission torque, brake-specific fuel consumption, and regulated emissions can be quantified under simulated transient and steady-state driving schedules."*

## 1.2 Two-Wheel vs. Four-Wheel Chassis Dynamometer

| Parameter | Two-Wheel Dynamometer (2WD) | Four-Wheel Dynamometer (4WD) |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Motorcycles, scooters | Passenger cars, light commercial vehicles |
| Drive Configuration | Rear wheel (or both) on a single roller / twin close-coupled rollers | All four wheels on twin roller sets (one per axle) or large single rollers |
| Typical Power Range | 5 kW – 150 kW | 50 kW – 600 kW |
| Absorption Unit | Eddy current, water brake, hysteresis | Eddy current, AC regenerative, water brake, electric dynamometer |
| Roller Diameter | 200 – 400 mm | 200 – 1200 mm (large single roller preferred for transient fidelity) |
| Load Capacity | Lower (single wheel contact) | Higher (full vehicle mass distributed) |
| Inertia Simulation | Mechanical flywheels or electrical | Electrical (preferred) or mechanical + electrical |
| Cooling | Compact air-cooled | Large water-cooled + blower for engine bay |
| Common Test Cycles | WMTC, ECE R40, IDC | FTP-75, ECE 15 + EUDC, WLTP, NEDC |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Indoor Treadmill for Cars"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine strapping your motorcycle or car onto a giant, motorised indoor treadmill. The treadmill (the **roller**) spins under the wheel, and the harder you rev the engine, the faster the roller turns. Now imagine placing a giant, adjustable magnetic brake on that treadmill — that brake is the **power-absorption unit (eddy-current or electric dynamometer)**. The brake's grip can be tightened (more load) or loosened (less load) to imitate driving up a hill, against wind, or accelerating from rest. Because the car is not actually moving forward, engineers can measure **power, torque, fuel use, and exhaust pollutants** with very precise instruments in a clean, controlled indoor lab — no road, no wind, no traffic police, no safety risk.

## 1.4 Why Chassis Dynamometers Are Used (KTU High-Yield Justification)

- **Regulatory emissions certification** (BS-VI / Euro 6 requires chassis-dyno running cycles).
- **Repeatability** — road tests are non-reproducible due to wind, gradient, traffic.
- **Safety** — high-performance vehicles tested without road risk.
- **Energy efficiency / fuel-economy labelling** (ARAI, IDC, WLTP).
- **Development of ECU calibration**, gearbox mapping, hybrid-control strategies.
- **Diagnostics** — on-board diagnostics (OBD) functional checks.

## 1.5 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values used in chassis-dyno analysis:**
> - Air density at STP: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Reference speed (FTP-75): **v_ref = 26.82 m/s (≈ 96.56 km/h)**
> - WLTP Class-3 reference speed: **v_ref = 26.5 m/s**
> - Standard tyre rolling-resistance coefficient: **f_r = 0.008 – 0.015** (passenger car)
> - Motorcycle rolling-resistance coefficient: **f_r = 0.010 – 0.020**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Vehicle Placement & Strap-Down**
   The vehicle is centred on the roller(s); the non-driven wheels rest on free-turning support rollers. The vehicle is **strapped down** with chains/anchors to prevent longitudinal movement. A **cooling blower** is positioned in front of the radiator to simulate road air-flow at the corresponding test speed.

2. **Power Transmission Path**
   Engine → Clutch/Transmission → Driveshaft → Drive Wheel(s) → **Roller(s)** → **Power Absorption Unit (PAU)**.

3. **Force Equilibrium at the Tyre-Roller Interface**
   The tractive force at the wheel contact patch equals the sum of the force the roller PAU resists plus the inertia of the roller/flywheel assembly.

4. **Road-Load Simulation**
   The dynamometer controller adjusts the PAU torque in real time so that the wheel-roller interface force equals the force the vehicle would experience on the road at the same speed and acceleration.

5. **Data Acquisition**
   Torque sensor, roller-speed sensor, fuel-flow meter, exhaust-gas analyser (CO, CO₂, HC, NOₓ, PM), and OBD port collect synchronous data.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why strap-down?** A real vehicle accelerates forward; on a dyno, the chassis must be fixed so only the wheel drives the roller.
- **Why a blower?** Without forced air, the radiator would overheat — engine power would drop and the test would be invalid.
- **Why adjust PAU torque continuously?** Road load varies with v² (aerodynamic) and linearly with speed (rolling); a fixed brake cannot mimic this.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All marks-distributing formulas for the Module 1 chassis-dyno section are consolidated below. Memorise SI units and the sign convention.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Road Load Force | F_road = F_roll + F_aero + F_grad + F_inertia | N | Superposition principle |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | F_roll = f_r · W · cos θ | N | f_r dimensionless, W in N, θ in rad |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | F_aero = 0.5 · ρ · C_D · A_f · v² | N | A_f frontal area [m²], v [m/s] |
| 4 | Gradient Resistance | F_grad = W · sin θ | N | θ = road slope angle |
| 5 | Inertia (Linear) | F_inertia = m_eff · (dv/dt) | N | m_eff = m_vehicle + I_eng / r_w² + … |
| 6 | Equivalent Inertia at Roller | I_eq = m_vehicle · r_roll² + I_flywheel | kg·m² | Sets acceleration fidelity |
| 7 | Power at Roller (Absorbed) | P_abs = T_abs · ω_roll = F_tract · v | W | ω_roll = v / r_roll |
| 8 | Eddy-Current Dyno Torque | T_eddy = k · Φ² · ω | N·m | Φ = excitation flux |
| 9 | Power-Coefficient Form | P_abs = k' · I_exc² · N | W | I_exc excitation current, N rpm |
| 10 | Hydraulic (Water-Brake) Power | P_hyd = ρ_w · Q · g · H | W | Stator-filling variable |
| 11 | Roller Surface Speed | v_roll = ω_roll · r_roll = v_veh · (r_roll / r_wheel) | m/s | Gear-ratio independent |
| 12 | Test-Schedule Speed | v(t) = prescribed (FTP, WLTP…) | m/s | Input to dyno controller |
| 13 | Dyno-Setting (Target Force) | F_target = a + b·v + c·v² | N | A, B, C coeffs from coastdown |
| 14 | Vehicle-Side Power (wheel) | P_wheel = (F_roll + F_aero + F_grad)·v + m·v·(dv/dt) | W | Energy balance at wheel |
| 15 | Drivetrain Loss Power | P_loss = P_engine_brake − P_wheel | W | Used in efficiency calculations |
| 16 | Fuel-Energy Conversion | ṁ_fuel · LHV · η_th · η_m = P_wheel | kg/s | LHV ≈ 42.5 MJ/kg petrol |
| 17 | Dyno Coastdown Deceleration | a_coast = (a + b·v + c·v²) / (m + I_eq / r_roll²) | m/s² | Used in calibration check |
| 18 | A, B, C Coefficient Fit | F = A + B·v + C·v² | N | Quadratic regression from road data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU Solutions:**
> - All forces are **resisting** (opposing motion) → positive when resisting.
> - **v** is the longitudinal vehicle speed in m/s.
> - **θ** is the road gradient angle (positive uphill).
> - **W** is the curb weight × g (vehicle weight force, not mass).

## 2.4 Road-Load Coefficient Determination (A, B, C)

The **target road-load equation** at the roller is fitted as a second-order polynomial in speed:

$$F_{\text{road}}(v) = A + B \cdot v + C \cdot v^{2}$$

These coefficients are obtained from a **coastdown test** on a flat road:
- Vehicle is brought to a calibrated speed (typically > 100 km/h).
- Clutch is depressed (or gear put in neutral); vehicle coasts to rest.
- Time vs. speed is recorded → decel vs. speed is derived.
- The quadratic fit yields A (≈ rolling), B (≈ rolling-speed sensitivity), C (≈ ½·ρ·C_D·A_f).

The dynamometer control system then commands the PAU torque in real time to match this F(v).

## 2.5 Inertia Simulation — Mechanical vs. Electrical

| Aspect | Mechanical Flywheels | Electrical (Computer-Controlled) |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Physical discs coupled to roller shaft | Software models inertia; PAU adjusts torque |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Discretisation | Stepped inertia (discrete values) | Continuous, any mass equivalent |
| Accuracy | ± 2–5 % | ± 0.5–1 % |
| Switching | Manual flywheel change | Automatic |
| Modern use | 2W / small 4W dynos | Modern 4W regulatory dynos |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **equivalent rotational inertia** at the roller shaft is set such that
>
> $$I_{\text{eq}} = m_{\text{vehicle,eq}} \cdot r_{\text{roll}}^{2}$$
>
> where m_vehicle,eq is the **equivalent test mass** (vehicle kerb mass + 75/100/150 kg driver + cargo, per regulation).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai R&D): use chassis dynos for BS-VI / Euro 6 type-approval and ARAL certification.
- **Motorcycle manufacturers** (Bajaj, TVS, Royal Enfield, KTM India): use 2W dynos for WMTC-class compliance and power curve development.
- **F1 & motorsport teams**: use 4WD dynos with driver-in-loop simulators.
- **Defence / heavy vehicles**: large single-roller dynos for full axle-load testing.
- **After-market / tuning shops**: in-line chassis dynos for remap validation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Road-Load Force Equation

The total longitudinal force opposing a vehicle's motion on a level road is the sum of four contributions. We derive each from first principles.

### Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force (F_roll)

A pneumatic tyre deforms as it rolls. The normal force is shifted slightly forward of the geometric centre of contact, producing an offset `a` between the resultant normal force N and the wheel centre.

**Moment equilibrium about the wheel centre:**

$$F_{\text{roll}} \cdot r_{\text{w}} = N \cdot a$$

Substituting the empirical rolling-resistance coefficient f_r = a / r_w:

$$F_{\text{roll}} = N \cdot \frac{a}{r_{\text{w}}} = f_{r} \cdot N$$

On a flat surface (θ = 0), N = W = m·g, hence:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{roll}} = f_{r} \cdot m \cdot g}$$

> For a 1500 kg car with f_r = 0.012 at 100 km/h → F_roll = 0.012 × 1500 × 9.81 = **176.6 N**.

### Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag (F_aero)

Air exerts a pressure differential on the vehicle frontal area. Using **Bernoulli's principle** for an ideal incompressible flow:

$$\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,v^{2}$$

Multiplying by the effective frontal area A_f and the dimensionless drag coefficient C_D (which accounts for shape, separation, and viscous effects):

$$F_{\text{aero}} = C_{D} \cdot A_{f} \cdot \Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,A_{f}\,v^{2}$$

> For ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, C_D = 0.30, A_f = 2.2 m², v = 27.78 m/s (100 km/h):
> F_aero = 0.5 × 1.225 × 0.30 × 2.2 × (27.78)² ≈ **311.9 N**.

### Step 3 — Gradient Resistance (F_grad)

For a vehicle on an incline of angle θ, the component of weight parallel to the slope (and opposing motion when going uphill) is:

$$F_{\text{grad}} = W \cdot \sin\theta = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

> At 5 % grade, sin θ ≈ 0.05; for 1500 kg → F_grad = 1500 × 9.81 × 0.05 = **735.75 N**.

### Step 4 — Inertia Force (F_inertia)

By **Newton's Second Law**, the force required to accelerate the equivalent vehicle mass:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = m_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

where m_eff includes the vehicle's translational mass and the rotational inertias of rotating components (engine, gearbox, wheels) referred to the vehicle's centre of mass.

### Step 5 — Total Road-Load (Superposition)

Summing the four contributions (level road, no wind, considering the resisting force direction):

$$F_{\text{road}} = f_{r} \cdot m \cdot g + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,A_{f}\,v^{2} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

For chassis-dyno testing, θ = 0 (level simulation) and the inertia term is realised through the dyno's flywheel/electrical inertia:

$$F_{\text{dyno}} = f_{r} \cdot W + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,A_{f}\,v^{2} + m_{\text{eff,test}} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Equivalent Inertia at the Roller

The **kinetic energy** of the test mass on the dyno must equal the kinetic energy of the actual vehicle in linear motion plus the rotational KE of rotating parts, all referred to the roller.

$$E_{\text{K,dyno}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_{\text{eq}} \cdot \omega_{\text{roll}}^{2}$$

$$E_{\text{K,road}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_{\text{eff}} \cdot v^{2}$$

Equating (since v = ω_roll · r_roll):

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\,I_{\text{eq}} \cdot \omega_{\text{roll}}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_{\text{eff}} \cdot (\omega_{\text{roll}} \cdot r_{\text{roll}})^{2}$$

Solving for I_eq:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{eq}} = m_{\text{eff}} \cdot r_{\text{roll}}^{2}}$$

**Numerical example:** Test mass = 1500 kg, r_roll = 0.30 m:

$$I_{\text{eq}} = 1500 \times (0.30)^{2} = 1500 \times 0.09 = 135 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

This is typically realised by either physically adding flywheels of that moment of inertia, or by electronic command of the dyno controller (electrical inertia simulation).

## 3.3 Derivation — Power at the Roller (Equivalence to Wheel Power)

The power absorbed at the roller equals the tractive power at the wheel (neglecting roller-bearing friction, which is calibrated out):

$$P_{\text{abs}} = T_{\text{PAU}} \cdot \omega_{\text{roll}} = F_{\text{tract,roller}} \cdot v_{\text{roll}}$$

The tractive force at the roller corresponds (through the tyre-roller contact) to the tractive force at the wheel-road contact:

$$F_{\text{tract,roller}} = F_{\text{tract,wheel}} = F_{\text{road,total}}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{abs}} = F_{\text{road,total}} \cdot v}$$

This is the **fundamental energy-conservation identity** on which chassis-dyno testing is based.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern]:**
> A 1200 kg passenger car is tested on a chassis dynamometer. Frontal area A_f = 2.0 m², drag coefficient C_D = 0.32, rolling-resistance coefficient f_r = 0.011. The car is running steadily at 90 km/h on a level road. Compute the total road-load force, the absorbed power, and the equivalent inertia required at the roller (r_roll = 0.25 m).

**Given:**
- m = 1200 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²
- A_f = 2.0 m², C_D = 0.32, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³
- f_r = 0.011, v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s
- r_roll = 0.25 m

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance:**

$$F_{\text{roll}} = 0.011 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 129.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times (25)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times 625 = 245.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Gradient (level, θ = 0):**

$$F_{\text{grad}} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Inertia (steady, dv/dt = 0):**

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Total road load:**

$$F_{\text{road}} = 129.5 + 245.0 + 0 + 0 = 374.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Absorbed power:**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = F_{\text{road}} \cdot v = 374.5 \times 25 = 9362.5 \text{ W} \approx 9.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Equivalent inertia at roller (assume m_eff = m for simplicity):**

$$I_{\text{eq}} = 1200 \times (0.25)^{2} = 1200 \times 0.0625 = 75 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This single problem tests all three primary formulas of Module 1 chassis-dyno theory. KTU examiners frequently combine two or three into a 14-mark sub-question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Contribution vs. Speed

The relative contribution of rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and inertia at different speeds (for the 1200 kg car above) is tabulated below:

| Speed (km/h) | Speed (m/s) | F_roll (N) | F_aero (N) | F_inertia (steady) (N) | Total (N) | P_abs (kW) | % Aerodynamic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 8.33 | 129.5 | 27.2 | 0 | 156.7 | 1.31 | 17.3 % |
| 60 | 16.67 | 129.5 | 108.7 | 0 | 238.2 | 3.97 | 45.6 % |
| 90 | 25.00 | 129.5 | 245.0 | 0 | 374.5 | 9.36 | 65.4 % |
| 120 | 33.33 | 129.5 | 435.6 | 0 | 565.1 | 18.84 | 77.1 % |
| 150 | 41.67 | 129.5 | 680.4 | 0 | 809.9 | 33.74 | 84.0 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference:** Aerodynamic drag dominates above ≈ 80 km/h, which is why chassis-dyno tests are highly sensitive to A_f and C_D at highway cruise. Below ≈ 50 km/h, rolling resistance dominates. This is a frequent 3-mark conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Four-Wheel Chassis Dynamometer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine and Transmission] --> B[Drive Shaft]
    B --> C[Drive Wheels]
    C --> D[Tyre Roller Contact Patch]
    D --> E[Roller Set Front Axle]
    D --> F[Roller Set Rear Axle]
    E --> G[Front PAU Eddy Current]
    F --> H[Rear PAU Eddy Current]
    G --> I[Inertia Module Flywheel Bank]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Torque and Speed Sensors]
    J --> K[Dyno Controller ECU]
    K --> G
    K --> H
    L[Cooling Blower] --> A
    M[Exhaust Gas Analyser] --> A
    N[Fuel Flow Meter] --> A
    J --> O[Data Acquisition PC]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    K --> O
    O --> P[Test Report Power Emissions Fuel Economy]
```

**Functional Flow Description:**
- The vehicle's drive wheels transfer torque to **front and rear roller sets**.
- Each roller set feeds a dedicated **Power Absorption Unit (PAU)** — typically eddy-current or AC regenerative.
- An **inertia module** (mechanical flywheels or electronic simulation) provides the equivalent mass.
- **Torque and speed sensors** feed real-time data to the **dyno controller**, which adjusts PAU excitation to follow the A + B·v + C·v² target force.
- A **cooling blower** maintains engine thermal equilibrium; an **exhaust analyser** and **fuel-flow meter** complete the regulatory measurement chain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Wheel (Motorcycle) Chassis Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Motorcycle Engine] --> A2[Clutch and Gearbox]
    A2 --> A3[Rear Wheel]
    A3 --> A4[Tyre Roller Contact]
    A4 --> A5[Single Twin Roller]
    A5 --> A6[Mechanical Flywheel Bank]
    A6 --> A7[Eddy Current PAU]
    A7 --> A8[Torque Speed Sensor]
    A8 --> A9[Microcontroller Setpoint]
    A9 --> A7
    B1[Roller Cooling Fan] --> A5
    C1[Engine Bay Blower] --> A1
    A8 --> D1[Laptop with WMTC Cycle]
    D1 --> A9
```

**Key Differences Highlighted:**
- Only the **rear (driven) wheel** is on the power roller; the **front wheel** sits on a free support.
- **Smaller PAU** (5–150 kW range).
- **Mechanical flywheel bank** (simpler inertia simulation) is still common.
- Cycles used: **WMTC, ECE R40, IDC (India)**.

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Selection Logic for Dyno Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Vehicle Test Requirement] --> Q1{Vehicle Type}
    Q1 -->|Two Wheeler| M[Motorcycle Chassis Dyno]
    Q1 -->|Four Wheeler| C[Passenger Car Chassis Dyno]
    Q1 -->|Heavy Commercial| H[Heavy Duty Multi Roller Dyno]
    M --> M1{Single Roller or Twin}
    M1 --> M2[Single Front and Single Rear Roller]
    M1 --> M3[Twin Coupled Roller for Rear Wheel]
    C --> C1{Power Absorption Type}
    C1 --> C2[Eddy Current Dyno]
    C1 --> C3[AC Regenerative Dyno]
    C1 --> C4[Water Brake Dyno]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow — Dyno Control Loop (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Schedule WLTP v t] --> B[Reference Force Computer]
    B --> C[Target F v]
    C --> D[Controller PI]
    D --> E[PAU Excitation Current]
    E --> F[Eddy Current PAU]
    F --> G[Roller Torque T]
    G --> H[Tyre Roller Contact]
    H --> I[Vehicle Wheel Speed]
    I --> J[Speed Sensor]
    J --> K[Feedback to Controller]
    J --> L[Actual F v]
    L --> B
    H --> M[Torque Sensor Output]
    M --> N[Data Logger]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Interpretation:** The WLTP velocity profile generates a target force; the controller minimises the error between actual measured roller force and target by adjusting the PAU excitation current. This is a classical **PI control** problem — frequently asked as a 7-mark question on dyno working principle.
