# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 pattern]

> **Q: Define a chassis dynamometer. List any two advantages of testing vehicles on a chassis dynamometer compared to on-road testing. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A chassis dynamometer is a stationary test rig in which a vehicle's drive wheel(s) are placed on motorised rollers; a power-absorption unit applies a controlled torque to the rollers to simulate the road-load resistance that the vehicle would experience in real driving.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Repeatability and accuracy** — climatic, traffic, and gradient variables are eliminated, so identical test conditions can be reproduced for certification and development.
2. **Safety and instrumentation** — high-power, high-emission, and high-speed tests are conducted indoors with high-precision exhaust, fuel, and torque instrumentation without road risk.

*(Any other valid point: emission compliance, ECU calibration, fuel-economy labelling.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern]

> **Q: Differentiate between a two-wheel chassis dynamometer and a four-wheel chassis dynamometer. Give one example application of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 2-Wheel Dyno | 4-Wheel Dyno |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Motorcycles, scooters | Cars, LCVs |
| Driven Wheels Tested | One (typically rear) | All four (AWD) or two (FWD/RWD) |
| Typical Use | WMTC, ECE R40, IDC | FTP-75, WLTP, NEDC |

**Example:**
- 2W: WMTC type-approval of a 200 cc commuter motorcycle.
- 4W: WLTP certification of a 1.5 L BS-VI hatchback.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You are required to answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 pattern]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **four-wheel chassis dynamometer** used for testing passenger cars. Clearly indicate the function of the power-absorption unit, the inertia simulation module, and the cooling blower. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1400 kg passenger car is tested on a chassis dynamometer. Given: A_f = 2.1 m², C_D = 0.30, f_r = 0.010, roller radius r_roll = 0.30 m, air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. The car is driven at a steady speed of 108 km/h on a level road.
> **(i)** Compute the total road-load force and absorbed power. **(4 Marks)**
> **(ii)** Calculate the equivalent inertia required at the roller. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block diagram and working (7 marks):**

A four-wheel chassis dynamometer consists of:

1. **Two roller sets** (one for the front axle, one for the rear axle) coupled to **power-absorption units (PAUs)** — typically eddy-current or AC regenerative dynamometers.
2. **Inertia simulation module** — provides equivalent test mass through mechanical flywheels or computer-controlled electrical inertia.
3. **Torque and speed sensors** on the roller shafts feed data to a **digital controller**.
4. **Cooling blower** placed in front of the vehicle to simulate road air-flow across the radiator.
5. **Exhaust gas analyser** and **fuel flow meter** for emission and consumption measurement.

**Working:**
- The vehicle is positioned on the rollers, strapped down, and started.
- The drive wheels rotate the rollers, which oppose rotation through the PAU.
- The controller adjusts the PAU excitation current so that the resisting force at the roller equals the prescribed road load: F = A + B·v + C·v².
- The cooling blower maintains engine thermal conditions, while the analyser measures exhaust emissions during standard test cycles (FTP-75, WLTP, etc.).
- Output: power curve, fuel consumption map, and emission values.

*(A clean block diagram carries 2 marks; functional explanation 3 marks; PAU/inertia/blower function 2 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** m = 1400 kg, g = 9.81 m/s², A_f = 2.1 m², C_D = 0.30, f_r = 0.010, r_roll = 0.30 m, v = 108 km/h = 30 m/s, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, θ = 0, dv/dt = 0.

**(i) Total road-load force and absorbed power (4 marks):**

Rolling resistance:

$$F_{\text{roll}} = f_{r} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.010 \times 1400 \times 9.81 = 137.34 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 \times (30)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 \times 900 = 347.2 \text{ N}$$

Gradient (level road): F_grad = 0 N. Inertia (steady): F_inertia = 0 N.

**Total road-load force:**

$$F_{\text{road}} = 137.34 + 347.2 + 0 + 0 = 484.5 \text{ N}$$

**Absorbed power:**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = F_{\text{road}} \cdot v = 484.5 \times 30 = 14535 \text{ W} \approx 14.54 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Equivalent inertia (3 marks):**

$$I_{\text{eq}} = m \cdot r_{\text{roll}}^{2} = 1400 \times (0.30)^{2} = 1400 \times 0.09 = 126 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **road-load simulation** on a chassis dynamometer. Derive the equation for the total road-load force, identifying each term. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 250 kg motorcycle with one driven wheel (wheel radius r_w = 0.30 m) is tested on a single-roller chassis dynamometer of roller radius r_roll = 0.25 m. The roller inertia is supplemented by a flywheel of 12 kg·m². The combined rolling-resistance and drivetrain-loss coefficient is 0.015, frontal area A_f = 0.5 m², C_D = 0.55, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. The motorcycle runs at a steady 90 km/h. Calculate:
> **(i)** The total road-load force at the wheel. **(4 Marks)**
> **(ii)** The torque that must be applied by the absorption unit, and the power absorbed. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Road-load simulation and derivation (7 marks):**

**Road-load simulation** is the process by which a chassis dynamometer's PAU applies a force at the tyre-roller interface equal to the force a vehicle would experience on a real road at the same speed and acceleration. This allows stationary measurement of tractive force, power, fuel economy, and emissions.

**Derivation of total road-load force:**

Consider a vehicle of mass m moving at speed v on a road of slope angle θ. The forces opposing motion are:

1. **Rolling resistance** — due to tyre deformation hysteresis:

$$F_{\text{roll}} = f_{r} \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta = f_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$

2. **Aerodynamic drag** — from the pressure differential across the frontal area:

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{D} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$$

3. **Gradient resistance** — component of weight along the slope:

$$F_{\text{grad}} = W \cdot \sin\theta = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

4. **Inertial resistance** — force needed to accelerate the equivalent mass:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} = m_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

By **superposition**, the total road-load force is:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{road}} = f_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,A_{f}\,v^{2} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}}$$

For chassis-dyno testing, θ = 0 (level simulation) and the inertia term is implemented through the dyno's flywheel or electrical inertia equivalent.

*(Derivation: 4 marks; identification and explanation of each term: 3 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (Motorcycle 2W dyno):**

**Given:** m = 250 kg, r_w = 0.30 m, r_roll = 0.25 m, I_flywheel = 12 kg·m², f_eff = 0.015, A_f = 0.5 m², C_D = 0.55, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s, steady state.

**(i) Total road-load force at the wheel (4 marks):**

Rolling + drivetrain loss (combined coefficient):

$$F_{\text{roll,eff}} = 0.015 \times 250 \times 9.81 = 36.79 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.55 \times 0.5 \times (25)^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.55 \times 0.5 \times 625 = 105.3 \text{ N}$$

Gradient (level) and inertia (steady): both zero.

**Total road-load force:**

$$F_{\text{road}} = 36.79 + 105.3 + 0 + 0 = 142.1 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Torque and power absorbed (3 marks):**

Equivalent inertia at the roller:

$$I_{\text{eq}} = m \cdot r_{\text{roll}}^{2} + I_{\text{flywheel}} = 250 \times (0.25)^{2} + 12 = 250 \times 0.0625 + 12 = 15.625 + 12 = 27.625 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

Roller angular velocity:

$$\omega_{\text{roll}} = \frac{v}{r_{\text{roll}}} = \frac{25}{0.25} = 100 \text{ rad/s}$$

Torque required from PAU:

$$T_{\text{PAU}} = F_{\text{road}} \cdot r_{\text{roll}} = 142.1 \times 0.25 = 35.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Power absorbed:

$$P_{\text{abs}} = T_{\text{PAU}} \cdot \omega_{\text{roll}} = 35.5 \times 100 = 3550 \text{ W} \approx 3.55 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap** — Speed must be in **m/s**, not km/h, when used in F_aero. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors.
> 2. **Mass vs. Weight trap** — Rolling resistance formula uses **W = m·g** (weight in Newtons), **not** mass in kg. Writing F_roll = f_r · m (forgetting g) costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Inertia formula trap** — Many students write I_eq = m · r_w² (using wheel radius) instead of m · r_roll² (using **roller** radius). Always remember: **the inertia is set at the roller shaft**.
> 4. **Steady-state trap** — If "steady speed" is mentioned, dv/dt = 0, so the inertia term **must be dropped**. Forgetting this adds a fictitious force.
> 5. **Block-diagram trap** — For 7-mark "with neat block diagram" questions, **labelling every block** is essential; an unlabelled diagram earns only 1–2 marks.
> 6. **PAU explanation trap** — Do not confuse *eddy-current* (steady excitation, hysteresis loss) with *water brake* (variable stator filling). Examiners expect correct terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Chassis Dynamometer: High-Density Revision Notes**

- ✅ **Chassis dynamometer** = vehicle-on-rollers rig; static, repeatable, instrumented vehicle test.
- ✅ **Two main types**: 2W (motorcycle) and 4W (car); differ in power range, roller configuration, and inertia simulation.
- ✅ **Core road-load equation**: F = f_r·m·g + 0.5·ρ·C_D·A_f·v² + m·g·sinθ + m_eff·(dv/dt).
- ✅ **Power at roller**: P = F · v = T · ω (energy conservation).
- ✅ **Equivalent inertia at roller**: I_eq = m_test · r_roll² (+ I_flywheel if mechanical).
- ✅ **A, B, C coefficients** describe dyno target force: F = A + B·v + C·v²; obtained from **coastdown test**.
- ✅ **PAU types**: eddy-current (common, robust), AC regenerative (energy-efficient), water brake (high-power labs), hysteresis (low-power).
- ✅ **Cooling blower** is **mandatory** to simulate road air-flow; without it, radiator overheats and power drops.
- ✅ **Inertia simulation**: mechanical flywheels (stepped, cheap) vs. electrical (continuous, accurate).
- ✅ **Cycle tests on 4W dyno**: FTP-75, WLTP, NEDC, ECE 15 + EUDC. **On 2W dyno**: WMTC, IDC, ECE R40.
- ✅ **Tractive force at roller = road-load force** (fundamental equality used in all numerical problems).
- ✅ **Speed v must be in m/s** in F_aero formula; common conversion: 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s.
- ✅ **Steady state → dv/dt = 0** → no inertia term; level road → θ = 0 → no gradient term.
- ✅ **Roller radius** (r_roll) is **not** the wheel radius (r_w) — use r_roll in dyno inertia and torque equations.
- ✅ **C_D × A_f product** is sometimes tabulated as a single "drag area" parameter; for typical cars it ranges from 0.5 to 0.9 m².
- ✅ **Typical absorbed power at 90 km/h, 1500 kg car** ≈ 9–15 kW; **at 150 km/h** ≈ 30–50 kW — confirms v² dependence of aerodynamic loss.
- ✅ **Modern regulatory dynos** (WLTP, BS-VI) use **electrical inertia** + **AC regenerative PAU** for highest accuracy and energy recovery.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Vehicle Testing on Chassis Dynamometers (2W & 4W)**

*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme) | OEAUT831 — Vehicle Performance and Testing*

topics.title: Vehicle Testing Lanes (Side Slip Testers, Brake Testers, Headlight Alignment Testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Testing Lanes (Side Slip Testers, Brake Testers, Headlight Alignment Testing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: VEHICLE TESTING LANES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vehicle Testing Lane – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Testing Lane (VTL):** A dedicated, mechanized inspection track equipped with multiple integrated diagnostic stations that sequentially evaluate critical vehicle safety and performance parameters — wheel alignment (toe & slip), braking efficiency, and headlight beam aim — as the vehicle moves slowly over or through the testing equipment at prescribed speeds, typically under its own power at crawl speeds of **4 to 6 km/h**.

A Vehicle Testing Lane is essentially a one-stop automated diagnostic bay used by:
- **Authorised Service Centres** (OEM dealerships)
- **Government Inspection Stations** (e.g., Fitness Centres, RTO-approved centres)
- **Roadworthiness Testing Facilities** (PVT — Periodic Vehicle Testing)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Vehicle Testing Lane as a "Toll Booth of Diagnostics".** Just as a tunnel has a series of scanners — a license-plate camera, a smoke detector, a weighing bridge — a vehicle testing lane has a chain of mechanical/electronic testers laid out on the floor. The vehicle drives over them slowly, and each tester measures one specific safety attribute, just like a health check-up where you move from station to station (eye test → blood test → ECG).

### 1.3 The Three Core Testers — Overview

| S.No. | Tester Name | What it Measures | Safety Concern Addressed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Side Slip Tester (SST)** | Toe-in / Toe-out / Slip Angle of steered wheels | Steering stability, Tyre wear, Pulling tendency |
| 2 | **Brake Tester (BT)** | Brake force, Brake efficiency, Deceleration | Stopping distance, Accident prevention |
| 3 | **Headlight Alignment Tester (HAT)** | Beam aim direction, Lux intensity, Hot-spot position | Night-time visibility, Glare to oncoming traffic |

### 1.4 Physical Layout of a Typical VTL

A standard lane is **15 to 25 metres long** and roughly **3 to 5 metres wide**, with sequential stations arranged in a fixed order:

> **Sequence of Stations:** Entry Ramp → Wheel Play Detector → Side Slip Tester → Brake Tester (roller type) → Speedometer Tester → Headlight Alignment Tester → Pit / Lift → Exit

The vehicle enters the lane, is guided by a **canalised track** (rails or painted lines), and is subjected to each test in succession. Data from each station is recorded on a **central computer** which generates a **consolidated test report** for regulatory or service purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** In India, automotive testing lanes are specified under **AIS-052 (Automotive Industry Standards)** and the equipment conforms to **BIS / ISO standards**. The vehicle is driven at a crawl speed of **4–6 km/h** (typically **5 km/h** as a standard reference speed for side slip) unless otherwise specified by the OEM.

### 1.5 Why Vehicle Testing Lanes Matter — Engineering Motivation

A modern VTL helps in:
1. **Quantifying** otherwise qualitative attributes like steering "feel" or "headlight brightness"
2. **Compressing** what was once a day-long inspection into a **10 to 15 minute** automated pass
3. **Ensuring objectivity** — the same numerical value is produced regardless of the technician
4. **Generating legal evidence** for fitness certification and insurance claims
5. **Enforcing emission & safety compliance** as mandated by the Motor Vehicles Act
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SIDE SLIP TESTER (SST)

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Side Slip** refers to the inward or outward sliding tendency of a steered wheel as it rolls forward. If both front wheels are not parallel, the vehicle will **pull to one side** and the tyres will scrub against the road, causing rapid and uneven tread wear.

The **Side Slip Tester** measures this slip as either:
- **Slip Angle** (in degrees), or more commonly,
- **Slip Value** (in **mm/m**, i.e., millimetres of side displacement per metre of forward travel)

#### 2.1.2 Construction of a Side Slip Tester

The most common type is the **Slip Plate (Top Plate) Type**:

1. **Two non-powered slip plates** (left and right), each about **800 mm long × 500 mm wide**
2. Each plate rests on a **fulcrum/pivot** at its centre
3. A **measuring transducer** (LVDT, potentiometer, or strain gauge) is attached to the plate
4. The plates are mounted flush with the floor of the testing lane
5. The plates can slide **sideways by approximately ±10 mm** in a frictionless manner
6. A **recording device / digital display** converts plate displacement into mm/m

#### 2.1.3 Test Procedure

1. Vehicle is positioned with **front wheels on the slip plates**
2. Vehicle is driven straight ahead at **4–6 km/h**
3. Each wheel crosses its own slip plate
4. The plate, being free to move sideways, is dragged along by the tyre's slip tendency
5. The total sideways movement is summed over the plate length
6. Result is displayed as **In / Out** value in **mm/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Convention):**
> - **Positive (Inward / Toe-in):** Plates move towards the vehicle centreline — indicates toe-in condition
> - **Negative (Outward / Toe-out):** Plates move away from centreline — indicates toe-out condition

#### 2.1.4 Acceptance Limits

| Vehicle Class | Permissible Side Slip (In + Out) |
|---|---|
| Passenger Cars | **0 to +5 mm/m** (typically within **3 mm/m**) |
| Commercial Vehicles | **0 to +7 mm/m** |
| As per AIS-052 | **Within ±5 mm/m** for M1 category |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students confuse **mm/m with degrees**. They are NOT directly convertible without knowing the wheelbase. Always express the answer in **mm/m** unless specifically asked to convert.

#### 2.1.5 Relationship Between Slip Value and Slip Angle

The side slip value in mm/m and the slip angle in degrees are related by:

$$
\text{Slip Value (mm/m)} = 1000 \times \tan(\theta)
$$

where **θ** is the slip angle in radians (small angles approximation gives Slip Value ≈ 1000 × θ in radians).

For a more rigorous form, when the slip angle is in **degrees**:

$$
\text{Slip Value (mm/m)} = 1000 \times \tan\!\left(\theta \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right)
$$

#### 2.1.6 Toe-In and Toe-Out Distinction

The Side Slip Tester measures the **net** slip, but it does NOT directly tell the technician the toe value. Toe is measured separately using **wheel alignment equipment** (toe gauges, laser aligners, or 3D imagers).

$$
\text{Toe-in (total)} = \frac{\text{Front track} - \text{Rear track of front wheels}}{2}
$$

#### 2.1.7 Real-World Utility

Side slip testing is critical for:
- **Passenger cars** with independent front suspension
- **Heavy trucks** with twin-tyred front axles
- **Race cars** where even 1 mm/m of slip costs lap time
- **Pre-delivery inspection (PDI)** of new vehicles

---

### 2.2 BRAKE TESTER (BT)

#### 2.2.1 Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brake Tester (Roller-type / Dynamometer-type):** A roller-type dynamometer in which the vehicle's wheel is spun by a motor-driven roller. The brake is applied, and the **braking torque** is measured by the reaction force on the roller frame. This reaction force is directly proportional to the braking force at the tyre-road contact patch.

There are two principal categories:
1. **Plate (Skid) Type Brake Tester** — vehicle skids a locked wheel over a plate
2. **Roller (Drum) Type Brake Tester** — wheel rotates on a motor-driven roller; most common in modern VTLs

#### 2.2.2 Construction of Roller-Type Brake Tester

1. **Two rollers per axle** — one roller for each wheel position
2. The rollers are **motor-driven** (typically by a **7.5 kW to 15 kW** electric motor)
3. Rollers are **steel**, knurled or coated with high-friction material
4. The roller assembly is mounted on a **load cell** (or strain gauge bridge) at each end
5. **Load cell** measures the horizontal reaction force (= braking force)
6. A **speed sensor (encoder)** measures roller speed (and thus wheel speed)
7. A **digital display / computer** shows braking force, brake efficiency, and deceleration

#### 2.2.3 Test Procedure (Roller Type)

1. Vehicle is positioned on the rollers — one axle at a time
2. **Rollers spin up** to a test speed of **2.5 to 5 km/h**
3. Brake pedal is applied progressively (or fully) by the driver/operator
4. The locked or partially locked wheel transmits a **braking torque** to the roller
5. The **reaction force** on the roller frame is sensed by the load cell
6. Maximum braking force is recorded
7. Both wheels (left and right) are tested simultaneously

#### 2.2.4 Key Formulas for Brake Tester

**Brake Force at a single wheel:**

$$
F_b = T / r_r
$$

where:
- **F_b** = Brake force at the tyre contact patch (N)
- **T** = Braking torque transmitted to roller (N·m)
- **r_r** = Effective radius of the roller (m)

**Brake Efficiency (per wheel):**

$$
\eta_w = \frac{F_b}{W_w} \times 100
$$

where:
- **η_w** = Wheel brake efficiency (%)
- **F_b** = Brake force at wheel (N or kgf)
- **W_w** = Vertical load on that wheel (N or kgf)

**Overall Vehicle Brake Efficiency (CMVR prescribed):**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{\sum F_{b,\text{all wheels}}}{W_{vehicle}} \times 100
$$

where **W_vehicle** is the total kerb weight of the vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules) Standard:** As per **Rule 96** and **AIS-052**, service brake efficiency for passenger cars must be **≥ 50%** and for goods vehicles **≥ 45%** (with secondary brake ≥ 25%). Parking brake efficiency must be **≥ 18%** for M1 category vehicles.

**Deceleration from Brake Tester data:**

$$
a = \eta \cdot g
$$

where:
- **a** = Deceleration of the vehicle (m/s²)
- **η** = Brake efficiency (as a fraction, not percentage)
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

**Stopping distance (theoretical) from deceleration:**

$$
s = \frac{v^2}{2a}
$$

where:
- **s** = Stopping distance (m)
- **v** = Initial vehicle speed (m/s)
- **a** = Deceleration (m/s²)

**Imbalance between left and right brakes (an important diagnostic):**

$$
\text{Imbalance (\%)} = \frac{F_{b,L} - F_{b,R}}{\max(F_{b,L}, F_{b,R})} \times 100
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Brake imbalance > 20–25% is dangerous and leads to vehicle pulling to one side during braking. Many students ignore this in calculations.

#### 2.2.5 Roller Brake Tester Acceptance Limits

| Parameter | Passenger Car | Heavy Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Service Brake Efficiency | **≥ 50%** | **≥ 45%** |
| Parking Brake Efficiency | **≥ 18%** | **≥ 18–20%** |
| Brake Imbalance (L vs R) | **≤ 25%** | **≤ 25%** |
| Test Speed | **2.5–5 km/h** | **2.5–5 km/h** |

#### 2.2.6 Real-World Utility

- Pre-delivery inspection (PDI) at dealerships
- Fitness certification for commercial vehicles
- Insurance accident investigations
- Workshop diagnostics for brake pad wear
- Identifying hydraulic system faults (e.g., seized caliper)

---

### 2.3 HEADLIGHT ALIGNMENT TESTER (HAT)

#### 2.3.1 Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Headlight Alignment Tester (HAT):** An optical/photometric device that measures the **direction, intensity, and pattern** of the headlight beam relative to the vehicle's longitudinal axis and the road surface. Modern HATs use a **fresnel lens** to project the beam onto a viewing screen, where the **"hot spot" (brightest region)** and the **cut-off line** (for low beam) are evaluated for proper aim.

The two principal types are:
1. **Screen-Type (Mechanical) HAT** — Projector box with internal screen
2. **Photoelectric / Lux Meter Type HAT** — Uses light sensors to quantify intensity

#### 2.3.2 Construction of Screen-Type HAT

1. **Fresnel lens** (large diameter, typically **300–500 mm**) — collimates the headlight beam
2. **Internal viewing screen** marked with reference axes:
   - **Vertical line (H-H line)** for horizontal aim
   - **Horizontal line (cut-off check)** for vertical aim
3. **Movable / adjustable rails** for the lens box — to align with headlight centre
4. **Laser or optical alignment aid** for centring
5. **Lux meter** (optional) for intensity measurement
6. **Digital display** for the numerical values

#### 2.3.3 Test Procedure

1. Vehicle is parked on a **level surface**, with **tyres inflated to recommended pressure**
2. A **headlight alignment tester (HAT)** is placed in front of each headlight, at the **headlight centre height** (typically **750 to 1000 mm** from ground)
3. The tester is centred on the headlight using **alignment lasers / mirrors**
4. The headlight is switched on (low beam first, then high beam)
5. The image of the beam is observed on the internal screen
6. The **deviation** of the hot spot from the reference point is read in **cm per 10 m** or in **degrees**

#### 2.3.4 Reference Beam Aim — Beam Set Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Beam Set (CMVR / AIS-008 / AIS-010):**
> - The **cut-off line of the low beam** should be **below the horizontal (H-H) line** by approximately **0.5% to 1.0% of the headlight mounting height**
> - In practice, the cut-off is set so that, at **10 metres from the vehicle**, the bright/dark transition lies **50 to 100 mm below the headlight centre line (H-line)**
> - The high beam hot-spot should be **centred on the H-V point** (intersection of horizontal and vertical reference axes)

#### 2.3.5 Key Formulas for HAT

**Aim deviation in mm per 10 m (most common expression):**

$$
D_{10} = d \times \frac{10}{L}
$$

where:
- **D_10** = Beam deviation expressed per 10 metres
- **d** = Measured deviation on the tester screen (mm)
- **L** = Distance from headlight to tester screen (m, typically 1 to 3 m)

**Aim angle in degrees from deviation:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D_{10}}{10 \times 1000}\right)
$$

**where θ is in radians**; multiply by **180/π** to get degrees.

> **Common headlight aim values (low beam):**
> - Vertical aim: **0.5% to 1.0% downward** of mounting height
> - Example: If headlight centre is at **750 mm**, the beam should be aimed **3.75 to 7.5 mm lower** at 1 m from the headlight

**Required lux intensity (regulatory minimum, indicative):**

| Lamp Type | Minimum Beam Intensity |
|---|---|
| Halogen Headlamp (Low Beam) | **≥ 12,000 candela** on screen |
| Halogen Headlamp (High Beam) | **≥ 50,000 candela** on screen |
| HID (Xenon) Headlamp | **≥ 30,000 candela** (low) / **≥ 75,000 cd** (high) |
| LED Headlamp | As per AIS-008 / AIS-010 |

#### 2.3.6 Real-World Utility

- Ensures night-time visibility without causing glare to oncoming drivers
- Mandatory at fitness testing centres
- Critical after windscreen replacement or front-end collision repair
- Used during PDI of new vehicles

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slip Value from Slip Angle

**Given:**
- A wheel with slip angle **θ** (in radians, small)
- The wheel rolls forward a distance **L** (metres)
- The lateral (sideways) displacement of the tyre tread is **δ** (mm)

**To derive:** The slip value **S** in mm/m.

**Step 1:** For a small slip angle, the lateral displacement of the contact patch is geometrically given by:

$$
\delta = L \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

**Step 2:** For small angles, **tan(θ) ≈ θ** (in radians), so:

$$
\delta \approx L \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3:** Slip value is defined as lateral displacement per metre of forward travel:

$$
S = \frac{\delta}{L} \quad \text{(in m/m)}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$
S = \frac{L \cdot \theta}{L} = \theta \quad \text{(in rad/m)}
$$

**Step 5:** To express in **mm/m**, multiply by 1000:

$$
\boxed{S \text{ (mm/m)} = 1000 \cdot \theta \text{ (radians)}}
$$

**Step 6:** If the angle is given in degrees, first convert:

$$
\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{S \text{ (mm/m)} = 1000 \cdot \tan\!\left(\theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Brake Force from Roller Reaction

**Given:**
- A roller of radius **r_r** rotates at angular velocity **ω** (rad/s)
- A wheel of radius **r_w** sits on the roller with vertical load **W**
- Brake torque at the wheel = **T_b** (N·m)
- Roller-bearing friction and inertia are neglected (ideal case)

**To derive:** The expression for braking force at the tyre contact patch.

**Step 1:** When the wheel is braked, friction at the wheel–roller interface creates a tangential force. This force is transmitted to the roller as a torque opposing its rotation.

**Step 2:** Equilibrium of the wheel on the roller (force balance at the contact patch):

$$
F_b = \frac{T_{roller}}{r_r}
$$

where **F_b** is the tangential force at the roller surface, and **T_roller** is the torque reaction on the roller.

**Step 3:** In steady state with no slip (or with uniform slip), the wheel torque equals the roller reaction torque (by Newton's third law, action–reaction):

$$
T_{roller} = T_b \cdot \frac{r_r}{r_w}
$$

This arises because the wheel applies a force **F_b** at radius **r_w**, and the roller resists at radius **r_r**.

**Step 4:** The roller frame is mounted on a **load cell** that senses the **horizontal reaction force** F_R at the roller axle:

$$
F_R = \frac{T_{roller}}{r_r} = \frac{T_b}{r_w}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the **braking force at the tyre contact patch** is:

$$
\boxed{F_b = F_R = \frac{T_b}{r_w}}
$$

where **r_w** is the effective rolling radius of the wheel under load.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Overall Brake Efficiency and Equivalent Deceleration

**Given:**
- Brake force on each wheel: **F_b1, F_b2, F_b3, F_b4** (N)
- Total vehicle weight: **W = m·g** (N)
- **m** = mass of vehicle (kg), **g = 9.81 m/s²**

**To derive:** Overall brake efficiency and equivalent deceleration.

**Step 1:** Sum all individual wheel brake forces (during straight-line braking, all four wheels contribute):

$$
\sum F_b = F_{b1} + F_{b2} + F_{b3} + F_{b4}
$$

**Step 2:** Define overall brake efficiency as the ratio of total brake force to vehicle weight:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{\sum F_b}{W}
$$

As a percentage:

$$
\eta_{overall} (\%) = \frac{\sum F_b}{W} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** For uniform deceleration, apply Newton's second law to the vehicle:

$$
m \cdot a = \sum F_b
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute **W = m·g**:

$$
a = \frac{\sum F_b}{m} = \frac{\sum F_b}{W/g} = g \cdot \frac{\sum F_b}{W} = g \cdot \eta_{overall}
$$

where **η_overall** is now used as a fraction (not %).

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$
\boxed{a = \eta_{overall} \cdot g}
$$

**Step 6:** Stopping distance from initial speed **v** (m/s):

$$
\boxed{s = \frac{v^2}{2a} = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot \eta \cdot g}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Brake Tester

> **Problem:** A car of mass **1500 kg** is tested on a roller brake tester. The measured brake forces are:
> - Front Left: **4500 N**
> - Front Right: **4400 N**
> - Rear Left: **2200 N**
> - Rear Right: **2100 N**
>
> Calculate: **(a)** Total brake force, **(b)** Overall brake efficiency, **(c)** Equivalent deceleration, **(d)** Brake imbalance on front axle.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) Total Brake Force:**

$$
\sum F_b = 4500 + 4400 + 2200 + 2100 = 13{,}200 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (b) Overall Brake Efficiency:**

Vehicle weight:

$$
W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715 \text{ N}
$$

Brake efficiency:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{13{,}200}{14{,}715} \times 100 = 89.7\%
$$

**Step (c) Equivalent Deceleration:**

$$
a = \eta \cdot g = 0.897 \times 9.81 = 8.80 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step (d) Front Axle Brake Imbalance:**

$$
\text{Imbalance} = \frac{F_{b,FL} - F_{b,FR}}{\max(F_{b,FL}, F_{b,FR})} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{4500 - 4400}{4500} \times 100 = 2.22\%
$$

> **Verdict:** Imbalance is well within the safe limit (≤ 25%). Brake efficiency of 89.7% is excellent. Vehicle passes the brake test.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Headlight Aim

> **Problem:** A car has a headlight mounted at a height of **800 mm** from the ground. The HAT screen is placed at a distance of **3 m** in front of the headlight. The beam hot-spot on the screen is **45 mm below** the H-line of the HAT.
>
> Calculate: **(a)** The beam deviation per 10 m, **(b)** The aim angle in degrees, **(c)** Whether the aim conforms to the standard (0.5% to 1.0% downward of mounting height).

**Solution:**

**Step (a) Beam Deviation per 10 m:**

$$
D_{10} = d \times \frac{10}{L} = 45 \times \frac{10}{3} = 150 \text{ mm per 10 m}
$$

Equivalently, **D_10 = 15 mm per 1 m** = **15 mm/m**.

**Step (b) Aim Angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{150}{10 \times 1000}\right) = \arctan(0.015)
$$

$$
\theta = 0.8594° \approx 0.86°
$$

(approximately 51.6 arc-minutes, downward)

**Step (c) Conformance to Standard:**

Required downward aim (lower bound, 0.5%):

$$
0.5\% \times 800 = 4 \text{ mm at 1 m}
$$

Required downward aim (upper bound, 1.0%):

$$
1.0\% \times 800 = 8 \text{ mm at 1 m}
$$

Measured aim at 1 m = **15 mm**, which is **above the upper limit (8 mm)**.

> **Verdict:** The headlight is **aimed too low**. The driver will have reduced long-range visibility. Adjustment is required by turning the headlight levelling screw to raise the beam.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Side Slip Tester | Brake Tester | Headlight Alignment Tester |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Principle** | Geometric (lateral tyre slip) | Frictional (brake torque) | Optical (photometric) |
| **Sensor Type** | LVDT / Potentiometer | Load cell (strain gauge) | Lux meter / Fresnel lens |
| **Standard Speed** | 4–6 km/h | 2.5–5 km/h | Static (vehicle stationary) |
| **Output Unit** | mm/m | N, kgf, % efficiency | mm/10m, degrees, cd/lux |
| **Key Formula** | S = 1000·tan(θ) | η = (ΣF_b / W)·100 | D_10 = d·(10/L) |
| **Acceptance Limit** | ≤ 5 mm/m (cars) | ≥ 50% (cars) | 0.5%–1.0% down |
| **Test Duration** | ~5 seconds per pass | ~10 seconds per axle | ~30 seconds per lamp |
| **Failure Consequence** | Uneven tyre wear, pull | Accident, brake fade | Glare / poor night vision |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Side Slip Tester Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vehicle Driven at 5 km/h] --> B1[Front Wheel Rests on Slip Plate L]
    A1 --> C1[Front Wheel Rests on Slip Plate R]
    B1 --> D1[Slip Plate L Translates Sideways]
    C1 --> E1[Slip Plate R Translates Sideways]
    D1 --> F1[LVDT Transducer L]
    E1 --> G1[LVDT Transducer R]
    F1 --> H1[Signal Conditioner and ADC]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Microcontroller]
    I1 --> J1[Digital Display mm per m]
    I1 --> K1[PC Based Data Logger]
    I1 --> L1[Printer for Test Report]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Roller-Type Brake Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[Vehicle Positioned on Rollers] --> B2[Motor Drives Roller Pair at 5 km per h]
    B2 --> C2[Driver Applies Brake Pedal]
    C2 --> D2[Brake Torque Generated at Wheel]
    D2 --> E2[Tangential Force at Roller Surface]
    E2 --> F2[Reaction Force on Roller Frame]
    F2 --> G2[Load Cell Measures F Reaction]
    G2 --> H2[Signal Amplifier]
    H2 --> I2[ADC Converter]
    I2 --> J2[Microprocessor Unit]
    J2 --> K2[Display Brake Force in N or kgf]
    J2 --> L2[Compute Brake Efficiency percent]
    J2 --> M2[Compute Deceleration m per s squared]
    J2 --> N2[Compare with CMVR Limit and Pass or Fail]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Headlight Alignment Tester

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Vehicle Parked on Level Floor] --> B3[Headlamp Switched ON]
    B3 --> C3[Beam Enters Fresnel Lens]
    C3 --> D3[Lens Collimates Beam onto Screen]
    D3 --> E3[Hot Spot Visible on Reference Grid]
    E3 --> F3[Operator Marks Cut Off Line]
    E3 --> G3[Lux Meter Records Intensity]
    F3 --> H3[Microcontroller Calculates Deviation]
    G3 --> H3
    H3 --> I3[Display Aim in mm per 10 m]
    H3 --> J3[Display Intensity in candela]
    H3 --> K3[Compare with AIS Standard]
    K3 --> L3[Pass or Fail Result]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```
