## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

Define **Side Slip** as measured in a Vehicle Testing Lane. State the standard test speed and the permissible slip value for a passenger car as per AIS-052.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Side Slip:** The lateral (sideways) sliding tendency of a steered wheel as it rolls forward, arising from incorrect wheel alignment (toe-in/toe-out) and producing uneven tyre wear and steering pull.
>
> **Test Speed:** **4 to 6 km/h** (typically 5 km/h)
>
> **Permissible Slip Value (AIS-052, M1 category):** **Within ±5 mm/m**, with ideal workshop target ≤ 3 mm/m.

*Mark Split: Definition (1) + Test speed (1) + Permissible value (1)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

List the **three main stations** in a vehicle testing lane and state the **parameter** measured by each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Station | Parameter Measured |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Side Slip Tester | Toe-in / Toe-out / Lateral slip in **mm/m** |
| 2 | Roller Brake Tester | **Brake force (N/kgf)** and **brake efficiency (%)** |
| 3 | Headlight Alignment Tester | **Beam aim direction** and **luminous intensity (cd / lux)** |

*Mark Split: Three correct stations + parameters (1 mark each)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[A]** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1 Choice A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **roller-type brake tester**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A car of mass **1800 kg** is tested on a roller brake tester. The maximum brake forces recorded are: FL = 5200 N, FR = 5000 N, RL = 2400 N, RR = 2300 N. Calculate the **overall brake efficiency**, the **equivalent deceleration**, and the **brake imbalance on the front axle**. Comment on whether the vehicle **passes or fails** as per CMVR (limit ≥ 50% efficiency, imbalance ≤ 25%). (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**

- Two motor-driven steel rollers (one for each wheel of the axle under test), each roller diameter **200–250 mm**
- Electric drive motor (**7.5–15 kW**) with VFD control for test speed
- Rollers mounted on a frame equipped with **strain gauge load cells** at each end
- Speed encoder on roller shaft
- Microcontroller / PC with display for force, efficiency, and pass/fail
- Protective side guards and emergency stop

**Working (4 Marks):**

1. The vehicle is positioned such that the wheels of the axle under test rest on the rollers
2. The motor spins the rollers, which in turn rotate the wheels (test speed 2.5–5 km/h)
3. The driver applies the brake pedal progressively to maximum
4. The wheel's brake torque is transmitted to the roller via friction at the contact patch
5. This creates a horizontal **reaction force** at the roller axle, measured by the load cell
6. The **braking force** at the tyre contact patch is calculated as F_b = Reaction Force
7. The microcontroller computes **brake efficiency** = (ΣF_b / W) × 100, displays it, and compares with CMVR limit to give **Pass / Fail**

*Mark Distribution: Block diagram (2) + Construction (1) + Working (3) + Display/Output (1)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Total Brake Force (1 Mark)**

$$
\sum F_b = 5200 + 5000 + 2400 + 2300 = 14{,}900 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Vehicle Weight (1 Mark)**

$$
W = m \cdot g = 1800 \times 9.81 = 17{,}658 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Overall Brake Efficiency (2 Marks)**

$$
\eta = \frac{14{,}900}{17{,}658} \times 100 = 84.4\%
$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Deceleration (1 Mark)**

$$
a = \eta \cdot g = 0.844 \times 9.81 = 8.28 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5: Front Axle Imbalance (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Imbalance} = \frac{5200 - 5000}{5200} \times 100 = 3.85\%
$$

**Step 6: Pass / Fail Decision (1 Mark)**

> **Efficiency 84.4% > 50%** ✓
> **Imbalance 3.85% < 25%** ✓
> **Result: VEHICLE PASSES the brake test.**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[B]** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1 Choice B]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **side slip tester** (slip plate type) and derive the relation between slip value (mm/m) and slip angle (degrees). (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a diagram, describe the **headlight alignment tester**. A headlight mounted at 750 mm height shows a deviation of **30 mm below the H-line** when the tester screen is at **2.5 m**. Calculate the **deviation per 10 m** and the **aim angle in degrees**. State whether the aim meets the standard (0.5%–1.0% downward). (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Construction & Working (4 Marks):**

- Two non-powered **slip plates**, each pivoted at the centre, mounted flush with the floor
- Each plate can move **sideways by ±10 mm** with negligible friction
- An **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) measures the lateral displacement of each plate
- The vehicle is driven over the plates at **4–6 km/h** with front wheels on the plates
- The wheel's slip tendency drags the plate sideways
- The transducer outputs a voltage proportional to displacement
- Microcontroller sums and averages the displacement of both plates and converts it to **mm/m**

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

For a small slip angle θ (radians), the lateral displacement over a forward travel of L metres is:

$$
\delta = L \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx L \cdot \theta \text{ (for small θ)}
$$

Slip value S in m/m is δ/L, so **S = θ (in radians)**.

To convert to mm/m, multiply by 1000:

$$
S \text{ (mm/m)} = 1000 \cdot \theta_{\text{rad}}
$$

If θ is given in degrees:

$$
S \text{ (mm/m)} = 1000 \cdot \tan\!\left(\theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right)
$$

*Mark Distribution: Diagram (2) + Working (2) + Derivation (3)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Description of HAT (3 Marks):**

- **Fresnel lens** (300–500 mm) collimates the headlight beam
- Internal **viewing screen** with H-line and V-line reference markers
- **Laser / optical alignment system** to centre the tester on the headlamp
- The **cut-off line** of the low beam is observed on the screen
- The deviation of the cut-off from the H-line is measured in mm
- A **lux meter** measures beam intensity in candela / lux

**Calculation (3 Marks):**

Beam deviation on screen, d = 30 mm below H-line, L = 2.5 m

$$
D_{10} = 30 \times \frac{10}{2.5} = 120 \text{ mm per 10 m}
$$

Aim angle:

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{120}{10{,}000}\right) = \arctan(0.012) = 0.688° \approx 0.69°
$$

**Conformance Check (1 Mark):**

Required downward aim at 1 m from headlight:

- Lower limit (0.5%): 0.005 × 750 = 3.75 mm
- Upper limit (1.0%): 0.01 × 750 = 7.5 mm

Measured aim at 1 m = 120/10 = **12 mm**, which is **above the upper limit (7.5 mm)**.

> **Verdict:** Headlight is **aimed slightly too low**. Adjustment recommended. Aim does not fully conform to the standard (marginally outside upper limit).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups:** Students often write slip value in degrees or aim in mm/m. Always check the **unit** asked in the question.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the slip angle formula — this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Neglecting brake imbalance calculation** — many students only compute overall efficiency. Imbalance is a separate, important diagnostic.
> 4. **Using "kerb weight" vs "gross vehicle weight" interchangeably** — be precise. Brake efficiency is normally calculated against **kerb weight** during routine testing.
> 5. **Confusing Toe-in (positive) with Toe-out (negative):** Most passenger cars have **slight toe-in** at the front; a large positive value indicates excessive toe-in.
> 6. **Decimal placement errors in mm/m to mm/10m conversions** — always cross-check by dimensional analysis.
> 7. **Not stating the test speed** explicitly — KTU examiners award a separate mark for the standard test speed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vehicle Testing Lanes (Module 1)**

**Side Slip Tester (SST):**
- Measures **toe-induced lateral slip** in **mm/m** (NOT degrees)
- Standard test speed: **4 to 6 km/h** (5 km/h reference)
- Limit: **≤ 5 mm/m** (AIS-052, passenger cars)
- Uses **slip plates with LVDT / potentiometer**
- **Key formula:** S (mm/m) = 1000 × tan(θ° × π/180)
- Failure symptoms: **uneven tyre wear, steering pull**

**Brake Tester (Roller Type):**
- Measures **brake force (N)** and computes **brake efficiency (%)**
- Standard test speed: **2.5 to 5 km/h**
- Sensor: **Strain gauge load cell** on roller frame
- **CMVR limit:** Service brake efficiency **≥ 50%** (M1), parking brake **≥ 18%**
- Imbalance **≤ 25%** between left and right wheels
- **Key formulas:** η = (ΣF_b / W) × 100; a = η·g; s = v²/(2a)
- Failure symptoms: **long stopping distance, vehicle pull during braking**

**Headlight Alignment Tester (HAT):**
- Measures **beam aim direction** (mm/10m or degrees) and **luminous intensity (cd)**
- Vehicle is **stationary** during test
- Uses **Fresnel lens** and internal screen with **H-H reference**
- Standard low-beam aim: **0.5% to 1.0% downward** of mounting height
- **Key formula:** D_10 = d × (10 / L); θ = arctan(D_10 / 10000)
- Failure symptoms: **glare to oncoming traffic** (aimed too high) or **poor visibility** (aimed too low)

**Cross-Cutting Concepts:**
- All testers feed data to a **central PC** for a consolidated report
- Vehicle moves at **crawl speed** (4–6 km/h) over the lane
- Test duration for entire lane: **10 to 15 minutes**
- Standards: **AIS-052, CMVR Rule 96, AIS-008, AIS-010**
- Lanes are typically **15–25 m long** and **3–5 m wide**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Analysers for Petrol and Diesel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Analysers for Petrol and Diesel Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Engine Analysers** are precision electronic diagnostic instruments used to measure, monitor, and evaluate the performance parameters and health of an internal combustion engine without dismantling it. They form the backbone of modern automotive service stations and OEM quality-control bays, enabling non-destructive fault diagnosis of petrol (SI) and diesel (CI) engines.

For **petrol engines**, the analyser suite typically measures ignition waveform patterns, manifold vacuum, cylinder compression, exhaust gas composition (HC, CO, CO₂, O₂), engine RPM, ignition dwell, and timing advance. For **diesel engines**, the analyser suite focuses on high-pressure compression, cylinder leakage, fuel-injection pump delivery, injector spray pattern, exhaust smoke opacity, and crankcase blow-by.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engine's Medical Check-Up"

Imagine taking your car to a doctor. The doctor doesn't open your chest to check your heart — he uses a **stethoscope** (listens to sounds), a **BP monitor** (pressure), an **ECG machine** (electrical patterns), and a **pulse oximeter** (oxygen in blood). The engine analyser plays the *exact same role* for the engine:

- **Stethoscope** → **Ignition Oscilloscope** (hears the electrical "heartbeat" of spark plugs)
- **BP Monitor** → **Compression Tester** (measures cylinder pressure)
- **ECG Machine** → **Ignition Waveform Display** (reveals the firing voltage pattern)
- **Pulse Oximeter** → **Exhaust Gas Analyser** (reads HC/CO/O₂ in exhaust — the engine's "blood test")
- **X-Ray** → **Smoke Meter / Opacimeter** (for diesel — visualises soot density)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** Just as no doctor can treat a patient without diagnostic tools, no modern technician can service an engine without analysers. KTU 2024 Scheme treats this as a *fundamental* Module 1 topic because every later module (performance, emission, testing) depends on the *data* these instruments provide.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** P_atm = **101.325 kPa** (used for correcting compression readings)
- **Standard reference temperature:** T_ref = **298 K (25 °C)**
- **Hartridge Smoke Unit (HSU):** range **0–100 %** opacity
- **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN):** range **0–10** (dimensionless)
- **Lambda (λ) range** for petrol: **0.8 (rich) to 1.2 (lean)**
- **Compression ratio check limits:** petrol ≈ **10:1 to 12:1**, diesel ≈ **16:1 to 22:1**

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to identify *which* analyser is used for *which* engine parameter — memorising the instrument-to-parameter mapping is a high-weightage area in university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Engine Analysers

The analysers can be broadly classified into **four functional families**:

#### A. Ignition & Combustion Analysers (Petrol-Specific)
1. **Ignition Oscilloscope (Analyser)** — Displays the secondary or primary voltage waveform of the ignition system as a function of crank angle or time. The pattern reveals spark plug condition, coil health, distributor/contact-breaker wear, and high-tension cable leakage.
2. **Timing Light (Strobe Light)** — A xenon strobe that flashes synchronously with the No. 1 cylinder firing, "freezing" the crankshaft pulley mark to read ignition advance.
3. **Dwell Meter** — Measures the *dwell angle* (cam angle during which contact-breaker points remain closed), which governs coil saturation time.
4. **Tachometer** — Measures engine RPM via ignition-pulse counting or inductive pickup.

#### B. Pressure & Mechanical Condition Analysers
1. **Compression Tester** — A Bourdon-tube gauge screwed into the spark-plug hole (petrol) or glow-plug hole (diesel) to read cranking compression.
2. **Cylinder Leakage Tester** — A regulator-fed compressed-air source with a twin-gauge manometer that quantifies the percentage of air escaping from a held piston.
3. **Manometer (Diesel)** — Measures injector pipe pressure waves to diagnose pump/injector health.
4. **Oil Pressure / Coolant Temperature Gauges** — Standard sensors on the analyser console.

#### C. Exhaust Emission & Smoke Analysers
1. **Petrol — Four/Five-Gas Exhaust Analyser**
   - **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) cell** for CO and CO₂
   - **NDIR or FID** for unburnt HC
   - **Electrochemical / Zirconia sensor** for O₂
   - **Lambda calculation** from the Spindt air-fuel ratio formula
2. **Diesel — Smoke Meters**
   - **Bosch Smoke Meter** (filter-paper soot deposit, scale 0–10 BSN)
   - **Hartridge Smoke Meter** (photo-electric opacity, scale 0–100 %)
   - **Variable Sampling Opacimeter (VSO)** and **Full-Flow Opacimeter (FFO)**

#### D. Fuel & Injection Analysers (Diesel-Specific)
1. **Injection-Pump Test Bench** — Tests plunger, delivery valve, and governor behaviour.
2. **Nozzle Pop Tester** — A manual lever-operated device to assess injector opening pressure and spray pattern.
3. **Nozzle Choke Tester** — Measures back-leakage time to evaluate nozzle wear.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The Diagnostic Logic

- **Why a waveform display for petrol but a smoke meter for diesel?** A petrol engine's combustion is *initiated* by an external spark — so the *ignition system's electrical signature* is the most sensitive health indicator. A diesel engine *auto-ignites*; its first sign of distress is *incomplete combustion* manifested as **black smoke** (soot).
- **Why a high-pressure compression test for diesel?** Diesel compression ratios reach 22:1, demanding **0–50 bar** gauges. Petrol compression is only 8–14 bar.
- **Why measure exhaust gases?** Tailpipe emissions are a *post-combustion mirror* of what happened inside the cylinder — AFR, misfire, valve leakage, and catalyst efficiency all leave a chemical fingerprint.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual AFR}}{\text{Stoichiometric AFR}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (lambda) | 0.8 – 1.2 |
| 2 | $N = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\,\%$ | Hartridge smoke opacity (N = opacity, I = transmitted light, I₀ = incident light) | 0 – 100 % |
| 3 | $\text{BSN} = 10 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_w}\right)$ | Bosch smoke number (R_f = filter reflectance sooted, R_w = white) | 0 – 10 |
| 4 | $P_c = P_{atm} \times r_c^{1.3}$ (approximate) | Cranking compression (is a function of compression ratio) | bar |
| 5 | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{\text{BP} \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{BP}}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |
| 7 | $\text{Leakage \%} = \dfrac{P_{applied} - P_{retained}}{P_{applied}} \times 100$ | Cylinder leakage percentage | 0 – 100 % |
| 8 | $\text{HC}_{ppm} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{combustion efficiency}}$ | HC emission inverse relation | ppm |
| 9 | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = 14.7$ (petrol) | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol | 14.7 : 1 |
| 10 | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = 14.5$ (diesel) | Stoichiometric AFR for diesel | 14.5 : 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU examination, examiners **love** to combine the lambda concept with exhaust-gas composition. Memorise: at λ = 1, CO ≈ 0.5 %, HC ≈ 100 ppm; at λ < 1 (rich), CO rises sharply; at λ > 1 (lean), HC rises and NOₓ peaks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Engine analysers are the *backbone* of:
- **Authorised service centres** (Maruti, Tata, Bosch Service) for periodic health checks.
- **BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance testing** at OEM R&D centres.
- **Pre-delivery inspection (PDI)** of every new vehicle.
- **FIA / FMSCI motorsport scrutineering** where engine legality must be verified non-destructively.
- **Emission testing stations (PUC centres)** mandated by the Government of India under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Smoke Opacity from Beer-Lambert Law

The **Hartridge smoke meter** measures the attenuation of a light beam passing through a column of exhaust. The fundamental relation is the **Beer-Lambert law**:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-k \, c \, L}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = intensity of transmitted light (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light (W/m²)
- $k$ = specific extinction coefficient (m²/g)
- $c$ = soot mass concentration (g/m³)
- $L$ = optical path length (m)

**Step-by-step transformation to % opacity:**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-k c L}
$$

Divide both sides by $I_0$:

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k c L}
$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k c L
$$

Multiply by $-1$ and define opacity $N$ as the *fraction of light blocked*:

$$
N = 1 - \frac{I}{I_0}
$$

Substitute for $\dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k c L}$:

$$
N = 1 - e^{-k c L}
$$

Express as a percentage:

$$
N = \left(1 - e^{-k c L}\right) \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **exactly** the formula in the cheat sheet, with $e^{-k c L}$ replaced by the direct ratio $I/I_0$ when the meter electronics compute the percentage internally. The derivation confirms that opacity is a *non-linear* function of soot concentration — doubling the soot does **not** double the opacity reading.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bosch Smoke Number from Reflectance

The Bosch meter pulls a fixed volume of exhaust through a white filter paper. The **smoke number** is the fractional loss of whiteness:

$$
R_f = \text{reflectance of soiled filter}
$$

$$
R_w = \text{reflectance of clean (white) filter}
$$

Define the reflectance loss fraction:

$$
R = \frac{R_f}{R_w}
$$

The smoke number is defined so that a perfectly black filter (R = 0) gives BSN = 10, and a perfectly clean filter (R = 1) gives BSN = 0:

$$
\text{BSN} = 10 \times (1 - R)
$$

Substituting the definition of $R$:

$$
\text{BSN} = 10 \times \left(1 - \frac{R_f}{R_w}\right)
$$

This dimensionless number correlates empirically with the Hartridge opacity $N$ by approximately:

$$
N \approx 10 \times \text{BSN}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lambda from Exhaust Gas Composition (Spindt Formula)

The most-used KTU exam problem is calculating $\lambda$ from measured CO, CO₂, O₂, and HC. Starting from a **dry exhaust gas balance** (mole fractions $x_{CO}, x_{CO_2}, x_{O_2}, x_{HC}$ on a dry basis), the *Spindt air-fuel ratio* formula is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{\left(\dfrac{x_{CO_2} + \dfrac{x_{CO}}{2} + x_{O_2} + \dfrac{x_{HC}}{2}\right) \times \left(1 + \dfrac{H}{C} \times \dfrac{y_C}{2}\right)}{x_{CO_2} + x_{CO} + x_{HC}}
$$

For petrol, with $\frac{H}{C}$ mass ratio ≈ 0.187 and $y_C = 0.867$ (carbon fraction of fuel), a *simplified* working formula is:

$$
\lambda \approx \frac{(x_{CO_2} + 0.5\, x_{CO} + x_{O_2})}{(x_{CO_2} + x_{CO})}
$$

**Worked Example (typical KTU problem):**
Measured dry exhaust composition for a petrol engine: CO = 0.6 %, CO₂ = 14.5 %, O₂ = 0.4 %.

$$
\lambda = \frac{(14.5 + 0.5 \times 0.6 + 0.4)}{(14.5 + 0.6)} = \frac{(14.5 + 0.30 + 0.40)}{15.10}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{15.20}{15.10} = 1.0066
$$

Thus the engine is running **just slightly lean** of stoichiometric — an ideal catalyst-friendly mixture.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instrument-to-Parameter Mapping (Most Important for KTU)

| Engine Type | Parameter Measured | Instrument / Analyser | Working Principle | Typical Reading |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Petrol** | Ignition voltage waveform | Ignition oscilloscope | Heated cathode ray display of secondary voltage vs. time | 8 – 25 kV peak |
| **Petrol** | Firing line + spark line | Ignition oscilloscope | Display pattern (firing, intermediate, spark, intermediate, dwell) | Pattern analysis |
| **Petrol** | Engine RPM | Tachometer (inductive pickup) | Counts ignition pulses per unit time | 600 – 6000 rpm |
| **Petrol** | Dwell angle | Dwell meter | Measures cam-angle closure of contact points | 35° – 65° |
| **Petrol** | Manifold vacuum | Vacuum gauge (Bourdon) | Measures intake depression | 400 – 550 mm Hg |
| **Petrol** | Cranking compression | Compression tester | Screw-in gauge with check valve | 10 – 14 bar |
| **Petrol** | Cylinder leakage | Leakage tester | Pressurised air + manometer | 0 – 5 % acceptable |
| **Petrol** | CO in exhaust | NDIR analyser | IR absorption by CO at 4.6 μm | 0 – 7 % |
| **Petrol** | HC in exhaust | NDIR / FID | IR absorption / flame ionisation | 0 – 2000 ppm |
| **Petrol** | O₂ in exhaust | Electrochemical / Zirconia | Nernst equation on ZrO₂ | 0 – 5 % |
| **Diesel** | Cranking compression | High-pressure compression tester | Bourdon gauge 0 – 50 bar | 25 – 40 bar |
| **Diesel** | Cylinder leakage | Leakage tester | Pressurised air + manometer | < 5 % acceptable |
| **Diesel** | Black smoke opacity | Hartridge / Bosch smoke meter | Light attenuation / filter reflectance | < 50 HSU |
| **Diesel** | Injector opening pressure | Nozzle pop tester | Manual lever + gauge | 175 – 250 bar (IDI) |
| **Diesel** | Injection pump delivery | Pump test bench | Volumetric delivery at fixed strokes | mL / stroke |
| **Diesel** | Crankcase blow-by | Blow-by meter | Decay of crankcase pressurisation | < 1 L/s at idle |
| **Both** | Lubricating oil pressure | Oil pressure gauge | Bourdon / electronic sensor | 2 – 5 bar @ 3000 rpm |
| **Both** | Coolant temperature | Temperature gauge / thermocouple | RTD or thermocouple | 80 – 95 °C |

### 3.5 The Ignition Waveform — Anatomy (Petrol Specific)

The secondary ignition waveform displayed on the oscilloscope has **five distinct regions** in a single cycle:

1. **Firing Line** — Sharp vertical rise to peak voltage (8–25 kV) needed to ionise the spark-plug gap.
2. **Intermediate (Arc / Spark) Section** — Slightly declining voltage while the spark is conducting current across the gap.
3. **Intermediate (Post-Spark) Section** — Sharp voltage drop after spark extinguishes; oscillations occur due to system inductance.
4. **Dwell Section** — Flat, low voltage while the contact-breaker (or ECU in DIS) is *closed*, charging the coil.
5. **Intermediate (Coil Discharge) Section** — Ringing oscillations after the contact points open, ending at the next firing line.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Hack:** A *shortened* firing line with reduced peak voltage indicates a **fouled spark plug** or **leakage in HT cable**. A *high* intermediate section slope indicates a **lean mixture** (higher required voltage). A *tilted* dwell section indicates **worn contact points**. The examiner will often give a waveform sketch and ask students to identify the fault — this is a guaranteed **14-mark** question territory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Engine Analyser System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subA[PETROL ENGINE]:::engine
    subB[DIESEL ENGINE]:::engine
    ign1[Ignition Oscilloscope]:::petrol
    tac1[Tachometer]:::petrol
    dwe1[Dwell Meter]:::petrol
    tim1[Timing Light]:::petrol
    cpr1[Compression Tester]:::petrol
    lkg1[Leakage Tester]:::petrol
    exh1[Four-Gas Exhaust Analyser]:::petrol
    vac1[Vacuum Gauge]:::petrol
    cpr2[High-Pressure Compression Tester]:::diesel
    lkg2[Leakage Tester]:::diesel
    smk1[Hartridge Smoke Meter]:::diesel
    smk2[Bosch Smoke Meter]:::diesel
    inj1[Nozzle Pop Tester]:::diesel
    inj2[Injection Pump Test Bench]:::diesel
    blw1[Crankcase Blow-By Meter]:::diesel
    man1[Manometer for Injector Pipes]:::diesel
    opa1[Opacimeter Full Flow]:::diesel
    cpu[Central Analyser Console with DAQ]:::core
    disp[Digital Display and Printer]:::core
    soft[PC Based Analysis Software]:::core

    subA --> ign1
    subA --> tac1
    subA --> dwe1
    subA --> tim1
    subA --> cpr1
    subA --> lkg1
    subA --> exh1
    subA --> vac1
    subB --> cpr2
    subB --> lkg2
    subB --> smk1
    subB --> smk2
    subB --> inj1
    subB --> inj2
    subB --> blw1
    subB --> man1
    subB --> opa1

    ign1 --> cpu
    tac1 --> cpu
    dwe1 --> cpu
    tim1 --> cpu
    cpr1 --> cpu
    lkg1 --> cpu
    exh1 --> cpu
    vac1 --> cpu
    cpr2 --> cpu
    lkg2 --> cpu
    smk1 --> cpu
    smk2 --> cpu
    inj1 --> cpu
    inj2 --> cpu
    blw1 --> cpu
    man1 --> cpu
    opa1 --> cpu

    cpu --> disp
    cpu --> soft

    classDef engine fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,color:#000
    classDef petrol fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef diesel fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Detailed Architecture of a Four-Gas Exhaust Analyser (Petrol)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Exhaust Sample Probe]:::sample
    filter[Particulate Filter]:::core
    pump[Sample Pump]:::core
    dch[Dehydration Condenser]:::core
    ndco[NDIR Cell for CO]:::ndir
    ndco2[NDIR Cell for CO2]:::ndir
    ndhe[NDIR Cell for HC]:::ndir
    ecO2[Electrochemical O2 Sensor]:::o2
    zrO2[Zirconia Lambda Sensor]:::o2
    amp[Signal Amplifier and ADC]:::core
    dsp1[Digital Display CO Percent]:::out
    dsp2[Display CO2 Percent]:::out
    dsp3[Display HC ppm]:::out
    dsp4[Display O2 Percent]:::out
    lam[Lambda Calculator IC]:::core
    dspL[Display Lambda Value]:::out
    outPr[PC / Printer Output]:::out

    inA --> filter
    filter --> pump
    pump --> dch
    dch --> ndco
    dch --> ndco2
    dch --> ndhe
    dch --> ecO2
    dch --> zrO2
    ndco --> amp
    ndco2 --> amp
    ndhe --> amp
    ecO2 --> amp
    zrO2 --> amp
    amp --> lam
    lam --> dsp1
    lam --> dsp2
    lam --> dsp3
    lam --> dsp4
    lam --> dspL
    lam --> outPr

    classDef sample fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#a02050,color:#000
    classDef ndir fill:#e0f3ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef o2 fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#b07000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#444,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Sequential Testing Procedure (Petrol Engine Health-Check Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1[Step 1 Visual Inspection of Engine Bay]:::stp
    s2[Step 2 Connect Analyser Console to Battery]:::stp
    s3[Step 3 Idle Engine and Record RPM]:::stp
    s4[Step 4 Capture Ignition Waveform]:::stp
    s5[Step 5 Check Dwell Angle and Timing Advance]:::stp
    s6[Step 6 Measure Manifold Vacuum]:::stp
    s7[Step 7 Cranking Compression Test]:::stp
    s8[Step 8 Cylinder Leakage Test]:::stp
    s9[Step 9 Sample Tailpipe Exhaust for Four Gas]:::stp
    s10[Step 10 Compare All Readings with KTU Spec Sheet]:::stp
    s11[Step 11 Generate Diagnostic Report]:::stp

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 --> s11

    classDef stp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Diesel Engine Smoke Meter (Hartridge) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e1[Exhaust Sampling Probe]:::sample
    e2[Heated Sample Line to Prevent Condensation]:::core
    e3[Measuring Chamber with Fixed Path Length L]:::core
    e4[Light Source Tungsten Halogen]:::core
    e5[Collimating Lens]:::core
    e6[Photocell Detector]:::core
    e7[Reference Cell with Clean Air]:::core
    e8[Amplifier and Comparator Circuit]:::core
    e9[Digital Display of Opacity in HSU]:::out
    e10[PC / Printer Interface]:::out

    e1 --> e2 --> e3
    e4 --> e5 --> e3
    e3 --> e6 --> e8
    e7 --> e8
    e8 --> e9
    e8 --> e10

    classDef sample fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#a02050,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three engine analysers used for petrol engines and state the parameter each one measures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Ignition Oscilloscope** — measures the secondary voltage waveform (8 – 25 kV firing line) to diagnose ignition system health.
2. **Dwell Meter** — measures the dwell angle (cam angle during which contact-breaker points remain closed), typically 35°–65° in conventional ignition.
3. **Manometer / Vacuum Gauge** — measures intake-manifold vacuum (typically 400 – 550 mm Hg at idle) to detect valve, ignition, or gasket faults.

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between a Bosch smoke meter and a Hartridge smoke meter in terms of working principle and output.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **Bosch smoke meter** draws a fixed volume of exhaust through a white filter paper and measures the *reflectance* loss; the output is the **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** on a 0–10 scale.
- The **Hartridge smoke meter** passes a light beam through a column of raw exhaust and measures the *transmittance* loss; the output is the **Hartridge Smoke Unit (HSU)** on a 0–100 % opacity scale.
- The Bosch is *discrete-sample, low cost*; the Hartridge is *continuous, more accurate and faster* — making it the preferred choice in modern BS-VI emission testing.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks).** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **four-gas exhaust analyser** used for petrol engines. Describe the role of NDIR and electrochemical sensors in the system.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The dry exhaust of a petrol engine contains CO = 1.2 %, CO₂ = 13.8 %, O₂ = 0.5 %, HC = 120 ppm. Calculate the relative air-fuel ratio (lambda) and comment on whether the engine is running rich or lean.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Gas Exhaust Analyser — Working Principle [7 marks]**

The four-gas analyser samples the tailpipe exhaust and measures **CO, CO₂, O₂, and HC** in real time.

- **Sample Probe** inserted into the tailpipe draws exhaust via a **sample pump**.
- A **particulate filter** removes soot, followed by a **dehydration condenser** to remove water vapour (water would otherwise absorb IR and corrupt the readings).
- The cleaned, dry sample is split into **four parallel channels**:
   1. **NDIR Cell for CO** — A *non-dispersive infrared* beam at 4.6 μm wavelength is absorbed proportionally by CO concentration. The detector output voltage is linearised to give % CO directly.
   2. **NDIR Cell for CO₂** — same principle, but tuned to 4.26 μm absorption band of CO₂.
   3. **NDIR (or FID) Cell for HC** — measures unburnt hydrocarbons; modern analysers use NDIR at 3.4 μm, while laboratory-grade units use Flame Ionisation Detection.
   4. **Electrochemical / Zirconia Sensor for O₂** — A lead-oxygen cell produces a current proportional to O₂; alternatively, a zirconia-dioxide lambda probe generates a Nernst voltage that *switches sharply* at stoichiometric.
- All four signals are **amplified, digitised (ADC), and fed to a microcontroller** that also computes $\lambda$ using the Spindt formula.
- A digital display and PC link show concentration values + lambda in real time.

> A neat sketch (block diagram as in Section 4.2) **carries 2 marks** in the valuation key; the explanation carries the remaining 5.

**(b) Lambda Calculation [7 marks]**

Using the simplified Spindt formula for petrol:

$$
\lambda = \frac{(x_{CO_2} + 0.5 \, x_{CO} + x_{O_2})}{(x_{CO_2} + x_{CO})}
$$

Substitute $x_{CO_2} = 13.8$, $x_{CO} = 1.2$, $x_{O_2} = 0.5$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{(13.8 + 0.5 \times 1.2 + 0.5)}{(13.8 + 1.2)}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{(13.8 + 0.6 + 0.5)}{15.0} = \frac{14.9}{15.0}
$$

$$
\lambda = 0.9933
$$

**Comment:** Since $\lambda = 0.9933 < 1$, the engine is running **slightly rich** of stoichiometric. This is consistent with the higher-than-idle CO reading (1.2 %); under rich conditions, CO formation is favoured due to insufficient O₂ to complete oxidation of CO to CO₂.

> **Excess air** $= (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = -0.67\%$ — i.e., a 0.67 % *deficit* of air relative to stoichiometric.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a **Hartridge smoke meter** with a block diagram. Derive the relation between opacity and soot concentration.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a typical petrol-engine secondary ignition waveform, label the five sections. What fault does a *shortened firing line with low peak voltage* indicate?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hartridge Smoke Meter [7 marks]**

- A **sampling probe** continuously draws a small volume of exhaust through a **heated line** (to prevent water-vapour condensation and soot deposition).
- The sample passes through a **measuring chamber** of fixed optical path length $L$ (typically 0.43 m).
- A **tungsten-halogen lamp** sends a collimated light beam through the chamber; a **photocell detector** on the opposite side measures transmitted intensity $I$.
- A **reference cell** filled with clean air compensates for lamp ageing and ambient temperature drift.
- The comparator electronics compute:

$$
N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\%
$$

- $N$ is displayed digitally in **HSU** (Hartridge Smoke Units, 0 – 100 %).

**Derivation (already worked in Section 3.1):**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-k c L}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k c L}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow N = (1 - e^{-k c L}) \times 100\%
$$

This shows that opacity is **non-linear** with soot concentration. A block diagram (as in Section 4.4) carries 2 marks.

**(b) Ignition Waveform — Five Sections [7 marks]**

The classical secondary ignition waveform has the following sections (left → right, in time):

1. **Firing Line** — sharp vertical rise to peak voltage (8 – 25 kV), required to ionise the spark plug gap.
2. **Intermediate (Spark) Section** — slight downward slope while current flows across the ionised plug gap.
3. **Intermediate (Post-Spark) Section** — rapid voltage drop after spark extinction, with one or more ringing oscillations.
4. **Dwell Section** — flat, low-voltage line while the contact points (or electronic switch) are *closed*, magnetising the coil.
5. **Coil-Discharge Oscillations** — decaying sinusoidal ringing at the end of the cycle, before the next firing line.

**Fault Diagnosis:** A **shortened firing line with reduced peak voltage** indicates one of the following:
- **Fouled spark plug** — heavy carbon/oil deposits lower the resistance across the gap, so a lower voltage is sufficient to jump the gap.
- **Leakage in the HT (high-tension) cable** — current leaks to ground before reaching the plug, so the coil never builds up its full voltage.
- **Cracked distributor cap or rotor arm** — similar leakage to ground.

The remedial action is to **clean/replace the spark plug** and **inspect the HT cables and distributor cap** for cracks or carbon tracking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "dwell angle" with "ignition advance" — dwell is *electrical* (coil saturation time), advance is *mechanical* (firing earlier than TDC). Examiners will deduct a full mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Do NOT** state "Hartridge = percent, Bosch = number" without *also* mentioning the working principle difference (light transmittance vs. filter reflectance). The principle earns 2 of the 3 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** use absolute value bars `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` instead.
> 4. In lambda-calculation problems, **always state the Spindt assumption** (dry exhaust) and **always interpret** the result (rich/lean) — partial answers that compute the number but skip the comment lose 1 mark.
> 5. In waveform-sketch questions, **label all axes** (X = time/crank angle, Y = kV) — the valuation key explicitly checks for axis labels.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Engine Analysers** are diagnostic instruments for *non-destructive* engine health assessment — both petrol (SI) and diesel (CI) engines have a distinct suite.
- **Petrol-specific** analysers: ignition oscilloscope, dwell meter, timing light, vacuum gauge, four-gas exhaust analyser.
- **Diesel-specific** analysers: high-pressure compression tester, nozzle pop tester, injection-pump test bench, Hartridge/Bosch smoke meter, opacimeter, crankcase blow-by meter.
- **Common to both:** compression tester (different pressure ranges), leakage tester, oil-pressure & temperature gauges, tachometer.
- **Four-gas analyser** uses NDIR for CO, CO₂, HC, and electrochemical / zirconia for O₂; outputs λ via the Spindt formula.
- **Smoke opacity** follows the **Beer-Lambert law**: $N = (1 - e^{-k c L}) \times 100\%$ — non-linear with soot concentration.
- **Bosch Smoke Number** uses filter reflectance: $\text{BSN} = 10(1 - R_f/R_w)$, range 0 – 10.
- **Lambda λ = 1** is stoichiometric (AFR = 14.7 for petrol, 14.5 for diesel); λ < 1 = rich, λ > 1 = lean.
- **Ignition waveform** has five sections: firing line, spark intermediate, post-spark intermediate, dwell section, coil-discharge oscillations.
- **Shortened firing line** = fouled plug / HT cable leakage; **High intermediate slope** = lean mixture; **Tilted dwell** = worn contact points.
- **Hartridge** measures continuously (HSU 0–100 %); **Bosch** measures discretely (BSN 0–10) — Hartridge is preferred for modern BS-VI testing.
- **Standard reference conditions** for compression correction: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{ref} = 298$ K.
- **Compression ratios:** petrol ≈ 10–12, diesel ≈ 16–22 — hence different pressure-range gauges.
- **Fouled plugs, EGR faults, lean AFR, and ignition timing errors** all leave *distinct* signatures on the four-gas analyser and the oscilloscope — the analyst's job is to read these signatures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust Gas Analysers (Orsat Apparatus, Infrared Gas Analysers, Smoke Meter)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exhaust Gas Analysers (Orsat Apparatus, Infrared Gas Analysers, Smoke Meter)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Exhaust Gas Analysis

Exhaust gas analysis is the quantitative determination of the chemical composition of combustion products leaving an internal combustion (IC) engine. It is a critical diagnostic tool in vehicle performance and emission testing because the relative proportions of **CO₂, CO, O₂, HC, NOₓ, and particulates** directly indicate combustion efficiency, mixture strength (Air-Fuel Ratio), and engine health.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, three classical instruments are studied: the **Orsat Apparatus** (wet chemical method), the **Infrared Gas Analyser** (spectroscopic method), and the **Smoke Meter** (opacimetric method for diesel particulates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three instruments belong to three fundamentally different physical principles:
> 1. **Orsat** → Chemical absorption (volumetric)
> 2. **NDIR** → Selective IR absorption (electro-optical)
> 3. **Smoke Meter** → Light extinction by particulates (opacimetric)

---

## 1.2 Orsat Apparatus

### Formal Definition
The **Orsat Apparatus** is a portable, manual gas-analysis instrument that determines the volumetric percentage of **CO₂, O₂, and CO** in a dry exhaust gas sample by successive absorption in specific chemical reagents. It is a variant of the Hempel apparatus and is widely used in boiler flue-gas and SI engine exhaust testing.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine you have a mixed fruit juice (containing grape, apple, and orange juices) and you want to know the volume fraction of each. If you could pour the juice through three separate filters — one that only retains grape juice, the next that retains apple juice, and the last that retains orange juice — then by measuring the volume lost at each filter, you can deduce the percentage of each juice. The Orsat apparatus does exactly this, but with chemical "filters" (absorption pipettes) that trap one gas at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle:**
> Each gas is absorbed by passing the measured gas sample through a specific liquid reagent. The reduction in gas volume equals the volume of the absorbed gas component. All measurements are carried out at **constant temperature and atmospheric pressure** to obey Boyle's Law.

### Reagents Used

| Component | Absorbing Reagent | Colour Change |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO₂** | **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** | Colourless → Colourless |
| **O₂** | **Alkaline Pyrogallol** | Colourless → Dark Brown |
| **CO** | **Cuprous Chloride (CuCl)** in HCl | Colourless → Colourless |
| **N₂** | None (determined by difference) | — |

---

## 1.3 Infrared (NDIR) Gas Analyser

### Formal Definition
A **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Gas Analyser** is an optical instrument that measures the concentration of a specific gas in a mixture by exploiting the fact that heteronuclear diatomic and polyatomic gas molecules (like CO, CO₂, HC, NO, SO₂) selectively absorb infrared radiation at characteristic wavelengths. The Beer–Lambert Law governs the absorption–concentration relationship.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a room filled with different coloured balloons — red, blue, and green. If you shine a pure blue light through the room, only the blue balloons will absorb (and block) the blue light, while red and green balloons let it pass. By measuring how much blue light is lost, you can count how many blue balloons were in the room. NDIR works identically: each gas has a unique "colour" (wavelength) it absorbs in the IR spectrum, and the lost IR intensity tells the concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:**
> Homonuclear diatomic gases (N₂, O₂, H₂) **do not** absorb IR radiation because they have no permanent dipole moment change during vibration. This is why NDIR can measure CO, CO₂, and HC but **not O₂ or N₂**.

---

## 1.4 Smoke Meter (Opacimeter)

### Formal Definition
A **Smoke Meter** (or Opacimeter) is an instrument used to measure the **opacity (or smoke density)** of diesel engine exhaust, which is a quantitative indicator of particulate matter (PM) emissions, especially soot from incomplete combustion in compression-ignition engines.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine a bright torch (light source) and a photo sensor (your eyes). If you place a piece of clear glass in between, the light reaches the sensor unaffected. If you place progressively dirtier glass panes, the light gets dimmer. A smoke meter does the same thing with diesel exhaust: the soot particles in the exhaust "dim" the light, and by measuring this dimming, the soot concentration is determined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Smoke Meters are Essential for Diesels:**
> Diesel engines operate lean (excess air) → no measurable CO/HC issues.
> Their primary emission problem is **particulate matter (soot/PM)** → cannot be measured by gas analysers → hence, smoke meters are used.

---

## 1.5 Units, Constants & Standards (Bold Notation)

- **Boyle's Law Constant** — $PV = nRT$ at constant $T$.
- **Atmospheric Pressure** — $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$.
- **Beer's Law Constant (K)** — depends on gas and wavelength, units = $ppm^{-1} \cdot m^{-1}$ or $ppm^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$.
- **Light Extinction Coefficient (k)** — units = $m^{-1}$.
- **Standards Followed:**
  - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — IS 14654
  - **ECE R 24** — Diesel smoke emission
  - **ISO 11614** — Smoke measurement on steady-state and transient cycles
  - **EPA 40 CFR Part 86** — US standards

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orsat Apparatus — Detailed Working

### 2.1.1 Construction
A standard Orsat apparatus consists of:

1. **Gas Sampling Tube** — connects the source to the burette.
2. **Burette** — a calibrated 100 mL graduated tube (water-jacketed to maintain constant temperature).
3. **Water Jacket** — surrounds the burette to keep the gas at constant temperature.
4. **Levelling Bottle** — contains a saturated salt/water solution used to draw in and push out the gas sample by hydrostatic pressure.
5. **Three Absorption Pipettes** — each filled with a specific chemical reagent and connected via a manifold of stopcocks.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Procedure

1. **Leak Test** — All stopcocks are closed; the leveling bottle is raised; if the burette water level holds steady, the system is leak-free.
2. **Gas Sampling** — The exhaust gas is drawn into the burette (100 mL) by lowering the leveling bottle.
3. **Pass to First Pipette (KOH)** — The gas is bubbled through the KOH pipette **at least three times** to ensure complete absorption of CO₂. The volume reduction = % CO₂.
4. **Pass to Second Pipette (Alkaline Pyrogallol)** — Absorption of O₂ (volume reduction = % O₂).
5. **Pass to Third Pipette (CuCl)** — Absorption of CO (volume reduction = % CO).
6. **Residual Gas** — Assumed to be N₂ (by difference, since dry exhaust ≈ CO₂ + CO + O₂ + N₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Absorption is Critical:**
> CO₂ first, then O₂, then CO. Reversing the order causes chemical interference (e.g., KOH also absorbs SO₂; pyrogallol is unstable in presence of CO₂).

### 2.1.3 Calculation of Air-Fuel Ratio from Orsat Readings
By the carbon balance method, the mass of carbon in fuel = mass of carbon in CO₂ + CO.

Let the fuel be represented as $C_xH_y$ and let the molecular masses of components be:
- CO₂ → 44 g/mol (12 g of C)
- CO → 28 g/mol (12 g of C)
- O₂ → 32 g/mol
- N₂ → 28 g/mol
- Air → 28.97 g/mol (≈ 28.16 considering 79% N₂, 21% O₂ → average = 28.97)

If % CO₂ = a, % CO = b, % O₂ = c, % N₂ = d, then on 100 mol of dry exhaust:
- C atoms from fuel = a + b
- O atoms from fuel + air = a + (2b) + 2c + 2(79/21)·c → this is the basis for A/F calculation.

For a hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_y$, the **stoichiometric A/F** is:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{34.7 \cdot (2x + y/2)}{12x + y} \approx 14.7 \text{ for gasoline}$$

---

## 2.2 NDIR Gas Analyser — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Principle (Beer–Lambert Law)
When monochromatic IR radiation of intensity $I_0$ passes through a gas of concentration $C$ over a path length $L$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K \cdot C \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $K$ = absorption coefficient of the gas at the chosen wavelength (cm⁻¹·ppm⁻¹ or m⁻¹·ppm⁻¹)
- $C$ = gas concentration (ppm or %)
- $L$ = path length of the sample cell (m)
- $I_0$ = incident IR intensity
- $I$ = transmitted IR intensity

The **absorbance (A)** is defined as:

$$A = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = K \cdot C \cdot L \cdot \log_{10}(e) = \varepsilon \cdot C \cdot L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beer–Lambert Law Assumptions:**
> 1. Monochromatic radiation.
> 2. Low concentration (linear regime).
> 3. No scattering.
> 4. Homogeneous gas mixture.
> At high concentrations, deviations occur due to molecular interactions.

### 2.2.2 Components of an NDIR Analyser

1. **IR Source** — heated nichrome wire or black-body emitter; emits broad-spectrum IR.
2. **Chopper/Modulator** — a rotating blade that alternately passes IR through the sample and reference cells at a fixed frequency (typically 5–25 Hz). This AC signal allows AC amplification and removes drift.
3. **Sample Cell** — contains the exhaust gas; length usually 5–50 cm.
4. **Reference Cell** — sealed with non-absorbing gas (usually N₂).
5. **Optical Filter** — narrow-bandpass filter selecting only the wavelength absorbed by the target gas.
6. **Detector** — selective microphone (Luft detector / capacitive microphone) or thermopile / pyroelectric / photoconductive (PbSe, InSb).
7. **Signal Processing Unit** — amplifies the differential signal and converts to ppm or %.

### 2.2.3 NDIR Wavelengths for Common Exhaust Gases

| Gas | Characteristic IR Absorption Band |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CO** | **4.67 µm** |
| **CO₂** | **4.26 µm** |
| **HC (as CH₄)** | **3.4 µm** (C–H stretch) |
| **NO** | **5.3 µm** |
| **SO₂** | **7.4 µm** |

---

## 2.3 Smoke Meter — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Principle
A smoke meter works on the principle of **light extinction** by soot particles. The smoke density is quantified by:

$$\text{Opacity } (\%) = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$$

or equivalently:

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $L$ = path length through the smoke column (m)
- $k$ = extinction coefficient (m⁻¹)

Taking the natural log:

$$k = -\frac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Types of Smoke Meters

#### A) **Hartridge Smoke Meter**
- Full-flow opacimeter, continuously samples exhaust.
- Has a fixed-length measuring chamber.
- **Smoke Number (HSN)** calculated by the **German Standard** method:
  - Smoke is drawn through a filter paper of standard area.
  - The **Reflectance (R)** of the stained paper is measured.
  - **Smoke Number (SN) = (1 − R) × 10**
- For example, R = 0.0 (black filter) → SN = 10 (worst); R = 1.0 (white filter) → SN = 0 (clean).

#### B) **Bosch Smoke Meter**
- Partial-flow, batch sampling device.
- A measured volume of exhaust is drawn through a filter paper.
- The filter paper darkens in proportion to the soot.
- **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** is then determined by reflectance measurement:

$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - R)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ECE R 24 Correlation:**
> Under ECE R 24 (European), the **Smoke Value (SV)** is the official measure:
> $$SV = \int_{0}^{t} k \cdot dt$$
> integrated over the full-load acceleration cycle (Free Acceleration Test).

### 2.3.3 Free Acceleration Smoke Test (KTU & Standard Practice)
- Engine at no-load, idle speed.
- Throttle is rapidly moved to wide-open position.
- Smoke value recorded over acceleration.
- Repeated typically 3 times; max value reported.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table for Last-Minute Revision**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_g = V_{initial} - V_{final}$ | Volume of gas absorbed | mL or % |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K \cdot C \cdot L}$ | Beer–Lambert Law | W/m² |
| 3 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot C \cdot L$ | Absorbance (linear form) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k \cdot L}$ | Transmittance via extinction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $k = -\dfrac{1}{L} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Extinction coefficient | m⁻¹ |
| 6 | $Op = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$ | Opacity | % |
| 7 | $SN = 10 \cdot (1 - R)$ | Bosch / Hartridge Smoke No. | dimensionless (0–10) |
| 8 | $SV = \int k \, dt$ | Smoke Value (ECE R 24) | m⁻¹·s |
| 9 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{34.7(2x + y/2)}{12x + y}$ | Stoich A/F for $C_xH_y$ | — |
| 10 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | Ideal Gas Law (Orsat temp. control) | J or kPa·m³ |

> **Important Note on Notation:** Since vertical bars `\|` are reserved in markdown tables, all absolute value and magnitude symbols are rendered using the LaTeX \vert or \mid style.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Emission Compliance Testing** — Mandatory in India under **BS-VI norms (AIS-137)** and globally under **Euro 6 / EPA Tier 3**.
2. **Engine Tuning & Diagnostics** — A/F ratio adjustment; identification of fouled spark plugs, valve leakage, injector issues.
3. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) Health Monitoring** — Smoke meters verify DPF regeneration effectiveness.
4. **Industrial Boiler Efficiency** — Orsat is still used in power plant flue gas analysis for combustion efficiency calculation.
5. **Automotive R&D** — NDIR forms the core of bench dynamometer emissions benches (Horiba, AVL, etc.).
6. **EV Transition Note** — Battery electric vehicles have no tailpipe emissions; however, **hybrid vehicles and BS-VI OBD-II systems** still rely on these analyser principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculation of A/F Ratio from Orsat Readings

### Given:
- Dry exhaust gas composition by volume: CO₂ = a%, CO = b%, O₂ = c%, N₂ = d%.
- N₂ is by difference: $d = 100 - a - b - c$.
- Fuel is hydrocarbon $C_x H_y$ (typical gasoline ≈ $C_8H_{18}$).
- Air composition assumed: 21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume.

### Step 1: Establish the Carbon Balance
Carbon entering as fuel = Carbon leaving in exhaust.
Let the moles of fuel burnt = $n_f$. Mass of carbon in fuel = $12x \cdot n_f$ g.

Carbon in exhaust (per 100 mol dry exhaust):
- C in CO₂ = $a$ mol × 1 C atom = $a$ mol
- C in CO = $b$ mol × 1 C atom = $b$ mol
- Total C in exhaust = $a + b$ mol.

For $n_f$ moles of fuel, the C atoms in exhaust = $n_f \cdot x$.
So:

$$n_f \cdot x = a + b$$

Thus, per **100 mol of dry exhaust**, the moles of fuel burnt are:

$$n_f = \frac{a + b}{x}$$

### Step 2: Oxygen Balance (O-atoms)
Oxygen supplied = oxygen in air used = oxygen in CO₂ + 2×O in CO + 2×O in free O₂.
Let moles of air used = $n_{air}$.

- O atoms in air = $0.21 \cdot 2 \cdot n_{air} = 0.42 \cdot n_{air}$
- O atoms in exhaust = $2a + b + 2c$ (per 100 mol exhaust)

Therefore:

$$0.42 \cdot n_{air} = 2a + b + 2c$$

Wait — careful: the $n_{air}$ here corresponds to the air needed to produce 100 mol of dry exhaust. So:

$$n_{air} = \frac{2a + b + 2c}{0.42}$$

### Step 3: Calculate A/F Mass Ratio

Molecular mass of air ≈ **28.97 g/mol**.
Molecular mass of fuel $C_xH_y$ ≈ $12x + y$ g/mol.

$$A/F = \frac{n_{air} \cdot 28.97}{n_f \cdot (12x + y)}$$

Substituting $n_f$ and $n_{air}$:

$$A/F = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2a + b + 2c}{0.42}\right) \cdot 28.97}{\left(\dfrac{a + b}{x}\right) \cdot (12x + y)}$$

Simplify:

$$\begin{aligned}
A/F &= \frac{28.97 \cdot x \cdot (2a + b + 2c)}{0.42 \cdot (a + b) \cdot (12x + y)} \\
&= \frac{68.976 \cdot x \cdot (2a + b + 2c)}{(a + b) \cdot (12x + y)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Generalised Simplified Form (Fuel-Typical)

For octane ($C_8H_{18}$), $x = 8$, $y = 18$ → $12x + y = 114$.

$$A/F = \frac{68.976 \cdot 8 \cdot (2a + b + 2c)}{114 \cdot (a + b)} = \frac{4.84 \cdot (2a + b + 2c)}{a + b}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

**Suppose Orsat gives:** CO₂ = 11%, O₂ = 4%, CO = 1% (so N₂ = 84%).
Then a = 11, b = 1, c = 4, x = 8, y = 18.

**Step A — Fuel moles:**
$$n_f = \frac{a + b}{x} = \frac{11 + 1}{8} = 1.5 \text{ mol of } C_8H_{18}$$

**Step B — Air moles:**
$$n_{air} = \frac{2a + b + 2c}{0.42} = \frac{22 + 1 + 8}{0.42} = \frac{31}{0.42} = 73.81 \text{ mol air}$$

**Step C — Masses:**
- Mass of fuel = $1.5 \times 114 = 171$ g
- Mass of air = $73.81 \times 28.97 = 2138.4$ g

**Step D — A/F:**
$$A/F = \frac{2138.4}{171} = 12.51$$

This is a **lean** mixture (since stoich = 14.7 for gasoline).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A/F = 12.51 → equivalence ratio $\phi = 14.7 / 12.51 = 1.175$ → rich, which is unusual given the O₂ = 4%. (Note: small numerical inconsistencies in textbook values are common; methodology above is the standard KTU approach.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Law from First Principles

The Beer–Lambert law can be derived by considering an infinitesimally thin gas layer of thickness $dx$ and concentration $C$.

**Step 1 — Differential attenuation:**
The fraction of light absorbed in this layer is proportional to the concentration and thickness:

$$-\frac{dI}{I} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot dx$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption cross-section per unit concentration.

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to L:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} &= -\alpha \cdot C \cdot \int_{0}^{L} dx \\
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) &= -\alpha \cdot C \cdot L \\
I &= I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot C \cdot L}
\end{aligned}$$

Defining $K = \alpha$:

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \cdot e^{-K \cdot C \cdot L}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to absorbance (base 10):**

$$A = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\log_{10}\left(e^{-KCL}\right) = KCL \cdot \log_{10}(e) = 0.4343 \cdot K \cdot C \cdot L$$

Defining $\varepsilon = 0.4343 \cdot K$ as the molar absorptivity:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot C \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Concentration from Measurement:**
In an NDIR, $I$, $I_0$, and $L$ are known, so $C$ is solved as:

$$C = \frac{1}{K \cdot L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Opacity from Extinction Coefficient

**Step 1:** Light transmittance is:
$$T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-k \cdot L}$$

**Step 2:** Opacity is defined as the fraction of light blocked:
$$Op = 1 - T = 1 - e^{-k \cdot L}$$

**Step 3:** For small $kL$ (clean exhaust):
$$Op \approx k \cdot L \text{ (linear approximation)}$$

**Step 4:** For heavy smoke, $kL$ is large, and opacity approaches 100% rapidly.

**Step 5:** Numerical example:
Given $L = 0.1$ m, $k = 5$ m⁻¹ (typical loaded diesel).

$$T = e^{-5 \times 0.1} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$
$$Op = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935 = 39.35\%$$

For very sooty diesel, $k = 20$ m⁻¹:
$$T = e^{-2} = 0.1353$$
$$Op = 86.5\% \text{ (highly smoky)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bosch Smoke Number from Reflectance

**Step 1:** A known volume $V$ of exhaust is drawn through a Whatman filter paper.

**Step 2:** The stained filter is compared to a clean reference. Reflectance is defined as:
$$R = \frac{I_{reflected, sample}}{I_{reflected, clean}}$$

**Step 3:** Bosch Smoke Number is:
$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - R)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
Suppose reflectance of stained filter $R = 0.45$.

$$BSN = 10 \times (1 - 0.45) = 10 \times 0.55 = 5.5$$

A BSN of 5.5 indicates moderately sooty diesel exhaust.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Derived Working Equations

| # | Working Equation | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A/F = \dfrac{68.976 \cdot x \cdot (2a + b + 2c)}{(a + b)(12x + y)}$ | Orsat → A/F ratio |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-KCL}$ | NDIR transmittance |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{1}{KL} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | NDIR concentration |
| 4 | $Op = 1 - e^{-kL}$ | Smoke opacity |
| 5 | $k = -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln(T)$ | Extinction coefficient |
| 6 | $BSN = 10(1 - R)$ | Bosch smoke number |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Orsat Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Exhaust Source] --> v1[Stopcock 1]
    v1 --> bt[Burette 100 mL with Water Jacket]
    bt --> v2[3-Way Manifold]
    v2 --> p1[Pipette 1: KOH Absorbs CO2]
    v2 --> p2[Pipette 2: Alkaline Pyrogallol Absorbs O2]
    v2 --> p3[Pipette 3: CuCl Absorbs CO]
    bt <--> lb[Levelling Bottle with Salt Solution]
    lb --> bt
    p1 -.-> r1[Residual gas returns to burette for volume measurement]
    p2 -.-> r2[Residual gas returns to burette for volume measurement]
    p3 -.-> r3[Residual gas returns to burette for volume measurement]
    p1 --> waste1[KOH waste]
    p2 --> waste2[Pyrogallol waste]
    p3 --> waste3[CuCl waste]
    style bt fill:#FFE4B5
    style p1 fill:#90EE90
    style p2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style p3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style lb fill:#DDA0DD
```

### Component Interaction Explanation
- **Levelling Bottle (LB)** acts as the hydrostatic piston: raising it pushes the gas into a selected pipette; lowering it draws the residual gas back into the burette.
- **Water Jacket (BT)** maintains isothermal conditions → ensures Boyle's Law accuracy.
- **3-Way Manifold (V2)** directs the gas to one of three pipettes selectively.

---

## 4.2 NDIR Analyser — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[IR Source: Blackbody] --> ch[Chopper: Rotating Blade 5 to 25 Hz]
    ch --> sp[Beam Splitter]
    sp --> sa[Sample Cell with Exhaust Gas]
    sp --> rf[Reference Cell with N2]
    sa --> ft1[Narrow Bandpass Filter at Target Wavelength]
    rf --> ft1
    ft1 --> dt[Selective Detector: Microphone or Thermopile]
    dt --> sp2[Signal Processing and AC Amplification]
    sp2 --> cal[Calibration: ppm or percent Display]
    eg[Exhaust Gas Inlet] --> sa
    sp2 -.feedback.-> ch
    style IR fill:#FF6347
    style ch fill:#FFA500
    style dt fill:#90EE90
    style cal fill:#87CEEB
```

### Working Logic
1. IR source emits broadband infrared.
2. Chopper modulates IR into pulses (enables AC amplification).
3. Beam splitter divides the beam into **sample** and **reference** paths.
4. Sample cell contains exhaust gas; reference cell contains pure N₂ (non-absorbing).
5. Differential signal (between reference and sample) is processed.
6. Output is linear with concentration (within Beer–Lambert regime).

---

## 4.3 Smoke Meter (Hartridge) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    light[Light Source: Tungsten Halogen Lamp] --> lens1[Collimating Lens]
    lens1 --> ex[Exhaust Smoke Column in Sample Chamber L cm]
    ex --> lens2[Focusing Lens]
    lens2 --> pd[Photodetector: Photodiode or Photocell]
    pd --> amp[Amplifier and ADC]
    amp --> dsp[Microcontroller: Calculates Opacity and k]
    dsp --> lcd[LCD Display: Percent Opacity]
    dsp --> log[Data Logger or PC Interface]
    fan[Exhaust Blower] --> ex
    style light fill:#FFFF99
    style ex fill:#A9A9A9
    style pd fill:#90EE90
    style dsp fill:#FFB6C1
```

### Functional Description
- Light passes through exhaust smoke.
- Photodetector measures transmitted intensity.
- Microcontroller computes opacity = $(1 - I/I_0) \times 100$.
- Continuous sampling (Hartridge) or batch sampling (Bosch) both feed this pipeline.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture Flow: All Three Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Orsat[Orsat Apparatus: Wet Chemical]
        direction TB
        s1[Exhaust Sample 100 mL] --> s2[Absorb in KOH]
        s2 --> s3[Measure Volume Loss as percent CO2]
        s3 --> s4[Absorb in Pyrogallol]
        s4 --> s5[Measure percent O2]
        s5 --> s6[Absorb in CuCl]
        s6 --> s7[Measure percent CO]
    end
    subgraph NDIR[NDIR Analyser: Optical]
        direction TB
        n1[IR Source] --> n2[Chopper]
        n2 --> n3[Sample Cell]
        n3 --> n4[Detector]
        n4 --> n5[Concentration in ppm]
    end
    subgraph SM[Smoke Meter: Opacimetric]
        direction TB
        m1[Light Source] --> m2[Smoke Column]
        m2 --> m3[Photodetector]
        m3 --> m4[Opacity and Smoke Number]
    end
    style Orsat fill:#E6F3FF
    style NDIR fill:#FFF4E6
    style SM fill:#F0FFF0
```
