This is why designers aim for a **rectangularized crash pulse** — it is the **optimal shape** for minimizing peak occupant force. ### 3.3 Derivation of Crumple Zone Energy Absorption A front longitudinal member of length $L_0$ collapses to length $L_1$ under axial impact. The **crush distance** is: $$ \delta = L_0 - L_1 $$ Assume the collapse force is approximately constant at $\bar{F}$ during the stroke: $$ E_{abs} = \int_0^{\delta} F(x)\, dx = \bar{F} \cdot \delta $$ By energy conservation, the absorbed energy equals the initial kinetic energy: $$ \bar{F} \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 $$ Solving for the average structural force: $$ \bar{F} = \frac{m v_0^2}{2 \delta} $$ The **mean deceleration** of the passenger compartment (which has mass $m_c$) is: $$ \bar{a} = \frac{\bar{F}}{m_c} = \frac{m v_0^2}{2 \delta m_c} $$ If $m = m_c$ (rigid barrier, all vehicle mass decelerates): $$ \bar{a} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \delta} $$ **Numerical example:** $v_0 = 15.56$ m/s, $\delta = 0.5$ m: $$ \bar{a} = \frac{(15.56)^2}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{242.1}{1.0} = 242.1\,m/s^2 = 24.7\,g $$ This is below the 30 g limit that a restrained Hybrid III can typically survive. ✅ ### 3.4 Derivation of Neck Injury Criterion (NIC) The NIC was developed by **Boström et al. (1996)** for rear-end impacts. It is based on the **pressure gradient** in the cervical spine, which correlates with whiplash injury. Define the relative velocity and acceleration between head and T1 (first thoracic vertebra): $$ v_{rel}(t) = v_{head}(t) - v_{T1}(t) $$ $$ a_{rel}(t) = a_{head}(t) - a_{T1}(t) $$ The NIC formula: $$ NIC(t) = 0.2 \cdot a_{rel}(t) + v_{rel}(t) $$ The peak NIC is compared to a threshold of **15 m/s²** for whiplash risk. **Derivation logic:** The first term captures **inertial** loading (proportional to relative acceleration), while the second term captures **kinematic** separation (the head rebounds faster than the torso in whiplash). ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right ATD | Test Type | Primary ATD | Secondary ATD | Threshold HIC | Threshold Chest | Threshold Femur | |-----------|------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|----------------| | FMVSS 208 Frontal Driver | Hybrid III 50M | THOR-5F (NHTSA) | 1000 | 63 mm | 10 kN | | FMVSS 208 Frontal Passenger | Hybrid III 5F | — | 1000 | 63 mm | 10 kN | | FMVSS 214 Side | ES-2 / SID-IIs | WorldSID | 1000 | 12 mm rib defl. (SID) | — | | ECE R94 Frontal | Hybrid III 50M | — | 1000 | 50 mm | 10 kN | | ECE R95 Side | EuroSID-1 (ES-1) | WorldSID | 1000 | 42 mm | — | | Rear Impact (Whiplash) | BioRID II | — | — | — | NIC ≤ 15 m/s² | | Child restraint | Q3, Q6, Q10 | — | 1000 | 52 mm | — | | Pedestrian impact | Polor 2G, Hybrid III legform | — | — | — | Tibia index ≤ 1.0 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ATD Measurement System > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture of an ATD's data acquisition chain — from sensor excitation to final injury criterion computation. This is a block-level functional architecture flow (not a physical drawing), as the OEM (e.g., Humanetics) ATDs are sealed units and a physical schematic is not pedagogically useful at this level. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crash Event Initiation
Vehicle Impact Begins] --> B[ATD Sensor Excitation
Accelerometers and Load Cells] B --> C1[Head Triad
3 axis Accelerometer] B --> C2[Neck Load Cell
6 axis Force Moment] B --> C3[Thorax Potentiometer
Chest Deflection] B --> C4[Spine Load Cell
6 axis Lumbar] B --> C5[Femur Load Cell
2 per leg] B --> C6[Tibia Transducers
Bending and Axial] B --> C7[Foot Accelerometers
2 per foot] C1 --> D[Analog Signal Conditioning
Charge Amplifier and Filter] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D C6 --> D C7 --> D D --> E[CFC Filtering
CFC 60, CFC 180, CFC 1000] E --> F[12 bit ADC
10 kHz Sample Rate] F --> G[Onboard Data Logger
TDAS or DTS] G --> H[Post Processing Software
DTS DataPRO or similar] H --> I1[HIC15 Calculation] H --> I2[NIC Calculation] H --> I3[Nij Calculation] H --> I4[Chest Trauma Index] H --> I5[Femur Load Peak] H --> I6[Tibia Index] I1 --> J[Injury Assessment Report] I2 --> J I3 --> J I4 --> J I5 --> J I6 --> J J --> K{Compliance Check
All Metrics Below Threshold} K --> L[Pass: Vehicle Homologated] K --> M[Fail: Design Iteration Required] M --> N[Restraint System Redesign] N --> O[Structure Reinforcement] O --> A ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Crash Pulse Flow through Vehicle Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN[Impact Event] A1[Impact Velocity v0
56 kmh typical] end subgraph CZone[Front Crumple Zone] B1[Energy Absorption
0.5 m crush stroke] B2[Forces Managed
Average 200 kN] B3[Deformation Pattern
Axial Buckling] end subgraph Pulse[Crash Pulse Shaping] C1[Deceleration Pulse
20 to 30 g peak] C2[Duration 80 to 120 ms] C3[Shape Targeted Rectangular] end subgraph Cell[Passenger Cell Survival Space] D1[A pillar integrity] D2[B pillar integrity] D3[Roof rail integrity] D4[Floor pan integrity] end subgraph Rest[Restraint Subsystem] E1[Seat Belt with Pretensioner] E2[Frontal Airbag with Dual Stage] E3[Load Limiter at 4 kN] E4[Knee Bolster] end subgraph Occ[Occupant ATD Loading] F1[Hybrid III 50M] F2[Head Acceleration HIC15] F3[Chest Deflection CTI] F4[Femur Compression Force] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> D1 C3 --> D2 C3 --> D3 C3 --> D4 D1 --> E1 D1 --> E2 D1 --> E3 D1 --> E4 E1 --> F1 E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 E4 --> F1 F1 --> F2 F1 --> F3 F1 --> F4 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Crashworthiness Design Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vehicle Crash Event] --> B{Vehicle Architect} B --> B1[Frontal Architecture] B --> B2[Side Architecture] B --> B3[Rear Architecture] B --> B4[Rollover Architecture] B1 --> C1[Lower Load Path
Engine Cradle and Rails] B1 --> C2[Upper Load Path
Apron and Shock Towers] B1 --> C3[Pedestrian Protection
Energy Absorbing Bumper] B2 --> D1[B Pillar Reinforcement
Ultra High Strength Steel] B2 --> D2[Side Door Beams
Aluminum or Boron Steel] B2 --> D3[Sill Reinforcement
Hot Stamped Steel] B2 --> D4[Curtain Airbag Coverage] B3 --> E1[Rear Crumple Zone
0.4 m crush stroke] B3 --> E2[Rear Seatback Strength
Prevent Penetration] B4 --> F1[Roof Strength
Maintain Survival Space] B4 --> F2[A pillar Curtain Airbag] B4 --> F3[Seatbelt Pretensioner Integration] C1 --> G[Crash Pulse Attenuation] C2 --> G D1 --> G D2 --> G D3 --> G E1 --> G F1 --> G G --> H[ATD Injury Metric Pass] H --> I[NCAP Star Rating] I --> J[Production Vehicle] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > > **Q1. Define Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD) and list any four types of ATDs used in vehicle crash testing.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > An **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** is a biofidelic mechanical surrogate of the human body instrumented with sensors (accelerometers, load cells, potentiometers) at injury-relevant anatomical locations. It is used in vehicle crash tests to record occupant kinematic and dynamic responses for injury severity assessment. > > Four types of ATDs: > 1. **Hybrid III 50M** — 50th percentile male for frontal impact (FMVSS 208) > 2. **WorldSID** — 50th percentile male for side impact (ECE R95, IIHS) > 3. **SID-IIs** — 5th percentile female for side impact > 4. **Q-Series (Q3, Q6, Q10)** — Child dummies for child restraint system evaluation > > *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for types)* > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > > **Q2. What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)? Write the HIC₁₅ formula and state its regulatory threshold value.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > The **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** is a dimensionless numerical index that quantifies the severity of head injury based on the resultant head acceleration time-history. It is derived from the Wayne State University Tolerance Curve. > > Formula: > > $$HIC_{15} = \left[ \frac{1}{0.015} \int_{t_1}^{t_1+0.015} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (0.015)$$ > > where $a(t)$ is the resultant head acceleration in $m/s^2$. > > **Regulatory threshold (FMVSS 208): HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000.** > > *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for threshold)* ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** #### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain in detail the various types of crash test dummies (ATDs) used in frontal, side, and rear impact tests, with their salient instrumentation features. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Hybrid III dummy's head experiences a rectangular acceleration pulse of magnitude **70 g** for a duration of **10 ms** during a frontal crash test. Calculate the HIC₁₅ value. State whether the dummy passes the FMVSS 208 threshold of HIC₁₅ = 1000. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (Question A) **(a) Types of Crash Test Dummies — 7 marks** The crash test dummies are classified based on the impact direction, occupant size, and biofidelity level. **1. Frontal Impact Dummies:** - **Hybrid III 50M (50th Percentile Male):** The most widely used frontal impact ATD. Stature 175 cm, mass 78 kg. Features include: triaxial head accelerometer, six-axis upper and lower neck load cells, three chest deflection potentiometers, six-axis lumbar spine load cell, three-axis pelvis accelerometer, two femur load cells (one per leg), and two lower tibia load cells with bending moment measurement. (2 marks) - **THOR (Test device for Human Occupant Restraint):** Next-generation frontal dummy offering superior thoracic biofidelity through chestband technology (six horizontal bands measuring rib-cage deformation continuously), iliac load cell, and a more biofidelic shoulder. Variants: THOR-50M, THOR-5F, THOR-95F. (1 mark) - **Hybrid III 5F (5th Percentile Female):** Stature 151 cm, mass 49 kg; used for passenger-side assessment. (0.5 marks) **2. Side Impact Dummies:** - **WorldSID:** Modern 50th percentile male side impact dummy. Each rib is individually instrumented with strain gauges; contains pubic symphysis load cell, thoracic and abdominal deflection sensors, and shoulder force sensor. (1.5 marks) - **ES-2 (EuroSID-2):** Predecessor to WorldSID; two rib modules (thoracic and abdominal). (0.5 marks) - **SID-IIs (Side Impact Dummy, Small):** 5th percentile female side impact dummy; represents the smallest adult occupant. (0.5 marks) **3. Rear Impact (Whiplash) Dummies:** - **BioRID II:** A seated, reclined dummy specifically designed for whiplash and rear-impact studies. Contains seven cervical spine segments with bending moment measurement. (0.5 marks) **4. Child Dummies:** - **Q-Series (Q0 newborn to Q10 ten-year-old):** Family of child dummies for CRS testing. (0.5 marks) **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **Given:** - $a = 70\,g = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7\,m/s^2$ - Pulse duration = $10\,ms = 0.010\,s$ - Pulse shape = rectangular - Threshold: $HIC_{15} \leq 1000$ **Step 1:** Compute the integral of acceleration over the entire pulse: $$ \int_0^{0.010} 686.7\, dt = 686.7 \times 0.010 = 6.867\,m/s $$ **Step 2:** HIC₁₅ uses a 15 ms window. Since the actual pulse is 10 ms long, the window extends from $t_1 = 0$ to $t_1 + 0.015 = 0.015$ s. Beyond 0.010 s, the acceleration is zero: $$ \int_0^{0.015} a(t)\, dt = \int_0^{0.010} 686.7\, dt + \int_{0.010}^{0.015} 0\, dt = 6.867 + 0 = 6.867\,m/s $$ **Step 3:** Compute the mean acceleration over 15 ms: $$ \bar{a} = \frac{6.867}{0.015} = 457.8\,m/s^2 $$ **Step 4:** Raise to power 2.5: $$ \bar{a}^{2.5} = 457.8^{2.5} $$ Compute step-by-step: $$ 457.8^2 = 209{,}581 $$ $$ 457.8^{0.5} = \sqrt{457.8} = 21.397 $$ $$ 457.8^{2.5} = 209{,}581 \times 21.397 = 4{,}484{,}000 \approx 4.484 \times 10^6 $$ **Step 5:** Multiply by $\Delta t = 0.015$ s: $$ HIC_{15} = 4.484 \times 10^6 \times 0.015 = 67{,}264 $$ **Step 6:** Comparison with threshold: $$ HIC_{15} = 67{,}264 \gg 1000 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Conclusion:** The dummy **FAILS** the FMVSS 208 criterion by a factor of **67×**. The 70 g rectangular pulse for 10 ms is **catastrophically severe** because the 15 ms window captures the entire pulse, and the resulting head injury metric is well above the threshold of 1000. In practice, this would mandate immediate redesign of the restraint system or crash pulse. > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** #### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Define crashworthiness. Explain the four engineering principles that govern crashworthy vehicle design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vehicle of mass 1500 kg impacts a rigid barrier at 50 km/h. The front crumple zone is designed to crush 0.6 m. Calculate: (i) the initial kinetic energy, (ii) the average force in the crumple zone, and (iii) the mean deceleration experienced by the passenger compartment. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (Question B) **(a) Crashworthiness Definition and Principles — 7 marks** **Definition (2 marks):** Crashworthiness is the engineered capability of a vehicle structure and its restraint systems to protect occupants during and after a collision by managing crash energy, attenuating the crash pulse, maintaining survival space, and minimizing injurious loads transmitted to the occupant's body, as quantified by ATD injury criteria. **Four Engineering Principles (5 marks):** **1. Energy Management via Crumple Zones:** The front and rear structures are designed for **controlled plastic deformation** to absorb the vehicle's kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ over a longer crush stroke $\delta$, thereby reducing the average deceleration transmitted to the passenger cell. A longer $\delta$ means lower peak $\bar{a}$ for the same energy absorbed. (1.25 marks) **2. Survival Space Maintenance (Load-Path Engineering):** The passenger compartment — comprising the A-pillars, B-pillars, C-pillars, rocker panels, roof rails, and floor pan — must remain dimensionally intact. Hot-stamped boron steel (1500 MPa tensile strength) is increasingly used for these members. Compartment integrity is measured by **HIC₁₅ (no contact with intruding structure)** and **survival space dimensions** post-crash. (1.25 marks) **3. Crash Pulse Shaping:** The deceleration pulse is shaped to be as close to a **rectangular profile** as possible. A rectangular pulse minimizes the peak deceleration for a given velocity change $\Delta v$, since: $$ a_{peak,rect} = \frac{\Delta v}{T} $$ while a triangular pulse requires a peak twice as high for the same $\Delta v$ and $T$. Designers use **force-deflection characteristics** of the front rails to achieve this rectangularity. (1.25 marks) **4. Restraint System Synergy:** The three-point seatbelt (with pretensioner and load limiter), frontal airbag (single or dual stage), side airbag, curtain airbag, and knee bolster must work in concert to **distribute** loads over wide body areas, **extend** the occupant's deceleration stroke, and **prevent** occupant excursion into the intruding structure. (1.25 marks) **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **Given:** - $m = 1500\,kg$ - $v_0 = 50$ km/h $= 50/3.6 = 13.89\,m/s$ - $\delta = 0.6\,m$ - Rigid barrier (full velocity change, $m = m_c$) **(i) Initial Kinetic Energy (2 marks):** $$ E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.89)^2 $$ $$ E_k = 750 \times 192.93 = 144{,}700\,J \approx 144.7\,kJ $$ **(ii) Average Force in Crumple Zone (2.5 marks):** Assuming constant force during crushing: $$ \bar{F} \cdot \delta = E_k $$ $$ \bar{F} = \frac{E_k}{\delta} = \frac{144{,}700}{0.6} = 241{,}167\,N \approx 241.2\,kN $$ **(iii) Mean Deceleration (2.5 marks):** $$ \bar{a} = \frac{\bar{F}}{m} = \frac{241{,}167}{1500} = 160.78\,m/s^2 $$ Convert to g: $$ \bar{a} = \frac{160.78}{9.81} = 16.4\,g $$ **Alternative derivation using work-energy:** $$ \bar{a} = \frac{v_0^2}{2\delta} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 0.6} = \frac{192.93}{1.2} = 160.78\,m/s^2 $$ Same result. ✅ > [!WARNING] > **Conclusion:** A mean deceleration of **16.4 g** is within the survivable range for a **belted** Hybrid III 50M occupant, but is at the upper edge. If the pulse has a **triangular shape** (peak ~33 g), the dummy will likely fail the chest deflection and femur load criteria. Designers must ensure the force-deflection curve of the front rails is **rectangularized** to keep peak below ~25 g. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s (multiply by 1/3.6) results in wrong answers. Always check units first. > 2. **HIC exponent confusion:** Students often write HIC = [mean]^2.5 × Δt but forget that the bracketed term is the mean over the window, not the peak. Use $\bar{a}$ not $a_{peak}$. > 3. **Crash pulse area vs. peak:** The area under the crash pulse equals $\Delta v$ (velocity change), NOT the energy. Energy equals the integral of force × displacement. > 4. **ATD selection confusion:** ECE R94 = frontal Hybrid III; ECE R95 = side WorldSID/ES-2; AIS-098 (India) = frontal Hybrid III. Memorize the dummy-to-regulation mapping. > 5. **Nij direction confusion:** Nij uses **upper** ($F_z, M_y$) for tension/flexion and **lower** ($F_z, M_y$) for compression/extension. Both must satisfy Nij ≤ 1.0. > 6. **Crumple zone mass vs. compartment mass:** When computing $\bar{a}$, use the total vehicle mass (kinetic energy distributed across all mass), not just the front structure. > 7. **Rectangular vs. triangular pulse area:** Same area ≠ same peak. Triangular pulse has $a_{peak} = 2 \times \bar{a}$. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 OEAUT831 — Module 2 | High-Density Revision Notes** **Crash Test Dummies (ATDs):** - ATD = Anthropomorphic Test Device — mechanical surrogate of human - Hybrid III 50M = 50th percentile male, frontal, 175 cm / 78 kg - Hybrid III 5F = 5th percentile female, frontal, 151 cm / 49 kg - THOR = next-generation frontal with chestbands - WorldSID = modern 50M side impact - SID-IIs = 5th percentile female side impact - ES-2 = European side impact (predecessor to WorldSID) - BioRID II = rear-impact whiplash dummy - Q-series = child dummies (Q0 to Q10) - ATDs do NOT "feel" injury — they record mechanical quantities - Filtering: CFC 60 (head), CFC 180 (chest), CFC 1000 (foot) - Sample rate: ≥ 10 kHz **Injury Criteria — Memorize the Thresholds:** - HIC₁₅ ≤ **1000** (FMVSS 208, ECE R94, AIS-098) - Chest deflection ≤ **63 mm** (Hybrid III 50M) - Femur compression ≤ **10 kN** (per leg) - Nij ≤ **1.0** (both upper and lower neck) - Tibia index ≤ **1.0** - NIC peak ≤ **15 m/s²** (whiplash, BioRID) **Key Formulas:** - $HIC_{15} = \left[ \frac{1}{0.015} \int_{t_1}^{t_1+0.015} a(t) dt \right]^{2.5} (0.015)$ - $\Delta v = \int_0^T a(t) dt$ - $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ - $\bar{F} = \frac{m v_0^2}{2\delta}$ - $\bar{a} = \frac{v_0^2}{2\delta}$ - $NIC = 0.2 a_{rel} + v_{rel}$ - $N_{ij} = \frac{F_z}{F_{zc}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{yc}}$ **Crashworthiness Principles:** - Energy management via crumple zones - Survival space maintenance - Rectangular crash pulse shaping - Restraint system synergy - Wayne State Tolerance Curve — 400 g @ 4 ms, 60 g @ 100 ms - Frontal test: 56 km/h rigid barrier - Side MDB: 50 km/h (Euro NCAP), 61 km/h (IIHS) - Hot-stamped boron steel = 1500 MPa tensile - A-pillar, B-pillar, rocker, roof rail = key survival space members - Rectangular pulse is optimal: $a_{peak} = \frac{\Delta v}{T}$ - Triangular pulse peak: $a_{peak} = \frac{2\Delta v}{T}$ (twice as high!) **Key Distinctions:** - HIC vs. GSI: HIC uses 2.5 exponent + sliding window; GSI uses 2.0 + integral - Frontal vs. Side ATD: Hybrid III vs. WorldSID/SID-IIs - Crash pulse vs. Crash energy: pulse = $\int a \, dt = \Delta v$; energy = $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ - Occupant mass vs. Vehicle mass: $\bar{a}$ uses vehicle mass; occupant force = $m_{occ} \times a$ - Biofidelity vs. Repeatability: biofidelity = human likeness; repeatability = sensor consistency **Regulation to ATD Mapping:** - FMVSS 208 → Hybrid III 50M (frontal) - FMVSS 214 → ES-2 / SID-IIs (side) - ECE R94 → Hybrid III 50M (frontal) - ECE R95 → WorldSID / ES-2 (side) - AIS-098 → Hybrid III 50M (India, frontal) - Euro NCAP → Hybrid III 50M + Q6 + Q10 - IIHS → Hybrid III 50M + THOR (modern) **Exam Tips:** - Always state units in your final answer (m/s², kN, mm, g) - Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by 3.6 - Always show step-by-step substitution in numerical problems - Memorize 2.5 exponent and 15 ms window for HIC - Memorize 1000 HIC threshold and 10 kN femur load - Draw Mermaid block diagrams for ATD subsystems if 7+ marks allocated - For derivation questions, start from Newton's second law and energy conservation ---

topics.title: Pole Crash Testing, Near Crash Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pole Crash Testing, Near Crash Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pole Crash Testing — Formal Definition

**Pole Crash Testing** is a highly specialised full-scale vehicle crashworthiness evaluation procedure in which a moving passenger car is propelled laterally into a **rigid, vertical, fixed-diameter cylindrical pole**, mounted perpendicularly to the direction of vehicle travel. The test is designed to evaluate the **lateral (side) crashworthiness** of the occupant compartment, with particular emphasis on the structural integrity of the B-pillar, roof rail, door cavity, and the effectiveness of side-impact restraint systems (curtain airbags, side torso airbags, and energy-absorbing foams).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardised):**
> *Pole Crash Test is a side-impact vehicle safety test where the test vehicle, travelling at a prescribed velocity, strikes a rigid vertical pole aligned perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis, simulating a tree or utility-pole type collision. The test assesses intrusion into the occupant survival space, dummy injury criteria, and side-airbag deployment efficacy.*

**Governing Standard / Protocol Reference:**
- **FMVSS 214** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard — Side Impact Protection)
- **Euro NCAP Side Pole Impact Test** (introduced in 2009)
- **AIS-096** (Automotive Industry Standards, India)
- **ECE R95** (Economic Commission for Europe Side Collision)

> [!NOTE]
> **Test Parameters (BOLD High-Yield Values for KTU Exam):**
> - **Impact Velocity: 29 km/h (≈ 8.06 m/s)**
> - **Pole Diameter: 254 mm (10 inches)**
> - **Pole Material: Rigid Steel (deformation negligible)**
> - **Pole Height: ≥ 3 m above ground**
> - **Impact Angle: 90° to vehicle's longitudinal centreline**
> - **Test Surface: Rigid, level, with a coefficient of friction < 0.5**

---

## 1.2 Near Crash Testing — Formal Definition

**Near Crash Testing** (also referred to as *Near-Crash Scenario Testing* or *Pre-Crash Testing*) is a contemporary vehicle safety evaluation methodology focused on the **pre-collision phase** of a vehicle's motion. It characterises the dynamic behaviour of the vehicle and its safety systems in the critical time interval **immediately preceding a potential collision** (typically **2 to 5 seconds before impact**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardised):**
> *Near Crash Testing is the evaluation of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and vehicle dynamic response in pre-collision scenarios through controlled real-world track tests, instrumented proving-ground experiments, or high-fidelity driving simulator trials. It quantifies evasive manoeuvres, braking response, and electronic stability intervention before impact occurs.*

**Primary Applications:**
- Validation of **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)**
- Validation of **ESC (Electronic Stability Control)**
- Validation of **LDW (Lane Departure Warning)**
- Validation of **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)**
- Driver behaviour analysis and **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Introduced — Time-To-Collision (TTC):**
> The most fundamental near-crash metric is **TTC**, defined as the time remaining before a projected collision if both the subject vehicle and the obstacle (or lead vehicle) maintain their instantaneous velocity and trajectory:
> $$\text{TTC} = \frac{D_{\text{relative}}}{\Delta v}$$
> where **D_relative** is the relative distance and **Δv** is the relative velocity between the two bodies.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for Pole Crash Testing — "The Boxing Punch":**
> Imagine a boxer (the car) standing still, and a heavyweight trainer swings a single, immovable vertical pole into the boxer's ribcage (the car's side). The boxer has a gum shield, a body protector, and well-conditioned ribs (the side airbags, foam, and B-pillar). The Pole Crash Test measures how much of the punch's energy reaches the boxer's internal organs (the dummy's HIC, chest deflection, femur force). The pole doesn't deform — only the boxer does. This is why the pole is "rigid": it isolates the *vehicle's* energy-absorption capability as the variable.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for Near Crash Testing — "The Stalking Cat & The Mouse":**
> Picture a cat stalking a mouse. Two seconds before the pounce, the mouse is still nibbling seeds. The cat accelerates. The mouse flicks its tail and darts sideways. Near-Crash Testing measures exactly this — the cat's AEB, the mouse's evasive lane-change, the lateral acceleration, the TTC, and which safety system "fires" first. The collision never happens, but the *behaviour* is what gets measured. The absence of impact is the point of the test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pole Crash Testing — Theoretical Framework

The pole crash test, although physically a side-impact event, is governed by **energy conservation, momentum transfer, and occupant biomechanics**. The theoretical underpinning is built on four pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Initial Kinetic Energy Budget
The vehicle's pre-impact kinetic energy is the total energy that must be dissipated (partially by the structure, partially by the restraints, partially transferred to the dummy):

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

For the KTU standard test velocity of **v = 29 km/h = 8.06 m/s** and a typical test mass of **m = 1500 kg**:

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (8.06)^2 \approx 48{,}723 \text{ J} \approx 48.7 \text{ kJ}$$

### Pillar 2 — Crush Mechanics & Energy Dissipation
Because the pole is rigid, the **entire deformation zone lies within the vehicle's side structure** (door, B-pillar, rocker, roof rail). The average **crush force** can be approximated as:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{d_{\text{crush}}}$$

where **d_crush** is the maximum intrusion depth into the occupant compartment (typically measured at the H-point or door waistline).

### Pillar 3 — Injury Biomechanics Indices
Three standardised injury criteria are evaluated post-test:

| # | Injury Index | Full Form | Region Assessed | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HIC₁₅** | Head Injury Criterion | Head | **≤ 700** |
| 2 | **TTI** | Thoracic Trauma Index | Chest/Thorax | **≤ 85 g (g's)** |
| 3 | **Femur Load** | Axial Femur Compression | Lower Extremity | **≤ 10 kN** |

### Pillar 4 — Vehicle Structural Performance Metrics
- **Maximum Intrusion** (mm) at the door, B-pillar, and sill
- **Door Opening Force** post-impact (N)
- **Roof Crush Resistance** (load vs. displacement curve)
- **Fuel System Integrity** — no leakage ≥ specified threshold

---

## 2.2 Near Crash Testing — Theoretical Framework

Near Crash Testing is a **probabilistic, scenario-based, and behaviour-driven** evaluation. It does not rely on rigid-body impact mechanics but on **driver/vehicle response dynamics** and **ADAS intervention logic**.

### Pillar 1 — Scenario Taxonomy
Near-crash scenarios are classified as:

| Scenario Code | Description | Critical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Lead vehicle sudden brake | TTC at AEB activation |
| **S2** | Pedestrian crossing | Lateral clearance at intervention |
| **S3** | Evasive lane change (swerve) | Lateral acceleration peak |
| **S4** | Curve understeer/oversteer | ESC intervention time |
| **S5** | Run-off-road (departure) | LDW alert time |

### Pillar 2 — Sensor & Data Acquisition Stack
- **RTK-GPS** (Real-Time Kinematic Global Positioning System) — positional accuracy **< 2 cm**
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — 6-DOF, 100–1000 Hz sampling
- **Vehicle CAN-bus telemetry** — speed, brake pressure, steering angle, throttle, yaw rate
- **Eye-tracker & Driver-facing camera** — gaze entropy, glance duration
- **Lidar/Radar target simulators** — for repeatable obstacle insertion

### Pillar 3 — Performance Evaluation Indices
- **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** — already defined above
- **Minimum Time-to-Collision (TTC_min)** — lowest value reached during the manoeuvre
- **Deceleration-to-Stop Time (DST)** — time from AEB trigger to full stop
- **Post-Encroachment Time (PET)** — gap between subject and obstacle clearing
- **Acceleration Severity Index (ASI):**

$$\text{ASI} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\bar{a}_x}{a_x^*}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\bar{a}_y}{a_y^*}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\bar{a}_z}{a_z^*}\right)^2}$$

where $a^*$ values are limit accelerations (typically $a_x^* = 12$ g, $a_y^* = 9$ g, $a_z^* = 10$ g).

### Pillar 4 — Test Environment
- **Proving Ground (Closed Track)**: Real vehicle + real obstacle (soft target, e.g., balloon car / pedestrian dummy)
- **Driving Simulator**: High-fidelity mock-up, repeatable, low risk
- **Instrumented Public Road**: Naturalistic driving study, statistically richest

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 student is expected to know for a question on Pole Crash / Near Crash Testing. **Memorise this table — it is the exam's most testable block.**

| # | Formula / Symbol | Physical Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Vehicle kinetic energy at impact | J (Joules) | Pole Crash |
| 2 | $F_{\text{avg}} = E_k / d_{\text{crush}}$ | Average crush force | N (Newton) | Pole Crash |
| 3 | $\text{HIC} = \left[(t_2 - t_1)\left(\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]^{0.5}$ | Head Injury Criterion | dimensionless | Pole Crash |
| 4 | $\text{HIC}_{15} \leq 700$ | 15-ms HIC threshold | dimensionless | Pole Crash |
| 5 | $\text{TTI} = \text{argmax}\left[\frac{a_{\text{shoulder}} + a_{\text{spine}}}{2}\right]$ | Thoracic Trauma Index | g | Pole Crash |
| 6 | $F_{\text{femur}} \leq 10\,\text{kN}$ | Axial femur load limit | kN | Pole Crash |
| 7 | $\text{TTC} = D_{\text{rel}} / \Delta v$ | Time-to-collision | s (seconds) | Near Crash |
| 8 | $\text{PET} = t_{\text{exit,B}} - t_{\text{entry,A}}$ | Post-encroachment time | s | Near Crash |
| 9 | $\text{ASI} = \sqrt{(\bar{a}_x/a_x^*)^2 + (\bar{a}_y/a_y^*)^2 + (\bar{a}_z/a_z^*)^2}$ | Acceleration Severity Index | dimensionless | Near Crash |
| 10 | $\Delta v = v_{\text{test}} - v_{\text{escape}}$ | Residual relative velocity | m/s | Both |
| 11 | $a = v^2 / (2D)$ | Required avg. deceleration to stop | m/s² | Near Crash |
| 12 | $t_{\text{stop}} = v / a$ | Time-to-stop under constant decel | s | Near Crash |
| 13 | $E_{\text{restraint}} = \tfrac{1}{2}m(\Delta v)^2$ | Energy managed by restraints | J | Pole Crash |
| 14 | $\text{SI} = \int_0^{T} [a(t)]^{2.5}\,dt$ | Severity Index (time-integral) | (m/s²)^2.5 · s | Near Crash |
| 15 | $\eta_{\text{structure}} = E_{\text{absorbed}} / E_{\text{kinetic}}$ | Energy-absorption efficiency | dimensionless | Pole Crash |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the industry care about Pole Crash Testing?**
> Approximately **25% of all fatal car crashes** involve a side impact. Side crashes have a fatality rate nearly **3× higher** than frontal crashes because the side structure offers only **~15–20 cm of crush space** between the outer skin and the occupant's torso, compared to **~60–80 cm** in the front. Pole crashes specifically replicate **run-off-road tree impacts**, which are disproportionately fatal due to the high local intrusion and lack of "ride-down" distance.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the industry care about Near Crash Testing?**
> With the proliferation of **ADAS, SAE Level-2 and Level-3 automation**, the legal and safety liability for pre-crash behaviour has shifted from the human driver to the **system supplier**. Near Crash Testing is now a regulatory requirement under **Euro NCAP's 2025 Roadmap**, **GSR 2 (EU General Safety Regulation 2019/2144)**, and **AIS-184 (India)**. Without near-crash validation, a vehicle cannot receive a 5-star safety rating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pole Crash Kinetic Energy & Crush Force

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A passenger car of mass **m = 1450 kg** impacts a rigid cylindrical pole at **v = 29 km/h** in a standardised pole crash test. The maximum intrusion into the occupant compartment is measured as **d_crush = 220 mm**. Calculate:
> (a) The initial kinetic energy of the vehicle.
> (b) The average crush force exerted on the side structure.
> (c) The energy-absorption efficiency if the pole is treated as 100% rigid.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Convert the impact velocity to SI units (m/s).**
The test speed is 29 km/h. We apply the conversion factor 1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s.

$$v = 29 \times \frac{1}{3.6} = 8.0556 \text{ m/s} \approx 8.06 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the kinetic energy formula.**
Kinetic energy is given by $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. Substitute m = 1450 kg and v = 8.0556 m/s.

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 1450 \times (8.0556)^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the squared velocity term.**

$$(8.0556)^2 = 64.8930 \text{ (m/s)}^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute and evaluate.**

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 1450 \times 64.8930 = 725 \times 64.8930$$

$$E_k = 47{,}047.4 \text{ J} \approx 47.05 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5: Convert the crush depth to metres.**

$$d_{\text{crush}} = 220 \text{ mm} = 0.220 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Apply the average crush force formula.**
Average crush force equals the total energy absorbed divided by the crush depth (work–energy theorem).

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_k}{d_{\text{crush}}} = \frac{47{,}047.4}{0.220}$$

$$F_{\text{avg}} = 213{,}852.7 \text{ N} \approx 213.85 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Determine the energy-absorption efficiency.**
Because the pole is rigid, the structural deformation of the vehicle is the only energy-absorbing mechanism (neglecting friction, sound, and heat losses to the dummy). The vehicle structure absorbs approximately all the kinetic energy.

$$\eta_{\text{structure}} = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{E_{\text{kinetic}}} = \frac{47{,}047.4}{47{,}047.4} = 1.00 \text{ (or } 100\%\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Results Block (for the 14-mark answer):**
> - (a) **E_k = 47.05 kJ**
> - (b) **F_avg = 213.85 kN**
> - (c) **η = 1.00 (100%)**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Head Injury Criterion (HIC₁₅) Computation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> The head acceleration-time history of a side-impact dummy during a pole crash test yields the following sampled data. The 15 ms HIC window is selected from t₁ = 8 ms to t₂ = 23 ms, during which the average head acceleration $\bar{a}$ is measured as **85 g**. Compute the HIC₁₅ value and comment on whether the dummy passes the regulatory threshold.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Recall the HIC formula (Wenzel, Levine, and Pintar 1989).**

$$\text{HIC} = \left[(t_2 - t_1)\left(\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right)^{2.5}\right]^{0.5}$$

**Step 2: Identify the parameters.**
- t₁ = 8 ms = 0.008 s
- t₂ = 23 ms = 0.023 s
- Time interval: $t_2 - t_1 = 15$ ms = 0.015 s
- Average acceleration: $\bar{a} = 85$ g (the integral term reduces to the average for a constant average case)

**Step 3: Convert 85 g to SI units (m/s²).**
1 g = 9.81 m/s², so:

$$\bar{a} = 85 \times 9.81 = 833.85 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the integral term.**
For a constant average acceleration, the integral simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt = \bar{a} = 833.85 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5: Raise the average acceleration to the power 2.5.**

$$(833.85)^{2.5} = (833.85)^{2} \times (833.85)^{0.5}$$

$$(833.85)^2 = 695{,}305.5$$

$$(833.85)^{0.5} = 28.876$$

$$(833.85)^{2.5} = 695{,}305.5 \times 28.876 \approx 20{,}078{,}929 \text{ (m/s}^2)^{2.5}$$

**Step 6: Multiply by the time interval.**

$$(t_2 - t_1) \times (\bar{a})^{2.5} = 0.015 \times 20{,}078{,}929 = 301{,}183.9$$

**Step 7: Take the square root to obtain HIC₁₅.**

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = \sqrt{301{,}183.9} \approx 548.8$$

**Step 8: Compare against the regulatory limit of 700.**

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = 548.8 < 700 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS (within FMVSS 214 limit)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result Block:**
> - **HIC₁₅ = 548.8** → **PASS** (Limit = 700)
> - Head-injury risk is below the threshold; side airbag and door foam effectively managed the energy.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Near-Crash TTC Computation & AEB Logic

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A test vehicle travelling at **v_s = 60 km/h** approaches a stationary lead vehicle at a closing distance of **D = 25 m**. The AEB system is calibrated to trigger when TTC falls below **2.5 s**. Calculate:
> (a) The initial TTC at the start of the scenario.
> (b) The distance at which AEB activates.
> (c) The required average deceleration if full stop must be achieved at the AEB activation point.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Convert the test vehicle speed to SI units.**

$$v_s = 60 \text{ km/h} = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Identify relative velocity and distance.**
- Relative velocity: $\Delta v = v_s - v_{\text{lead}} = 16.667 - 0 = 16.667$ m/s
- Initial relative distance: $D = 25$ m

**Step 3: Apply the TTC formula (Part a).**

$$\text{TTC}_0 = \frac{D}{\Delta v} = \frac{25}{16.667} = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compare against the AEB trigger threshold of 2.5 s.**
Since TTC₀ = 1.5 s < 2.5 s, AEB has already triggered at the start. Therefore, AEB activation distance is the initial scenario distance.

$$D_{\text{AEB}} = 25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute the required average deceleration (Part c).**
The vehicle must come to a stop (v_f = 0) over the distance D_AEB = 25 m starting from v_s = 16.667 m/s. Use the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_s^2 - 2 a D$:

$$0 = (16.667)^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot 25$$

$$a = \frac{(16.667)^2}{2 \times 25} = \frac{277.79}{50} = 5.556 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6: Convert to g-units for the standard 0.8 g comfort/safety limit.**

$$a = \frac{5.556}{9.81} = 0.566 \text{ g}$$

**Step 7: Compare to the 0.8 g regulatory deceleration limit.**

$$0.566 \text{ g} < 0.8 \text{ g} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{PASS (within AEB comfort limit)}$$

**Step 8: Calculate the time to stop.**

$$t_{\text{stop}} = \frac{v_s}{a} = \frac{16.667}{5.556} = 3.0 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result Block (for the 14-mark answer):**
> - (a) **TTC₀ = 1.5 s** (AEB pre-triggered, as 1.5 s < 2.5 s)
> - (b) **D_AEB = 25 m** (entire scenario distance)
> - (c) **a_required = 5.56 m/s² = 0.566 g (PASS vs. 0.8 g limit)**
> - **t_stop = 3.0 s**

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Pole vs. Near Crash Testing

| Analytical Dimension | Pole Crash Testing | Near Crash Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase Tested** | Impact (post-collision) | Pre-collision (2–5 s before) |
| **Energy Domain** | High (kJ range) | Negligible (no contact) |
| **Core Metric** | HIC, TTI, intrusion | TTC, PET, ASI, decel |
| **Physics Law** | Conservation of Momentum + Energy | Kinematics + Control Theory |
| **Test Apparatus** | Rigid steel pole, sled, dummies | Soft targets, ADAS ECU, sensors |
| **Regulatory Driver** | FMVSS 214, AIS-096 | GSR 2, Euro NCAP 2025, AIS-184 |
| **Failure Mode Studied** | Occupant compartment intrusion | Sensor latency, false activation |
| **Cost Per Test** | Very High (₹15–50 lakh) | Moderate (₹2–5 lakh) |
| **Repeatability** | Low (destructive) | High (synthetic targets) |
| **Time Output** | Milliseconds | Seconds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pole Crash Test — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional flow** of a pole crash test from initiation to data reduction, including the three principal subsystems: *Propulsion & Guidance*, *Pole & Fixture*, and *Occupant & Restraint Module*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["PROPULSION MODULE: Tow Cable / Winch System"] --> A2["GUIDANCE TRACK: Rail aligned to vehicle centerline"]
    A2 --> A3["RELEASE MECHANISM: Pneumatic clamp disengages at impact point"]
    A3 --> A4["TEST VEHICLE: Subject car, instrumented, dummy-laden"]
    A4 --> A5["RIGID POLE: 254 mm diameter steel cylinder, vertically fixed"]
    A5 --> A6["IMPACT EVENT: 29 km/h perpendicular collision"]
    A6 --> A7["DATA ACQUISITION: 1000 Hz sampling, on-board recorders"]
    A7 --> A8["POST-TEST ANALYSIS: HIC, TTI, Femur Force, Intrusion Map"]
    A8 --> A9["REPORT: Compliance with FMVSS 214 / Euro NCAP"]
```

## 4.2 Near Crash Test — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["SCENARIO DEFINITION: S1-S5 catalogue"] --> B2["TARGET INJECTION: Soft car / pedestrian / cyclist model"]
    B2 --> B3["TEST VEHICLE: Subject car, calibrated sensors and ADAS ECU"]
    B3 --> B4["SENSOR FUSION: Camera + Radar + Lidar + CAN bus"]
    B4 --> B5["ADAS DECISION LOGIC: AEB / ESC / LDW trigger"]
    B5 --> B6["RESPONSE: Brake actuator, steering override, warning"]
    B6 --> B7["MEASUREMENT: TTC, PET, ASI, peak deceleration"]
    B7 --> B8["DATA LOG: RTK-GPS, IMU, Driver Eye-Tracker"]
    B8 --> B9["EVALUATION: Compliance with GSR 2 / AIS-184"]
```

## 4.3 Pole Crash — Test Rig & Instrumentation Layout (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1["CABLE / WINCH: Provides 29 km/h kinetic energy"] --> C2["GUIDE RAIL: Aligns vehicle path"]
    C2 --> C3["RELEASE TRIGGER: Photoelectric sensor at last 0.5 m"]
    C3 --> C4["VEHICLE BODY IN WHITE: Painted for impact photography"]
    C4 --> C5["SIDE IMPACT DUMMIES: ES-2re or WorldSID on driver and rear passenger"]
    C5 --> C6["ON-BOARD DATA RECORDERS: 64 channels, 10 kHz"]
    C6 --> C7["HIGH-SPEED CAMERAS: 1000 fps, 3 angles"]
    C7 --> C8["RIGID POLE BASE: Concrete-anchored, deformation less than 1 mm"]
```

## 4.4 Near-Crash Scenario Trigger Logic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1["SENSOR INPUT: Camera, Radar, Lidar, GPS"] --> D2["FUSION UNIT: Track-level object list"]
    D2 --> D3{"TTC COMPUTATION: D / delta-v"}
    D3 -->|TTC less than 2.5 s| D4["AEB ALERT: Audible + Visual + Haptic"]
    D3 -->|TTC less than 1.4 s| D5["AEB FULL BRAKE: Hydraulic pressure 8 to 12 MPa"]
    D5 --> D6["VEHICLE STOPS: t greater than 3.0 s typical"]
    D3 -->|TTC safe greater than 4.0 s| D7["NO ACTION: Maintain cruise"]
    D4 --> D8["DATA LOGGED: PET, deceleration, jerk profile"]
    D6 --> D8
    D7 --> D8
    D8 --> D9["SCORE: Euro NCAP AEB Inter-Urban score out of 3"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]:**
> **Q: Define Pole Crash Testing. State the standard test velocity, pole diameter, and the two principal injury criteria used to evaluate dummy head and chest protection.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Pole Crash Testing** is a side-impact crashworthiness test in which a moving vehicle strikes a rigid vertical cylindrical pole perpendicularly to its longitudinal axis. The standard test velocity is **29 km/h**, and the pole diameter is **254 mm**. The two principal injury criteria are: **(i) Head Injury Criterion (HIC₁₅)**, with a regulatory limit of 700, evaluating head acceleration over a 15 ms window; and **(ii) Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)**, which quantifies the peak average chest-spine acceleration of the dummy. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark parameters + 1 mark injury indices.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]:**
> **Q: What is Near-Crash Testing? Differentiate it from full-scale crash testing with two key points.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Near-Crash Testing** is the evaluation of a vehicle's pre-collision dynamic response and ADAS intervention in the **2–5 seconds preceding a potential impact**, using controlled track tests, soft targets, or driving simulators. It differs from full-scale crash testing in two key aspects: **(i) Phase of Evaluation** — near-crash testing occurs *before* impact (kinematic domain), whereas full-scale testing occurs *during and after* impact (energy-dissipation domain); and **(ii) Destructiveness** — near-crash testing is **non-destructive** (uses soft, deformable targets) and can be repeated, whereas pole or frontal crash tests are **single-shot destructive events**. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark phase difference + 1 mark destructiveness difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Pole Crash Testing Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model 14-Mark Q]:**
> **(a)** Explain the Pole Crash Testing procedure in detail. Discuss the role of FMVSS 214, the standard test velocity, pole geometry, and the instrumentation used.
> **(b)** A test vehicle of mass **1600 kg** impacts a rigid pole at **29 km/h**. The side structure absorbs the entire kinetic energy with an average crush depth of **180 mm**. Compute (i) initial kinetic energy, (ii) average crush force, and (iii) HIC₁₅ if the average head acceleration over the 15 ms critical window is **90 g**. Comment on whether the head passes the regulatory threshold.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

The pole crash testing procedure is conducted as follows:

1. **Test Setup**: The subject vehicle is loaded with side-impact dummies (ES-2re on the driver seat; WorldSID on the rear seat), ballasted to its gross vehicle weight, and instrumented with **on-board data recorders (64+ channels, 10 kHz sampling)** and high-speed cameras (1000 fps).
2. **Pole Geometry**: A rigid, vertically mounted steel cylinder of **254 mm diameter** and at least 3 m height is fixed to a concrete base with negligible deformation (< 1 mm). The pole is perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal centreline.
3. **Test Velocity**: The vehicle is propelled to **29 km/h (8.06 m/s)** via a tow cable and pneumatic release system.
4. **Governing Standard**: **FMVSS 214** (and Euro NCAP Side Pole protocol) mandates test conditions, instrumentation, and pass/fail criteria. The test evaluates B-pillar integrity, side-airbag deployment timing, and occupant-compartment survival space.
5. **Data Reduction**: The on-board recorders capture acceleration-time histories; high-speed cameras document intrusion; the dummy's HIC₁₅, TTI, and femur force are computed post-test. A compliance report is generated.

*(7 marks: 1 mark setup + 1 mark pole geometry + 1 mark test velocity + 1 mark FMVSS 214 + 1 mark instrumentation + 1 mark data reduction + 1 mark compliance reporting.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- m = 1600 kg
- v = 29 km/h = 8.0556 m/s
- d_crush = 180 mm = 0.180 m
- $\bar{a}$ = 90 g = 90 × 9.81 = 882.9 m/s²

**(i) Initial Kinetic Energy:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1600 \times (8.0556)^2$$

$$E_k = 800 \times 64.893 = 51{,}914.4 \text{ J} \approx 51.91 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Average Crush Force:**

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_k}{d_{\text{crush}}} = \frac{51{,}914.4}{0.180} = 288{,}413 \text{ N} \approx 288.4 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) HIC₁₅ Calculation:**

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = \left[0.015 \times (882.9)^{2.5}\right]^{0.5}$$

$$(882.9)^{2.5} = (882.9)^2 \times (882.9)^{0.5} = 779{,}512.4 \times 29.714 = 23{,}162{,}500$$

$$0.015 \times 23{,}162{,}500 = 347{,}437.5$$

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = \sqrt{347{,}437.5} \approx 589.4$$

**Comment:** HIC₁₅ = 589.4 < 700 → **PASS** the regulatory head-injury threshold.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for E_k, 2 marks for F_avg, 2 marks for HIC₁₅, 1 mark for the compliance comment.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Near Crash Testing Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model 14-Mark Q]:**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of Near-Crash Testing. List the five standard scenarios (S1–S5) used in Euro NCAP and discuss the role of TTC, PET, and ASI in evaluating ADAS performance.
> **(b)** A test vehicle approaches a stationary obstacle at **80 km/h** with a relative distance of **40 m**. The AEB trigger threshold is **TTC = 2.0 s**, and the system must bring the vehicle to a halt within **40 m** using a constant deceleration. Calculate (i) initial TTC, (ii) the distance at which AEB activates, and (iii) the required average deceleration. Comment on passenger comfort (limit = 0.8 g).

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Near-Crash Testing** evaluates the pre-collision performance of a vehicle's ADAS suite in the **2–5 seconds before impact** using controlled real-world or simulator-based experiments. The five Euro NCAP scenarios are:

- **S1 — Lead Vehicle Sudden Brake**: Tests AEB inter-urban function.
- **S2 — Pedestrian Crossing**: Tests AEB pedestrian, with lateral clearance.
- **S3 — Evasive Lane Change (Swerve)**: Tests ESC and lane-support intervention.
- **S4 — Curve Understeer/Oversteer**: Tests ESC yaw control.
- **S5 — Run-Off-Road Departure**: Tests LDW and Emergency Lane Keeping.

The three key performance metrics are:

- **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**: The time before a projected collision if velocities remain constant. It triggers AEB. Threshold typically 2.5 s alert, 1.4 s full brake.
- **Post-Encroachment Time (PET)**: The time gap between two road users crossing a common spatial point. Lower PET = higher risk.
- **Acceleration Severity Index (ASI)**: A composite metric of 3-axis accelerations normalised by limit values; quantifies occupant exposure to sudden velocity changes.

*(7 marks: 1 mark concept + 2 marks S1–S5 list + 2 marks TTC/PET/ASI explanation + 2 marks threshold/role discussion.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- v_s = 80 km/h = 22.222 m/s
- D_initial = 40 m
- TTC_trigger = 2.0 s
- Stop distance = 40 m (i.e., complete stop within initial distance)

**(i) Initial TTC:**

$$\text{TTC}_0 = \frac{D_{\text{initial}}}{\Delta v} = \frac{40}{22.222} = 1.80 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) AEB Activation Distance:**
Since TTC₀ = 1.80 s < 2.0 s, AEB activates at the start of the scenario.

$$D_{\text{AEB}} = 40 \text{ m}$$

**Verification by reverse calculation:** The distance at which TTC = 2.0 s would be:

$$D_{\text{trigger}} = \text{TTC} \times \Delta v = 2.0 \times 22.222 = 44.44 \text{ m}$$

But our initial distance is only 40 m < 44.44 m → AEB is active throughout.

**(iii) Required Average Deceleration:**

$$a = \frac{v_s^2}{2 D} = \frac{(22.222)^2}{2 \times 40} = \frac{493.83}{80} = 6.173 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a = \frac{6.173}{9.81} = 0.629 \text{ g}$$

**Comment:** Required deceleration 0.629 g < 0.8 g limit → **PASS** for passenger comfort.

**Time-to-Stop:**

$$t_{\text{stop}} = \frac{v_s}{a} = \frac{22.222}{6.173} = 3.60 \text{ s}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks for TTC, 2 marks for activation distance, 2 marks for deceleration, 1.5 marks for comfort comment.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Notebook):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error** — Students forget to convert km/h to m/s by dividing by 3.6. This single error cascades through 4–5 sub-parts and loses up to **4 marks** per question.
> 2. **HIC Formula Mis-application** — Many students confuse the HIC exponent (2.5 inside, 0.5 outside) and compute it in g-units without converting to m/s². HIC is **dimensionless** but requires the input to be in consistent SI (m/s²) units.
> 3. **Pole Diameter Confusion** — A common trap answer is "200 mm" or "300 mm". The correct KTU/FMVSS value is **254 mm (10 inches)**. Misremembering loses the full mark.
> 4. **TTC Sign Convention** — TTC must always be **positive**. If the subject vehicle is moving away (Δv < 0), TTC is not defined. Students often compute a negative TTC, which is conceptually wrong.
> 5. **Confusing Near-Crash with Crash** — Examiners frequently ask "Is Near-Crash Testing destructive?" The answer is **NO** — it uses *soft targets* (balloon cars, pedestrian dummies on linear sleds) and is repeatable. Writing "Yes, it is destructive" loses 1 full mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the 0.8 g Comfort Threshold** — AEB deceleration above 0.8 g causes passenger discomfort. Always compare the computed deceleration against this limit for a complete answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pole Crash Testing — Key Recall Points:**
> - Standard test velocity: **29 km/h = 8.06 m/s**
> - Pole diameter: **254 mm (10 inches)**, rigid steel
> - Injury limits: **HIC₁₅ ≤ 700**, **TTI ≤ 85 g**, **Femur Force ≤ 10 kN**
> - Mass of test vehicle: typically **1300–1600 kg** (GVW)
> - Governing standards: **FMVSS 214**, **Euro NCAP Side Pole**, **ECE R95**, **AIS-096**
> - Test is **destructive**, **single-shot**, **high cost**
> - Lateral crush space: only **~15–20 cm** (vs. 60–80 cm in front)
> - Side crashes are **~3× more fatal** than frontal crashes
> - Energy domain: tens of **kJ**; typical F_avg ≈ 200–300 kN

> [!TIP]
> **Near Crash Testing — Key Recall Points:**
> - Phase tested: **2–5 seconds before impact** (pre-collision)
> - Five Euro NCAP scenarios: **S1–S5** (lead brake, pedestrian, swerve, curve, run-off-road)
> - Core metric: **TTC = D_relative / Δv**; threshold typically **2.5 s** for alert
> - Other metrics: **PET** (post-encroachment time), **ASI** (acceleration severity index)
> - AEB trigger logic: **TTC < 2.5 s** → alert; **TTC < 1.4 s** → full brake
> - Comfort deceleration limit: **0.8 g (≈ 7.85 m/s²)**
> - Sensors: **Camera + Radar + Lidar + RTK-GPS + IMU**
> - Governing standards: **Euro NCAP 2025**, **GSR 2 (EU 2019/2144)**, **AIS-184 (India)**
> - Test is **non-destructive** (soft targets), **repeatable**, **moderate cost**
> - Primary validation tool for **ADAS and SAE Level-2/3** autonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Question Self-Test (answer in 60 seconds each):**
> 1. *What is the regulatory limit for HIC₁₅?* → **700**
> 2. *What is the standard pole diameter in a Pole Crash Test?* → **254 mm**
> 3. *What does TTC stand for and what is its formula?* → **Time-To-Collision; TTC = D_relative / Δv**

If you can answer all three correctly in under 60 seconds each, you are fully prepared for the KTU 2024 examination on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact, Side Impact Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Impact — Formal Definition

**Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact** is a class of automotive crash event in which two independently moving (or stationary) road vehicles collide, exchanging momentum and dissipating kinetic energy through controlled structural deformation of the **crashworthy zones** (crumple zones, load-bearing members, and passenger cell). It is distinct from vehicle-to-fixed-object (VFO) or vehicle-to-pedestrian (V2P) impacts because **both collision partners possess their own mass, stiffness, and energy-absorbing structure**.

The fundamental parameters governing any V2V impact are:

- **Closing Velocity ($V_c$)** — Relative velocity at the moment of first contact (m/s).
- **Impact Configuration** — Full Frontal, Offset Frontal, Oblique, Side, Rear, or Underride.
- **Effective Mass ($m_e$)** — Reduced mass based on the combined mass of both vehicles and their structural coupling.
- **Coefficient of Restitution ($e$)** — Ratio of relative separation velocity to approach velocity.
- **Crash Pulse** — Deceleration-time profile transmitted to the occupant compartment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, always treat V2V impact as a **two-body inelastic collision** governed by conservation of linear momentum, unless explicitly stated as perfectly elastic ($e = 1$). The most common exam assumption is a perfectly plastic V2V collision ($e = 0$).

## 1.2 Side Impact Testing — Formal Definition

**Side Impact Testing** is a standardized laboratory crashworthiness assessment in which a **moving deformable barrier (MDB)** — or a striking vehicle in real-world V2V side-impact reconstruction — is propelled laterally into the stationary subject vehicle (SV). The test measures **occupant compartment intrusion**, lateral acceleration channel fidelity, and dummy biofidelic response to quantify injury risk to the **head, thorax, abdomen, and pelvis**.

Standard protocols referenced globally:

| Protocol | Striker Mass | Striker Velocity | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FMVSS 214** (USA) | 1367 kg MDB | 54 km/h | 2009 (revised) |
| **Euro NCAP Side Impact** | 1400 kg MDB | 50 km/h | 2020 protocol |
| **AIS / IIHS Side** | 1500 kg SUV | 32 km/h | Updated 2023 |
| **ECE R95** | 950 kg MDB | 50 km/h | European |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Side impact tests are categorized under the **"Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) Side Impact"** assessment. Always specify **barrier mass, impact velocity, and impact angle (90° default)** when defining a test setup.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pool Ball on a Sled" Intuition

Imagine two **identical pool balls** rolling toward each other on a frictionless ice sheet (this is your V2V impact). At collision, the balls briefly deform (the crumple zone), then bounce apart. Now replace one ball with a **hollow watermelon strapped to a skateboard** (this is your side impact into a stationary car): the skateboard-side is light and stiff, the watermelon-side is heavier but crushable.

- **Frontal V2V** = Two watermelons smashing nose-to-nose — both deform equally.
- **Side Impact** = Watermelon smashed into the side of a stationary watermelon — the side wall has *much less crumple distance* (≈ 100–250 mm) than the front (≈ 400–700 mm), making it inherently more dangerous.
- **Stiffer side structure + shorter crumple distance = higher peak acceleration = higher injury risk.**

This intuitive asymmetry — *less room to crush, less time to decelerate* — is the **entire reason** side impact testing is a separate, regulated test from frontal tests.

## 1.4 Governing Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Speed conversion | 1 km/h | **0.2778 m/s** |
| Standard dummy mass (HIII 50th M) | $m_d$ | **78 kg** |
| Reference thoracic deflection limit | $\delta_{th,max}$ | **< 42 mm** (Hybrid III) |
| Head Injury Criterion limit | HIC$_{15}$ | **< 1000** |
| Side-impact pulse duration (typical) | $t_{pulse}$ | **80–120 ms** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Body V2V Collision Mechanics

Consider two vehicles, **Vehicle A** (mass $m_A$, velocity $v_A$) and **Vehicle B** (mass $m_B$, velocity $v_B$), colliding along a common line of action.

### Step 1 — Conservation of Linear Momentum

Because external impulse during the short contact interval is negligible compared to internal impact forces, total momentum is conserved:

$$\vec{P}_{initial} = \vec{P}_{final}$$

$$m_A v_A + m_B v_B = m_A v_A' + m_B v_B'$$

### Step 2 — Coefficient of Restitution Definition

$$e = \frac{v_B' - v_A'}{v_A - v_B}$$

The boundary values are:
- $e = 1$ → Perfectly elastic collision (kinetic energy conserved).
- $0 < e < 1$ → Partially plastic collision (most V2V crashes).
- $e = 0$ → Perfectly plastic collision (vehicles move as one rigid body post-impact).

### Step 3 — Combined Velocity for Perfectly Plastic Case ($e = 0$)

$$v_{combined} = \frac{m_A v_A + m_B v_B}{m_A + m_B}$$

### Step 4 — Kinetic Energy Dissipated (EES — Energy Equivalent Speed)

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m_A m_B}{m_A + m_B} \cdot (v_A - v_B)^2$$

This lost kinetic energy is what the **crumple zones must absorb** through plastic deformation, friction, and material fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\frac{m_A m_B}{m_A + m_B}$ is the **Reduced Mass** $\mu$. Always compute $\mu$ first in any two-body impact problem — it simplifies all downstream energy equations.

## 2.2 V2V Impact Configuration Matrix

| Configuration | Stiffness Mismatch | Typical Test | KTU Yield |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Frontal** | Symmetric | FMVSS 208 | High |
| **Offset Frontal (40%)** | Asymmetric | Euro NCAP | High |
| **Oblique (15°)** | Rotational component | IIHS | Moderate |
| **Side (MDB 90°)** | Large mismatch | FMVSS 214 | **Very High** |
| **Rear (Rear Moving)** | Symmetric | FMVSS 301 | Moderate |
| **Underride** | Geometric mismatch | EU 2019/2144 | Low |

## 2.3 Side Impact Specific Physics

### 2.3.1 Intrusion Velocity Principle

For side impact, the **door intrudes inward** at velocity $v_{int}$ given by:

$$v_{int} = v_{striker} \cdot \frac{m_{striker}}{m_{striker} + m_{SV,eff}}$$

where $m_{SV,eff}$ is the effective lateral structural resistance mass of the stationary vehicle (typically 20–35% of the curb mass because only one side frame is engaged).

### 2.3.2 Lateral Crash Pulse Equation

The acceleration experienced by the occupant during side impact follows the standard 2-DOF mass-spring-damper model:

$$a(t) = \omega_n^2 \cdot x(t) - 2\zeta \omega_n \cdot v(t)$$

with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$.

### 2.3.3 Thorax Compression Criterion (ThCC)

$$\text{ThCC} = \frac{\delta_{peak}}{\delta_{max}}$$

where $\delta_{peak}$ is measured peak thorax deflection and $\delta_{max}$ is the human-survivability threshold (Hybrid III: 42 mm; WorldSID: 28 mm).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \frac{m_A m_B}{m_A + m_B}$ | Reduced mass of two-body system | kg |
| 2 | $v_c = v_A - v_B$ | Closing velocity (V2V) | m/s |
| 3 | $v_{comb} = \frac{m_A v_A + m_B v_B}{m_A + m_B}$ | Common velocity (plastic) | m/s |
| 4 | $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \mu v_c^2$ | Energy to be absorbed | J |
| 5 | $EES = \frac{\Delta KE}{m_{total}}$ | Energy Equivalent Speed (per unit mass) | J/kg |
| 6 | $HIC = \max \left[ \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \left( \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a \, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$ | Head Injury Criterion | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{ThCC} = \frac{\delta_{peak}}{\delta_{max}}$ | Thorax Compression Criterion | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_{int} = v_{s} \cdot \frac{m_s}{m_s + m_{SV,eff}}$ | Door intrusion velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of impact | rad/s |
| 10 | $F_{avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{d_{crumple}}$ | Average crush force | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (10) is the **direct design lever** for crashworthiness engineers. By increasing crumple distance $d_{crumple}$, the average crush force $F_{avg}$ drops, lowering peak acceleration transmitted to the occupant.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closing Velocity and Reduced Mass Relationship

**Problem Statement:** A 1500 kg car moving at 60 km/h strikes a stationary 1200 kg car in a perfect-plastic rear-end impact. Find (a) common velocity, (b) kinetic energy lost, (c) average crush force if total crumple distance is 0.6 m.

**Given:**
- $m_A = 1500$ kg, $v_A = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s
- $m_B = 1200$ kg, $v_B = 0$ m/s
- $e = 0$, $d_{crumple} = 0.6$ m

### Step (a) — Common Velocity

Apply conservation of momentum along the line of impact:

$$m_A v_A + m_B v_B = (m_A + m_B) v_{comb}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$1500 \cdot 16.67 + 1200 \cdot 0 = (1500 + 1200) \cdot v_{comb}$$

$$25005 + 0 = 2700 \cdot v_{comb}$$

Solve for the combined post-impact velocity:

$$v_{comb} = \frac{25005}{2700}$$

$$v_{comb} = 9.261 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{comb} \approx 9.26 \text{ m/s} \;(\approx 33.34 \text{ km/h})}$$

### Step (b) — Kinetic Energy Dissipated

Initial kinetic energy of the system:

$$KE_i = \frac{1}{2} m_A v_A^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_B v_B^2$$

$$KE_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (16.67)^2 + 0$$

$$KE_i = 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 277.89$$

$$KE_i = 208417.5 \text{ J}$$

Final kinetic energy (after they move together):

$$KE_f = \frac{1}{2} (m_A + m_B) v_{comb}^2$$

$$KE_f = 0.5 \cdot 2700 \cdot (9.261)^2$$

$$KE_f = 0.5 \cdot 2700 \cdot 85.766$$

$$KE_f = 115784.1 \text{ J}$$

Energy dissipated in plastic deformation:

$$\Delta KE = KE_i - KE_f$$

$$\Delta KE = 208417.5 - 115784.1$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE \approx 92633.4 \text{ J} \approx 92.6 \text{ kJ}}$$

### Step (c) — Average Crush Force

Apply the work-energy theorem on the crumple zone. The kinetic energy dissipated equals the work done by the average crush force over the crumple distance:

$$W_{crush} = F_{avg} \cdot d_{crumple} = \Delta KE$$

Rearrange to solve for the average crush force:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{d_{crumple}}$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{92633.4}{0.6}$$

$$\boxed{F_{avg} \approx 154389 \text{ N} \approx 154.4 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This average force corresponds to roughly **15–16 tonnes of deceleration load**. Real-world peak forces during a 60 km/h crash can reach **3–4× this value** because the actual force-displacement curve is highly non-linear (stiffer at the start, softer as crumple progresses).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Side Impact Intrusion Velocity

**Problem Statement:** A 1400 kg MDB strikes the side of a 1500 kg stationary sedan at 50 km/h. If the effective lateral resistance mass of the sedan is 30% of curb mass, find the door intrusion velocity.

**Given:**
- $m_{MDB} = 1400$ kg
- $v_{MDB} = 50$ km/h $= 13.89$ m/s
- $m_{SV,eff} = 0.30 \cdot 1500 = 450$ kg
- $v_{SV} = 0$ m/s

### Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Momentum

Treat the impact as a 1-D plastic collision between MDB and the effective lateral mass:

$$m_{MDB} \cdot v_{MDB} = (m_{MDB} + m_{SV,eff}) \cdot v_{comb}$$

$$1400 \cdot 13.89 = (1400 + 450) \cdot v_{comb}$$

$$19446 = 1850 \cdot v_{comb}$$

Solve for the common velocity (which equals the door intrusion velocity $v_{int}$):

$$v_{int} = \frac{19446}{1850}$$

$$\boxed{v_{int} \approx 10.51 \text{ m/s} \approx 37.84 \text{ km/h}}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Dissipated by Side Structure

$$\Delta KE_{side} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m_{MDB} \cdot m_{SV,eff}}{m_{MDB} + m_{SV,eff}} \cdot v_{MDB}^2$$

Calculate reduced mass:

$$\mu = \frac{1400 \cdot 450}{1400 + 450} = \frac{630000}{1850} = 340.54 \text{ kg}$$

Substitute into energy equation:

$$\Delta KE_{side} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 340.54 \cdot (13.89)^2$$

$$\Delta KE_{side} = 0.5 \cdot 340.54 \cdot 192.93$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE_{side} \approx 32849 \text{ J} \approx 32.85 \text{ kJ}}$$

### Step 3 — Required Side Crumple Distance

Assume average side crush force $F_{avg,side} = 80$ kN (typical B-pillar + door inner design):

$$d_{side} = \frac{\Delta KE_{side}}{F_{avg,side}}$$

$$d_{side} = \frac{32849}{80000}$$

$$\boxed{d_{side} \approx 0.411 \text{ m} \approx 411 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Real side structure typically provides only **150–250 mm** of usable crumple distance. This shortfall is why side impacts are inherently more severe and why **side airbags + curtain airbags + thorax-pelvis airbags** are mandated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HIC Calculation for Side Impact

**Problem Statement:** During a side impact test, the head accelerometer records the following lateral acceleration values: $a(t_1) = 35g$ at $t_1 = 30$ ms, $a(t_2) = 80g$ at $t_2 = 60$ ms. Compute a simplified HIC estimate using the trapezoidal averaging method.

**Given:** $a_1 = 35g$, $a_2 = 80g$, $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = 0.030$ s

### Step 1 — Compute Average Acceleration (Trapezoidal Rule)

$$\bar{a} = \frac{a_1 + a_2}{2}$$

$$\bar{a} = \frac{35 + 80}{2} = 57.5g$$

Convert to SI units:

$$\bar{a} = 57.5 \cdot 9.81 = 564.08 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Simplified HIC Formula

Using the HIC$_{15}$ formulation with $\Delta t = 30$ ms (within the 36 ms limit):

$$HIC_{15} = (\Delta t) \cdot (\bar{a})^{2.5}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$HIC_{15} = (0.030) \cdot (564.08)^{2.5}$$

Compute $(564.08)^{2.5}$ step by step:

First, $(564.08)^2 = 318186.0$

Then, $\sqrt{564.08} = 23.75$

So, $(564.08)^{2.5} = 318186.0 \cdot 23.75 = 7556917.5$

Finally:

$$HIC_{15} = 0.030 \cdot 7556917.5$$

$$\boxed{HIC_{15} \approx 226707}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Since $HIC_{15} \gg 1000$, this represents a **non-survivable** head injury scenario under FMVSS 214 criteria. The test would be flagged as a regulatory failure, requiring redesign of the side impact protection system.

> [!NOTE]
> The HIC exponent of **2.5** makes the metric extremely sensitive to peak accelerations. Doubling $a$ increases HIC by ~5.66×. This is why peak force reduction in the millisecond regime is critical in side impact design.
