5. **Boundary Layer Simulation (Moving Belt / Suction):** Full-scale auto tunnels often include a **moving ground plane (5-belt system)** to simulate the relative motion of the road, which is critical for drag and lift accuracy.

(7 marks: 2 marks for naming, 5 marks for detailed explanation)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Reynolds number** in terms of fluid density, velocity, and characteristic length. A wind tunnel test section is $2$ m × $2$ m and produces a flow of $50$ m/s. Compute the power required to drive the air through the test section assuming fan efficiency of 80%. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **major subsystems of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel** and discuss how **Reynolds number capability** differs between open-circuit and closed-circuit tunnels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation + Numerical:**

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from the ratio of inertial to viscous forces in a fluid element:

$$ Re = \frac{\text{Inertial force}}{\text{Viscous force}} = \frac{\rho V^{2}/L}{\mu V/L^{2}} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} $$

**Numerical calculation (5 marks):**

**Given:** $A_{ts} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ m}^2$, $V = 50 \text{ m/s}$, $\eta = 0.80$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1:** Dynamic pressure.

$$ q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 50^{2} = 1531.25 \text{ Pa} $$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate.

$$ \dot{m} = \rho V A_{ts} = 1.225 \times 50 \times 4 = 245 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 3:** Ideal fan power.

$$ P_{ideal} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = 0.5 \times 245 \times 2500 = 306{,}250 \text{ W} = 306.25 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4:** Actual shaft power.

$$ P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta} = \frac{306.25}{0.80} = 382.81 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 5:** Equivalent horsepower.

$$ P_{shaft} \approx \frac{382.81}{0.746} \approx 513 \text{ hp} $$

(7 marks: derivation 2, numerical 5)

---

**(b) Block diagram + discussion (7 marks):**

A **closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel** has the following major subsystems (refer to Section 4.3 block diagram above):

1. **Inlet Bellmouth** – Smoothly accelerates incoming air.
2. **Honeycomb Flow Straightener** – Breaks down large-scale turbulence.
3. **Contraction Nozzle** – Accelerates flow and reduces TI; contraction ratios of 9:1 to 25:1 are typical.
4. **Test Section** – Houses the model; may have moving ground plane.
5. **Diffuser** – Decelerates flow to reduce fan power requirements.
6. **Drive Fan with Motor** – Provides the energy to circulate air.
7. **Heat Exchanger** – Removes heat added by the fan and motor to maintain constant $\rho$ and $\mu$.
8. **Corner Vanes and Return Ducts** – Guide flow with minimal losses.

**Reynolds number capability comparison:**

| Tunnel Type | Max Re | Reason |
|-------------|--------|--------|
| **Open-circuit** | $\sim 1 \times 10^6$ | Limited by short run time (no cooling), single-pass fan design |
| **Closed-circuit** | $\sim 1 \times 10^7$ | Allows continuous high-speed operation; larger fan power; heat removal via heat exchanger |

Closed-circuit tunnels achieve higher Re due to: (i) larger fan diameter and power, (ii) thermal stability from heat exchangers, (iii) tighter flow quality control, and (iv) longer run durations for steady-state measurements.

(7 marks: 3 for block diagram, 4 for comparison)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Always convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by 5/18, not dividing. A common error is $V_p = 144/3.6$ vs $144/3.6 = 40$ vs mistakenly using 144 directly.
> 2. **Using wrong density:** At non-standard altitude or temperature, $\rho$ and $\mu$ change. Use $\rho = 1.225$ only at sea level, 15 °C.
> 3. **Forgetting fan efficiency:** Raw $P = q V A$ is ideal; always divide by $\eta_{fan}$ for shaft power.
> 4. **Confusing blockage and boundary layer trip:** Blockage is geometric (model cross-section vs test section). BL trip is a small wire or dot on the model to force transition. These are different concepts.
> 5. **Scale ratio inversion:** If model is 1:4, then $\lambda = L_m/L_p = 1/4$, not 4. Students often flip this.
> 6. **Mach number calculation:** Always divide by the local speed of sound $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$, not by 340 or 343 universally without noting the temperature.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember – Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ is the **primary similarity parameter** in vehicle aerodynamics.
- ✅ **Wind tunnel Re capability** = maximum Re the tunnel can deliver on a model of given length at max velocity.
- ✅ **Selection pillars:** Re match, blockage ≤ 5%, TI < 0.1%, power budget, cost.
- ✅ **Closed-circuit (Prandtl)** tunnels give higher Re than **open-circuit (Eiffel)** tunnels.
- ✅ **Subsonic tunnels** ($M < 0.3$ to $0.7$) are used for all ground-vehicle testing.
- ✅ **Velocity scaling law** for Re-similarity: $V_m = V_p / \lambda$ where $\lambda$ is geometric scale ratio.
- ✅ **Fan power formula:** $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{3} A_{ts} / \eta_{fan}$ — note cubic dependence on $V$.
- ✅ **Standard air values to memorize:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $a = 343$ m/s.
- ✅ **Blockage ratio** must be ≤ 5% (frontal) and ≤ 10% (side) for valid automotive tests.
- ✅ **Boundary layer transition trip** is used when full Re matching is impractical, to force turbulent flow on the model.
- ✅ **Moving ground plane / 5-belt system** is a critical feature of full-scale automotive tunnels.
- ✅ **Reynolds number range for typical passenger cars:** $Re = 1 \times 10^6$ (low speed) to $1 \times 10^7$ (highway).
- ✅ For F1 and motorsport: tunnel tests are usually done at 1:2 or 1:3 scale with $V_m$ of 30–50 m/s, with **forced transition**.
- ✅ **Dense gas tunnels** use SF₆ or Freon to increase $\rho$ and achieve higher Re at lower $V_m$.
- ✅ **Compressibility effects** are negligible for road vehicles (always $M < 0.3$), so air can be treated as incompressible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Requirements, Details, and Mounting
Test Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Test Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Procedure (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Test Procedure* is a formally documented, standardized sequence of operations, conditions, instruments, and methodologies prescribed by recognized national or international regulatory bodies (e.g., **SAE, ISO, BIS, AIS, ECE, FMVSS**) to quantitatively measure, record, and validate the performance characteristics of a vehicle under controlled, repeatable, and reproducible conditions. The procedure defines the **test environment, sample size, calibration requirements, data acquisition rate, statistical reduction method, and pass/fail criteria** for a given performance attribute.

In the context of **OEAUT831 (Vehicle Performance and Testing)**, "Test Procedure" specifically refers to the **engineered sequence of actions** used to evaluate the following prime vehicle performance attributes:

| S.No | Performance Attribute | Standard Test Reference (Typical) |
|------|----------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Maximum Speed | SAE J1591 / ISO 7117 |
| 2 | Acceleration (0–60, 0–100, 0–400 m) | SAE J1491 / ECE R.39 |
| 3 | Braking Distance & Efficiency | ECE R.13 / FMVSS 135 |
| 4 | Fuel Economy (Road & Cycle) | ISO 7851 / AIS 021 |
| 5 | Gradability (Hill Climb) | SAE J2188 / IS 11864 |
| 6 | Coast-down / Rolling Resistance | SAE J1263 / SAE J2452 |
| 7 | NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) | ISO 362 / ISO 2631 |
| 8 | Emissions (Tailpipe) | Euro 6 / BS-VI / EPA Tier 3 |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍳 Kitchen Analogy — "The Standardized Recipe"**
> Imagine you are a chef in a restaurant, and you want every customer in every branch across the world to taste *exactly* the same dish. To achieve this, you cannot just say "add some salt and cook for a while." You must write a **standardized recipe** specifying the *exact* quantity of ingredients, the *precise* temperature, the *exact* cooking time, the *order* of operations, and even the *brand* of the pan.
> 
> In the same way, an automotive engineer cannot say "drive the car and see how fast it goes." The test must be **standardized** so that a Maruti Suzuki tested in Manesar gives the **same numerical result** as the same model tested in Detroit or Stuttgart. The *Test Procedure* is that "standardized recipe" for measuring vehicle performance.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Connection:** When Tesla publishes a 0–100 km/h time of **2.1 seconds** for the Model S Plaid, that number is not an estimate — it is the result of a rigorously standardized test, conducted on a level surface, at a defined ambient temperature (**20 °C**), with a defined wind speed (< **3 m/s**), and tire pressure set to manufacturer specifications. That is what a *Test Procedure* guarantees.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Test Conditions (Bolder = Industry-Standard Default):**
> - **Ambient Temperature: 20 °C** (Range: 5 °C – 35 °C permissible, 20 °C ideal)
> - **Atmospheric Pressure: 101.325 kPa** (Standard sea-level pressure)
> - **Relative Humidity: 50%** (Permissible range: 30% – 70%)
> - **Wind Speed: < 3 m/s** (Component along test direction)
> - **Track Grade: < 0.5%** (Effectively level)
> - **Track Surface: Dry, clean asphalt or concrete** (Skid number ≥ 0.7)
> - **Test Mass: Kerb Mass + 75 kg driver** (or per AIS/ECE protocol)
> - **Tire Pressure: Manufacturer-recommended cold pressure** (typically 220–250 kPa passenger car)
> - **Fuel: Reference fuel (e.g., India: IS 2796, Europe: EN 228, USA: ASTM D4814)**
> - **Pre-conditioning Run:** Minimum **15 km** or until driveline is thermally stable
> - **Data Sampling Rate:** ≥ **10 Hz** (SAE J211 channel frequency class)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Standardized Test Procedure

A complete vehicle performance test procedure is composed of **seven logical layers**. Each layer builds on the previous one:

### Layer 1 — *Scope & Applicability*
Defines the vehicle class (M1, N1, L7, etc.), the performance attribute being measured, and the regulatory domain (Type Approval, Homologation, R&D, or Quality Audit).

### Layer 2 — *Pre-Test Conditioning*
- **Soak Time:** Vehicle left at ambient for a minimum period (typically **6–12 hours**) to equalize temperatures.
- **Tire Scrub-in:** To remove mold release and stabilize rolling resistance.
- **Brake Burnish:** Repetitive stops at defined deceleration to seat pads/discs.

### Layer 3 — *Test Environment Control*
- **Wind Tunnel / Calm-Air Site:** Wind component correction (no tailwind/headwind > **3 m/s**).
- **Track Surface Characterization:** Skid number, gradient map, length, and width.
- **Ambient Envelope:** Temperature, pressure, humidity recorded at start and end.

### Layer 4 — *Instrumentation & Calibration*
| Instrument | Typical Accuracy | Standard |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| Speed sensor (GPS / 5th wheel) | ± 0.1 km/h | SAE J2188 |
| Accelerometer (longitudinal) | ± 0.1 m/s² | SAE J211 CFC 60 |
| Fuel flow meter | ± 0.5% of reading | ISO 3930 |
| Thermocouples (oil, coolant) | ± 1 °C | ASTM E230 |
| Pneumatic tube / Photo-eye timer | ± 0.01 s | ISO 7117 |

### Layer 5 — *Test Execution Sequence*
- **Warm-up Phase** → Driver familiarization laps
- **Measurement Phase** → Run(s) in **opposite directions** to cancel track gradient and wind bias
- **Repetition Protocol** → Minimum **3 valid runs** (or as per standard) to allow statistical reduction

### Layer 6 — *Data Reduction & Statistical Analysis*
- **Outlier Rejection:** Dixon's Q-test or Chauvenet's criterion.
- **Mean, Standard Deviation, 95% Confidence Interval** reported.
- **Correction Factors:** Air density, road slope, wind.

### Layer 7 — *Reporting & Documentation*
- Pass/Fail against regulation threshold
- Traceability chain of instruments
- Test report signed by certified test engineer

---

## 2.2 Why the Procedure Matters — The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!TIP]
> **🧠 Engineering Rationale:**
> 1. **Repeatability** — If a test cannot be reproduced, its data is scientifically invalid.
> 2. **Comparability** — Buyers, regulators, and competitors must compare "apples to apples."
> 3. **Legal Standing** — For type approval, the test report is a legal document; non-standard procedures are inadmissible.
> 4. **Safety** — Performance tests (e.g., braking) push vehicles to physical limits; lack of procedure is catastrophic.
> 5. **Marketing & Benchmarking** — "World's fastest" claims are only credible if backed by a documented test.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📐 Master Formula Table for Test Procedure Calculations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Air Density | $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{abs}}$ | $P_{atm}$ in Pa, $T_{abs}$ in K, $R_{specific} = 287.05 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ | Used for aerodynamic correction |
| 2 | Acceleration (Mean) | $a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{t_f - t_i}$ | $a$ in m/s², $v$ in m/s, $t$ in s | Run in both directions |
| 3 | Time-Speed Integration | $t = \displaystyle\int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{m \cdot dv}{F_{net}(v)}$ | $F_{net}$ = tractive force − resistances | For analytical prediction |
| 4 | Coast-Down Deceleration | $a_{cd} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot s_{cd}} = f \cdot g + \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2m} v^2$ | $f$ = rolling resistance coeff., $C_d$ drag coeff., $A$ frontal area | SAE J2452 two-speed method |
| 5 | Braking Distance (KE) | $s_b = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{brake}} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | $v$ in m/s, $\mu$ = tire-road friction | Ideal locked-wheel braking |
| 6 | Hill Climb (Gradability) | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_{trac,max} - F_{roll} - F_{aero}}{m \cdot g}$ | $\theta$ in rad, $F$ in N | SAE J2188 |
| 7 | Wind Correction Factor | $C_{wind} = 1 + \dfrac{v_{wind}}{v_{vehicle}}$ (approx. headwind/tailwind bias) | Dimensionless | < 3 m/s allowed uncorrected |
| 8 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{roll} = f \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | $f$ ≈ 0.012 (car tire on asphalt) | ISO 18164 |
| 9 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s | Function of $v^2$ |
| 10 | Statistical CI (95%) | $CI = \bar{x} \pm t_{(0.975,n-1)} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $s$ = sample std. dev., $n$ = sample size | Used for reporting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Tip for KTU:** When writing formula tables, all absolute-value expressions are written using `\vert` rather than `|` to avoid breaking markdown parsing. Example: $\vert v_{wind} \vert < 3$ m/s.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Test Procedure is the **backbone of every automotive industry vertical**:

- 🏭 **OEM R&D** — Verifies design targets before SOP (Start of Production).
- 📜 **Type Approval / Homologation** — Legal certification required before sale in any market (e.g., **AIS in India, ECE in EU, FMVSS in USA**).
- 🏁 **Motorsport** — FIA-regulated procedures ensure fair competition.
- 🛡️ **Insurance & Legal** — Accident reconstruction uses coast-down data.
- 📊 **Marketing** — Public performance claims must be defensible (e.g., Tata Punch EV's claimed 0–100 km/h time).
- 🌍 **Emissions & Fuel Economy** — Mandatory **WLTP** (Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure) cycles since 2017 in major markets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acceleration Time from 0 to V (Analytical Form)

The general equation of motion for a vehicle accelerating from rest is governed by **Newton's Second Law**:

$$
\sum F_{x} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}
$$

The **net tractive force** is the driving force minus all resistive forces. For a rear-wheel-drive passenger car on level ground:

$$
F_{net}(v) = F_{trac}(v) - F_{roll}(v) - F_{aero}(v)
$$

**Step 1 — Define each resistive force:**

$$
F_{roll}(v) = f \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \quad ; \quad \theta \approx 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta \approx 1
$$

$$
F_{roll}(v) = f \cdot m \cdot g
$$

$$
F_{aero}(v) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the equation of motion:**

$$
m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac}(v) - f \cdot m \cdot g - \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $dt$:**

$$
dt = \dfrac{m \cdot dv}{F_{trac}(v) - f \cdot m \cdot g - \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $v = 0$ to $v = V$** to obtain the total acceleration time $T_{0 \to V}$:

$$
T_{0 \to V} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{V} \dfrac{m \cdot dv}{F_{trac}(v) - f \cdot m \cdot g - \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^2}
$$

**Step 5 — (Test Procedure Output)** The procedure is to **measure $T_{0 \to V}$** experimentally, then **back-solve** for any unknown parameter (e.g., $C_d$) by curve-fitting. This is the **inverse problem** of the test procedure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Coast-Down Test (Two-Speed Method — SAE J2452)

Coast-down is a **passive deceleration test** in which the vehicle is brought to a steady speed, then placed in neutral and allowed to decelerate freely. The energy balance gives:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2 - \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = F_{roll} \cdot d + F_{aero,avg} \cdot d
$$

**Step 1 — Define the average resistive forces over the distance $d = s_{12}$:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) = (f \cdot m \cdot g) \cdot s_{12} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A \cdot v_{avg}^2 \cdot s_{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean square velocity** (for the $v^2$ term in aerodynamic drag):

$$
v_{avg}^2 = \dfrac{v_1^2 + v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $s_{12}$ to express as average deceleration** $\bar{a}_{cd}$:

$$
\bar{a}_{cd} = \dfrac{v_1^2 - v_2^2}{2 s_{12}} = f \cdot g + \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} \cdot v_{avg}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Two-speed method (Yates, Berger, Tate — SAE 750964):**
Perform **two runs at two different speed brackets** (typically **90–70 km/h** and **60–40 km/h**). Subtract the two equations to eliminate $f$:

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \bar{a}_{cd,low} = \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} \cdot (v_{avg,high}^2 - v_{avg,low}^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the drag coefficient $C_d$:**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2 m (\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \bar{a}_{cd,low})}{\rho \cdot A \cdot (v_{avg,high}^2 - v_{avg,low}^2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Back-substitute to find $f$:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{g}\left[\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} v_{avg,high}^2\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two speeds?** A single coast-down run mixes $f$ and $C_d$ into one equation with two unknowns. The two-speed method provides an *over-determined* system, enabling algebraic separation. This is the **most cited KTU-level derivation** for the Test Procedure module.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A passenger car of mass **1500 kg** is tested on a level road. Frontal area **A = 2.2 m²**. Air density **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³**. Two coast-down runs yield:
- High-speed run: $v_1 = 90$ km/h = **25 m/s**, $v_2 = 70$ km/h = **19.44 m/s**, $s_{12} = 412$ m.
- Low-speed run: $v_3 = 60$ km/h = **16.67 m/s**, $v_4 = 40$ km/h = **11.11 m/s**, $s_{34} = 358$ m.

**Find** $C_d$ and $f$.

**Solution — Step A: Convert units and compute average decelerations.**

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,high} = \dfrac{25^2 - 19.44^2}{2 \cdot 412} = \dfrac{625 - 377.91}{824} = \dfrac{247.09}{824} = 0.2999 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,low} = \dfrac{16.67^2 - 11.11^2}{2 \cdot 358} = \dfrac{277.89 - 123.43}{716} = \dfrac{154.46}{716} = 0.2157 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Solution — Step B: Compute $v_{avg}^2$ for each run.**

$$
v_{avg,high}^2 = \dfrac{25^2 + 19.44^2}{2} = \dfrac{625 + 377.91}{2} = 501.46 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
v_{avg,low}^2 = \dfrac{16.67^2 + 11.11^2}{2} = \dfrac{277.89 + 123.43}{2} = 200.66 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Solution — Step C: Apply the two-speed formula.**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1500 \cdot (0.2999 - 0.2157)}{1.2 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (501.46 - 200.66)}
$$

$$
C_d = \dfrac{3000 \cdot 0.0842}{2.64 \cdot 300.80} = \dfrac{252.6}{794.1} = 0.318
$$

**Solution — Step D: Back-substitute to find $f$.**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2999 - \dfrac{1.2 \cdot 0.318 \cdot 2.2}{2 \cdot 1500} \cdot 501.46\right]
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2999 - 0.0002798 \cdot 501.46\right] = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2999 - 0.1403\right] = \dfrac{0.1596}{9.81} = 0.01627
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Result:** $C_d \approx 0.32$ and $f \approx 0.016$, which are realistic values for a modern sedan. The drag coefficient is dimensionless, and the rolling resistance coefficient is also dimensionless (a ratio of forces).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Test Procedure

| Test Goal | Track / Bench? | Speed Range | Minimum Runs | Critical Variable | Standard |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|----------|
| Maximum speed | Both | $v_{max}$ at $F_{trac} = F_{resist}$ | 2 (both directions) | Track length ≥ 1 km | ISO 7117 |
| 0–100 km/h | Track | 0 to 100 km/h | 3 in each direction | Runway surface, wind | ECE R.39 |
| Braking (cold) | Track | 100 → 0 km/h | 6 (3 cold, 3 hot) | Tire-road friction, fade | ECE R.13 |
| Fuel economy | Bench (chassis dyno) | WLTC / NEDC cycle | 3 cycles | Ambient temp, accessory load | UNECE R.101 |
| Coast-down | Track | 90→70, 60→40 km/h | 3 per bracket | Wind < 3 m/s, neutral gear | SAE J2452 |
| Gradability | Track | Steady crawl + grade | 1 per grade | Wheel slip, traction | SAE J2188 |
| Emissions | Bench | WLTP | 3 cycles | Reference fuel, precondition | UNECE R.83 |
| NVH (Pass-by) | Track | 50 km/h WOT / CRD | 4 (2 sides × 2 directions) | Microphone positions | ISO 362 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
> **Define "Test Procedure" in the context of vehicle performance evaluation. List any **four** essential preconditions that must be satisfied before a performance test can be conducted.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Test Procedure** is a formally documented, standardized sequence of operations, conditions, instruments, and methodologies prescribed by regulatory bodies (e.g., **SAE, ISO, AIS, ECE**) to quantitatively measure and validate vehicle performance attributes under controlled, repeatable conditions.

**Four Essential Preconditions (½ mark each):**
1. **Ambient conditions** within prescribed envelope: temperature 20 °C ± 15 °C, wind < 3 m/s.
2. **Track surface** characterization: dry, level (grade < 0.5%), skid number ≥ 0.7.
3. **Instrument calibration** traceable to national/international standards (valid certificates).
4. **Vehicle pre-conditioning**: soak time, tire scrub-in, brake burnish complete, and fuel type verified.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
> **Why is the "two-direction run" protocol mandatory in standardized acceleration and coast-down tests? What error does it eliminate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The two-direction protocol (running the test in **direction A** and then in the **opposite direction B**) is mandatory to **eliminate systematic bias due to track gradient and prevailing wind**.

- **Track gradient** causes one run to climb a slight incline (overestimates deceleration / underestimates speed) and the other to descend (opposite effect).
- **Wind bias** similarly affects aerodynamic drag in a one-sided manner.

By **averaging the results of opposite-direction runs**, the bias components cancel out, leaving only the **true vehicle performance** value. This is essential for **repeatability and legal validity** of the test data.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the formula $C_d = \dfrac{2 m \cdot a_{cd}}{\rho A v^2}$ from a **single** coast-down run. This is **mathematically incorrect** as it cannot separate $C_d$ from $f$. Use the **two-speed method** or a regression-based separation.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to **convert km/h to m/s** before substituting into the formula. Even one such error costs **2 marks** in the numerical part.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting final $C_d$ with units. $C_d$ is **dimensionless**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not stating the **standard reference** (e.g., SAE J2452) when describing the test — examiners allocate **1 mark** for this.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In the comparison between WLTP and NEDC, students often omit the **lab vs. real-world gap** discussion.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `Choice 1`

> **A.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete standardized procedure** for evaluating the **maximum speed of a passenger car** on a test track. Include all preconditions, instrumentation, run protocol, and reporting requirements.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 1400 kg passenger car is coasted down on a level test track. Two runs yield the following data:
> - High-speed run: $v_1 = 110$ km/h, $v_2 = 90$ km/h, distance covered = **520 m**.
> - Low-speed run: $v_3 = 70$ km/h, $v_4 = 50$ km/h, distance covered = **410 m**.
> - Frontal area $A = 2.0$ m², air density $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³.
> - Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².
>
> Determine the **aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$** and the **rolling resistance coefficient $f$** using the SAE J2452 two-speed method.

**📚 Model Solution (Part A-a — 7 Marks):**

The standardized maximum-speed test procedure has the following stages:

**Step 1: Pre-Test Verification (1 Mark)**
- Confirm track length ≥ **2 km**, gradient < 0.5%, dry surface, skid number ≥ 0.7.
- Verify ambient: temp 20 °C, wind < 3 m/s along track axis, pressure recorded.
- Vehicle mass = kerb + 75 kg driver. Tire pressure = manufacturer spec. Reference fuel loaded.

**Step 2: Conditioning (1 Mark)**
- **Soak** for minimum 6 hours.
- Tire scrub-in for 50 km. Brake burnish.
- Warm-up of 15 km at moderate speed.

**Step 3: Instrumentation (1 Mark)**
- 5th wheel or GPS speed sensor (± 0.1 km/h).
- Data logger ≥ 10 Hz.
- Anemometer, temperature probe, barometer.

**Step 4: Execution (2 Marks)**
- Accelerate in **direction A** until maximum speed is reached and held constant for ≥ 500 m.
- Repeat in **direction B** (opposite) to cancel wind/grade bias.
- Minimum **3 valid runs per direction**.

**Step 5: Data Reduction (1 Mark)**
- Apply Dixon's Q-test for outliers.
- Compute mean and 95% CI.
- Correct for any wind not fully cancelled.

**Step 6: Reporting (1 Mark)**
- Document test report with full traceability, instrument IDs, calibration certificates, weather log, raw data, and signed certification.

---

**📚 Model Solution (Part A-b — 7 Marks):**

**Given Data Conversion:**

$$
v_1 = 110 \text{ km/h} = 30.56 \text{ m/s} \quad ; \quad v_2 = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25.00 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_3 = 70 \text{ km/h} = 19.44 \text{ m/s} \quad ; \quad v_4 = 50 \text{ km/h} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 1: Compute average decelerations (1 Mark):**

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,high} = \dfrac{30.56^2 - 25.00^2}{2 \cdot 520} = \dfrac{933.91 - 625.00}{1040} = \dfrac{308.91}{1040} = 0.2970 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,low} = \dfrac{19.44^2 - 13.89^2}{2 \cdot 410} = \dfrac{377.91 - 192.93}{820} = \dfrac{184.98}{820} = 0.2256 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute $v_{avg}^2$ for each run (1 Mark):**

$$
v_{avg,high}^2 = \dfrac{30.56^2 + 25.00^2}{2} = \dfrac{933.91 + 625.00}{2} = 779.46 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
v_{avg,low}^2 = \dfrac{19.44^2 + 13.89^2}{2} = \dfrac{377.91 + 192.93}{2} = 285.42 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 3: Apply the two-speed formula (3 Marks):**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2 m (\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \bar{a}_{cd,low})}{\rho A (v_{avg,high}^2 - v_{avg,low}^2)}
$$

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1400 \cdot (0.2970 - 0.2256)}{1.18 \cdot 2.0 \cdot (779.46 - 285.42)}
$$

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2800 \cdot 0.0714}{2.36 \cdot 494.04} = \dfrac{199.92}{1165.93} = 0.1715
$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute to find $f$ (2 Marks):**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{g}\left[\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} v_{avg,high}^2\right]
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2970 - \dfrac{1.18 \cdot 0.1715 \cdot 2.0}{2 \cdot 1400} \cdot 779.46\right]
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2970 - 0.0001445 \cdot 779.46\right] = \dfrac{1}{9.81}\left[0.2970 - 0.1127\right]
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{0.1843}{9.81} = 0.01879
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$
\boxed{C_d \approx 0.172 \quad ; \quad f \approx 0.0188}
$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — `Choice 2 (Alternative)`

> **B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **SAE J2452 Coast-Down Test Procedure** for determining aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance. Include the principle, mathematical basis, instrumentation, and the "two-speed" rationale.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **WLTP** (Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure) with the older **NEDC** procedure. Tabulate differences in **cycle length, average speed, peak speed, ambient correction, and real-world correlation**. Why is WLTP considered more representative?

**📚 Model Solution (Part B-a — 7 Marks):**

**Principle (1 Mark):**
The coast-down test is a **passive deceleration** procedure in which the vehicle, in neutral, decelerates under its own resistive forces. By measuring the deceleration profile, the resistive forces (rolling + aerodynamic) can be **back-calculated** because no tractive force is applied.

**Mathematical Basis (3 Marks):**
The vehicle's net deceleration is:

$$
a_{cd}(v) = f \cdot g + \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} v^2
$$

This is a **quadratic in $v^2$**. A single coast-down run provides only **one equation with two unknowns** ($f$ and $C_d$). The **two-speed method** uses two runs at different speed brackets to form a solvable system:

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,high} = f \cdot g + \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} v_{avg,high}^2
$$

$$
\bar{a}_{cd,low} = f \cdot g + \dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2 m} v_{avg,low}^2
$$

Subtraction eliminates $f$, yielding:

$$
C_d = \dfrac{2 m (\bar{a}_{cd,high} - \bar{a}_{cd,low})}{\rho A (v_{avg,high}^2 - v_{avg,low}^2)}
$$

**Instrumentation (1.5 Marks):**
- Non-contact 5th-wheel or GPS speed sensor (CFC 60 class).
- Inertial measurement unit (optional cross-check).
- Anemometer, barometer, thermometer.
- Data logger with ≥ 10 Hz sampling.

**Two-Speed Rationale (1.5 Marks):**
The high-speed run (e.g., 110→90 km/h) is dominated by **aerodynamic drag** ($F_{aero} \propto v^2$).
The low-speed run (e.g., 70→50 km/h) is dominated by **rolling resistance** (which is nearly speed-independent for small speed change).
This separation enables algebraic decomposition, which is mathematically impossible with a single run.

---

**📚 Model Solution (Part B-b — 7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | NEDC (Legacy) | WLTP (Current) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Cycle length | **11.0 km** | **23.3 km** |
| Duration | **~20 min** | **~30 min** |
| Average speed | **33.6 km/h** | **46.5 km/h** |
| Peak speed | **120 km/h** | **131 km/h** |
| Number of phases | 2 (Urban + Extra-Urban) | 4 (Low, Medium, High, Extra-High) |
| Gear shifts | Fixed (pre-scripted) | Driver-model based (coherence) |
| Ambient correction | None | **Yes** (coast-down based family correction) |
| Real-world gap | **15–25%** (lab > real) | **~5–10%** |
| Introduced | 1992 (Euro 1) | 2017 (Euro 6), India adopted 2017 onwards |

**Why WLTP is more representative (2 Marks):**
1. **Higher average & peak speeds** match modern driving behavior on highways.
2. **Four-phase structure** (Low, Medium, High, Extra-High) captures diverse driving patterns.
3. **Driver-model-based gear shifts** simulate realistic accelerator-pedal use.
4. **Ambient and road-load family correction** accounts for optional equipment and altitude.
5. **Reduced laboratory-to-real-world gap** — closer to actual customer fuel economy.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Test Procedure**

- ✅ A **Test Procedure** = standardized, documented, repeatable measurement protocol.
- ✅ **Standard bodies** to remember: **SAE, ISO, BIS/AIS, ECE, FMVSS, EPA, UN/ECE**.
- ✅ **Ambient envelope:** $T = 20 \pm 15$ °C, $P$ near 101.325 kPa, wind $\vert v_{wind} \vert < 3$ m/s.
- ✅ **Pre-conditioning:** Soak ≥ 6 h, tire scrub-in, brake burnish, warm-up ≥ 15 km.
- ✅ **Minimum runs:** ≥ **3 valid runs in each of 2 directions** (to cancel grade + wind bias).
- ✅ **Outlier test:** Dixon's Q or Chauvenet's criterion at 95% confidence.
- ✅ **Data reporting:** Mean ± 95% CI; $C_d$ is **dimensionless**; $f$ is **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Two-speed coast-down (SAE J2452)** is the gold standard for $C_d$ and $f$ separation.
- ✅ **Braking test (ECE R.13):** Type-0 (cold) and Type-III (hot/fade) sequences mandatory.
- ✅ **WLTP** is the current global benchmark, replacing NEDC since 2017.
- ✅ **Always quote the standard** (e.g., "as per SAE J2452") in descriptive answers — **+1 mark**.
- ✅ **Always convert km/h → m/s** before numerical substitution — most common error.
- ✅ **Single-run coast-down** formulas are **wrong** unless regression-based.

> **🎓 Closing Note:** Master the **two-speed derivation** and the **WLTP vs. NEDC comparison** — these two alone account for over **40% of marks** in the ESE Module 3 of OEAUT831.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Noise Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automotive Noise Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — AUTOMOTIVE NOISE CRITERIA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Automotive Noise Criteria** refer to the standardized quantitative metrics, measurement procedures, and legal limits used to evaluate sound radiated by road vehicles — both the noise emitted to the environment (exterior noise) and the noise experienced by the occupants (interior noise). These criteria form the engineering and regulatory backbone of vehicle acoustic comfort, public health compliance, and homologation testing under bodies such as **ISO, ECE, SAE, AIS (India), and BIS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEAUT831 — Vehicle Performance and Testing, Module 3)**, students must be conversant with the fundamental acoustic quantities (Sound Pressure Level, Sound Power Level, A-weighting), the principal international standards (ISO 362 / ECE R51, ISO 5130), and interior noise rating systems (NC / NCB / RC Mark II curves).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brightness vs. Loudness" Idea
Imagine two people whispering and one person shouting. Our ears do not respond to sound energy linearly; a sound that is **10 times more energetic** only feels **twice as loud**. Similarly, light intensity measured linearly feels very different from what the eye perceives as "brightness." Automotive noise criteria translate raw acoustic pressure into human-perceived loudness scales (dBA, sones, phons) and into standardized rating curves that engineers and legislators can use to compare vehicles objectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu Pa = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ Pa$
> - **Reference Sound Power:** $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}\ W = 1\ pW$
> - **Threshold of Hearing:** $0\ dB$ SPL (at $1\ kHz$)
> - **Threshold of Pain:** $\approx 120\ dB$ SPL

### 1.3 Classification of Automotive Noise
Automotive noise is broadly classified into two domains:

1. **Exterior (Environmental) Noise** — radiated outward, regulated by **pass-by noise** standards.
2. **Interior (Cabin) Noise** — experienced by occupants, regulated by **NC/NCB/RC curves** and OEM comfort targets.

Within these, noise sources include **power-train (engine, transmission, exhaust)**, **tyre–road interaction**, **aerodynamic (wind) noise**, and **auxiliary systems (HVAC, accessories)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Acoustic Quantities

### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)
The **Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$)** is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure $p$ relative to the reference pressure $p_{ref}$.

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

**Why logarithmic?** The human ear responds logarithmically. A sound 10 times more energetic is heard as "twice as loud," but mathematically it is only a **+10 dB increase**. A doubling of perceived loudness requires approximately a **+10 dB rise**.

### 2.1.2 Sound Power Level (PWL)
**Sound Power ($W$)** is the acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source — an intrinsic property of the source independent of distance.

$$L_w = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{ref}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

### 2.1.3 Sound Intensity ($I$)
The vector quantity describing the time-averaged rate of acoustic energy flow per unit area:

$$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho \, c} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

where $\rho$ = air density ($\approx 1.21\ kg/m^3$) and $c$ = speed of sound ($\approx 343\ m/s$ at $20^\circ C$).

### 2.1.4 A-Weighting (dBA)
The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. **A-weighting** filters the raw SPL according to the equal-loudness contour near $40$ phons, de-emphasizing low-frequency components.

$$L_{A} = L_p + \Delta A(f) \quad \text{[dBA]}$$

where $\Delta A(f)$ is the frequency-dependent A-weighting correction (in dB), tabulated at standard octave bands ($31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000\ Hz$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why A-weighting is used in automotive noise regulations:** Almost all legal pass-by and stationary noise limits worldwide are quoted in **dBA**, because it correlates well with human annoyance and traffic noise perception.

### 2.1.5 Addition of Two Uncorrelated Sound Levels
When two independent sources act simultaneously, their powers add — not their decibel values.

$$L_{total} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + 10^{\left(\frac{L_2 - L_1}{10}\right)}\right] \quad \text{[dB]}$$

**Quick rule of thumb:**
- Difference $\geq 10\ dB$ → higher source dominates; lower source may be ignored.
- Difference $= 3\ dB$ → add **$+1.76\ dB$** ($\approx +2\ dB$).
- Difference $= 0\ dB$ → add **$+3\ dB$**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | $p_{ref} = 20\ \mu Pa$ |
| 2 | Sound Power Level | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}\ W$ |
| 3 | Sound Intensity | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$ | W/m² | $\rho c \approx 415$ rayl |
| 4 | A-weighted SPL | $L_A = L_p + \Delta A(f)$ | dBA | Tabulated $\Delta A(f)$ |
| 5 | Addition of two SPLs | $L_{tot} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}\!\left[1 + 10^{\frac{L_2-L_1}{10}}\right]$ | dB | Uncorrelated sources |
| 6 | Subtraction of two SPLs | $L_1 = L_{tot} - 10\log_{10}\!\left[1 - 10^{\frac{L_2 - L_{tot}}{10}}\right]$ | dB | Back-calculation |
| 7 | Halving of distance (free field) | $\Delta L_p = -6\ dB$ per halving | dB | Point source, spherical spreading |
| 8 | Doubling of distance | $\Delta L_p = +6\ dB$ per halving reversed | dB | Line source: $+3\ dB$ per halving |
| 9 | NC curve target | Octave-band SPL $\le$ NC value at each band | dB | Interior rating |
| 10 | Loudness (sone) | $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$ | sone | $P$ = phon level |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe `|` replaced by `\vert` / `\mid` inside table cells above to prevent markdown table corruption, per KTU-Premium-Engine formatting protocol.**

---

## 2.3 Major International Standards Governing Automotive Noise

| Standard | Scope | Microphone Setup | Test Condition |
|----------|-------|------------------|----------------|
| **ISO 362-1** | Pass-by noise (M & N categories) | $7.5\ m$ from track centerline, $1.2\ m$ height | Vehicle at full-throttle acceleration through PP' line |
| **ECE R51** | UN ECE pass-by noise | Same as ISO 362 | Homologation in Europe/India |
| **ISO 5130** | Stationary exhaust noise | $0.5\ m$ from exhaust outlet, $45^\circ$ from outlet axis | Engine at rated speed & $75\%$ rated speed |
| **ISO 5128** | Interior noise measurement | Microphones at driver's ear position | Sweeping engine RPM or constant road speed |
| **SAE J1477** | Stationary noise (US practice) | $0.5\ m$, $45^\circ$ from outlet | SAE J1349 engine speed |
| **AIS-018 / AIS-019** | Indian homologation (pass-by & stationary) | Adopted from ECE R51 / ISO 5130 | Mandatory under CMVR |

---

## 2.4 Interior Noise Rating — NC / NCB / RC Mark II Curves

**Noise Criterion (NC) curves**, introduced by Leo Beranek (1957), are families of equal-noise contours spanning octave bands from $63\ Hz$ to $8000\ Hz$. They are used to specify the maximum permissible sound pressure level in each octave band inside a vehicle cabin.

- **NC curves:** Set absolute upper limits; quietest curves (NC-15, NC-20) target bedrooms/hospitals; automotive cabins typically target **NC-45 to NC-55**.
- **NCB (Balanced Noise Criterion):** Adds low-frequency constraints to avoid "rumble" perception.
- **RC Mark II (Room Criterion):** Used in modern vehicles; combines a target RC curve with a calculated **Speech Interference Level (SIL)** and **Noise Criterion (NCB mark II)** value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams: remember the **three-step procedure to evaluate a vehicle interior against NC curves**:
> 1. Measure octave-band SPL from $63\ Hz$ to $8000\ Hz$.
> 2. Plot the spectrum on the NC chart template.
> 3. The **lowest NC curve that the spectrum touches (without crossing)** is the **NC rating** of the cabin.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Homologation:** Every vehicle sold in India/Europe must pass ECE R51 / AIS-018 type-approval tests.
- **NVH Engineering:** OEMs use interior NC ratings to benchmark luxury vs. economy cabins.
- **Public Health:** WHO recommends outdoor noise $< 53\ dBA$ to prevent sleep disturbance — a direct driver of vehicle exterior noise limits.
- **EV Acoustics:** With the rise of electric vehicles, AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System) introduces *new* criteria at low speeds ($< 30\ km/h$) — an emerging KTU-relevant topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Pressure to Decibels

We start with the definition of Sound Pressure Level (SPL). The RMS sound pressure $p_{rms}$ is the time-averaged effective pressure of a fluctuating acoustic wave. Because the ear's response is logarithmic, we express the ratio $p_{rms}/p_{ref}$ on a base-10 logarithmic scale.

### 3.1.1 Formal Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)\ \text{dB} \\[6pt]
&= 20\left[\log_{10}(p_{rms}) - \log_{10}(p_{ref})\right] \\[6pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{p_{ref}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $p_{ref} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ Pa$, we can substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{p_{ref}}\right) &= \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{4}) \\[6pt]
&= \log_{10}(5) + 4 \log_{10}(10) \\[6pt]
&= 0.69897 + 4 = 4.69897
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}) + 93.98\ \text{dB}$$

This rearranged form is useful when the absolute pressure is directly given in pascals.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example — Calculation of SPL

**Problem:** A vehicle engine produces a sound pressure of $0.1\ Pa$ at the driver's ear location. Calculate the SPL.

**Step 1 — Identify reference pressure:**

$$p_{ref} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ Pa$$

**Step 2 — Form the pressure ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}} &= \frac{0.1}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.1}{2 \times 10^{-5}} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}000 = 5 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SPL formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 20 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{3}) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \left[\log_{10}(5) + 3 \log_{10}(10)\right] \\[6pt]
&= 20 \left[0.69897 + 3\right] \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times 3.69897 \\[6pt]
&= 73.98\ \text{dB} \approx 74\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The SPL is approximately **$74\ \text{dB}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb verified:** An RMS pressure of $0.1\ Pa$ (a "moderately loud" engine at the ear) corresponds to $\approx 74\ \text{dB}$, consistent with typical cabin target for sedans at idle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Addition of Two Uncorrelated Sound Levels

We want the combined SPL when two independent sources of SPL $L_1$ and $L_2$ radiate simultaneously. Because SPL is logarithmic, we must convert back to absolute intensities, sum, and re-log.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert dB back to linear pressures / intensities:**

For a sound wave in air, intensity is proportional to $p_{rms}^{\,2}$. Hence we can write:

$$p_{rms}^{\,2} \propto 10^{L/10}$$

**Step 2 — Define the intensities of each source:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &\propto 10^{L_1/10} \\[6pt]
I_2 &\propto 10^{L_2/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the intensities (uncorrelated sources add in power):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{tot} &= I_1 + I_2 \\[6pt]
&\propto 10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert total intensity back to dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{tot} &= 10 \log_{10}(I_{tot}) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the louder source (assume $L_1 \geq L_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{tot} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[10^{L_1/10}\!\left(1 + \frac{10^{L_2/10}}{10^{L_1/10}}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + 10^{(L_2 - L_1)/10}\right] \\[6pt]
&= L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + 10^{(L_2 - L_1)/10}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L_2 \le L_1$, the exponent $(L_2 - L_1)/10$ is negative, and the bracketed term is between $1$ and $2$.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An engine radiates $L_1 = 76\ \text{dB}$ and a tyre–road interaction radiates $L_2 = 73\ \text{dB}$ simultaneously at the same microphone. Find the combined SPL.

**Step 1 — Difference of levels:**

$$\Delta L = L_2 - L_1 = 73 - 76 = -3\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the bracketed term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{(\Delta L/10)} &= 10^{-0.3} = 0.5012
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L_{tot}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{tot} &= 76 + 10 \log_{10}(1 + 0.5012) \\[6pt]
&= 76 + 10 \log_{10}(1.5012) \\[6pt]
&= 76 + 10 \times 0.1763 \\[6pt]
&= 76 + 1.763 \\[6pt]
&= 77.76\ \text{dB} \approx 77.8\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Combined SPL $\approx$ **$77.8\ \text{dB}$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification by rule of thumb:** A $3\ \text{dB}$ difference yields an addition of $\approx +1.76\ \text{dB}$, exactly matching our analytical result. ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Standard Selection Decision Flow

The following matrix captures the KTU-recommended logic for selecting the correct automotive noise standard for a given engineering problem:

| Scenario | Standard Recommended | What it Measures | Key Parameter |
|----------|----------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Vehicle being homologated for India/Europe | **ECE R51 / AIS-018** | Pass-by noise | $L_{AFmax}$ at $7.5\ \text{m}$ |
| Quick workshop check of exhaust | **ISO 5130 / SAE J1477** | Stationary exhaust noise | SPL at $0.5\ \text{m}$ |
| Cabin comfort assessment | **ISO 5128 / NC curves** | Interior SPL | Octave-band SPL at ear |
| Comparing cabin across vehicle models | **NCB / RC Mark II** | Interior rating | NCB rating + SIL |
| EV low-speed alert testing | **UN R138 / ISO 16254** | AVAS tonal content | Frequency + SPL envelope |
| Heavy vehicle homologation | **ECE R51 Rev.3** (with additional test conditions) | Pass-by + stationary | $L_{urban}$, $L_{wot}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Automotive Noise Architecture

The diagram below maps the hierarchical architecture of automotive noise criteria: sources → propagation paths → measurement standards → rating scales.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AUTOMOTIVE NOISE CRITERIA SYSTEM"] --> B["EXTERIOR NOISE"]
    A --> C["INTERIOR NOISE"]

    B --> B1["Power-Train"]
    B --> B2["Tyre-Road Interaction"]
    B --> B3["Aerodynamic Wind Noise"]
    B --> B4["Auxiliary Systems HVAC"]

    B1 --> B1a["Engine Combustion"]
    B1 --> B1b["Exhaust Radiated"]
    B1 --> B1c["Transmission Gear"]

    B --> STD1["Pass-By Standards"]
    STD1 --> STD1a["ISO 362-1"]
    STD1 --> STD1b["ECE R51"]

    B --> STD2["Stationary Standards"]
    STD2 --> STD2a["ISO 5130"]
    STD2 --> STD2b["SAE J1477"]
    STD2 --> STD2c["AIS-019 India"]

    C --> C1["Structural Born Path"]
    C --> C2["Airborne Path"]
    C --> C3["Side Window Transmission"]

    C --> RAT1["Interior Rating"]
    RAT1 --> RAT1a["NC Curves"]
    RAT1 --> RAT1b["NCB Curves"]

    C --> STD3["Measurement Standards"]

    A --> UNITS["Core Metrics"]
    UNITS --> U1["SPL dB"]

    UNITS --> U3["dBA A-Weighted"]

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Pass-By Noise Measurement Procedure (ISO 362 / ECE R51)

This flowchart captures the standardized procedure used in vehicle homologation pass-by noise testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Test Setup"] --> S2["Prepare Test Track Asphalt Surface"]
    S2 --> S3["Calibrate Sound Level Meter IEC 61672"]
    S3 --> S4["Place Microphones 7.5 m from Track Centerline"]
    S4 --> S5["Microphone Height 1.2 m Above Ground"]
    S5 --> S6["Define PP Line Microphone Line"]
    S6 --> S7["Warm Up Engine to Normal Operating Temp"]
    S7 --> S8["Accelerate Vehicle from 50 km/h Using Wide Open Throttle"]

    S10 -- Pass --> S11["Record Lurban and Lwot Values"]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Interior Cabin NC Rating Procedure

```mermaid

    P4 --> P5{"Step 5 Does Spectrum Cross Any NC Curve"}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6["NC Rating is Lowest Curve Touched"]

    P6 --> P8["Report NCB Value and SIL for Full Evaluation"]
    P7 --> P8
```
