$$m g h + m g \delta_{dyn} = \tfrac{1}{2} k \delta_{dyn}^{2}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The striker's potential energy has two contributions — the free-fall height $h$ (above the impact contact point) and the extra distance $\delta_{dyn}$ that it travels *into* the wheel after first contact, because the wheel is springy.

**Step 2.3 — Recognize the Dynamic Peak Force**

At the maximum dynamic deflection, the spring force equals the dynamic force (the striker has momentarily come to rest):

$$F_{dyn} = k \delta_{dyn}$$

Therefore, $\delta_{dyn} = F_{dyn}/k$.

**Step 2.4 — Substitute into the Energy Equation**

$$m g h + m g \cdot \dfrac{F_{dyn}}{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} k \left(\dfrac{F_{dyn}}{k}\right)^{2}$$

Recall $m g = F_{st}$ and $F_{st}/k = \delta_{st}$:

$$F_{st} h + F_{st} \cdot \dfrac{F_{dyn}}{k} = \dfrac{F_{dyn}^{2}}{2 k}$$

Multiply through by $2k$:

$$2 k F_{st} h + 2 F_{st} F_{dyn} = F_{dyn}^{2}$$

**Step 2.5 — Solve the Quadratic**

Rearrange into standard form ($F_{dyn}$ as the unknown):

$$F_{dyn}^{2} - 2 F_{st} F_{dyn} - 2 k F_{st} h = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a=1$, $b=-2F_{st}$, $c=-2kF_{st}h$:

$$F_{dyn} = F_{st} \pm \sqrt{F_{st}^{2} + 2 k F_{st} h}$$

Taking the positive root (force must be positive) and substituting $k F_{st} = F_{st}^{2}/\delta_{st}$:

$$F_{dyn} = F_{st} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 h}{\delta_{st}}} \right]$$

**Step 2.6 — Define the Dynamic Load Factor**

$$\boxed{DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 h}{\delta_{st}}}}$$

> **Physical meaning:** If $h \to 0$ (no drop), $DLF \to 2$ (just the static weight plus a sudden-release impulse). If $h \gg \delta_{st}$, the term under the square root dominates, and $DLF \approx \sqrt{2h/\delta_{st}}$ — meaning a *very stiff* wheel (small $\delta_{st}$) experiences a *very large* dynamic force. This is why compliant wheels survive impact better than rigid ones.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percentage Pressure Loss as the Failure Metric

**Step 3.1 — Initial Inflation**

The wheel-tyre assembly is inflated to the manufacturer's recommended cold inflation pressure:

$$P_{0} = 220 \text{ kPa (typical passenger car)}$$

**Step 3.2 — Post-Impact Pressure Reading**

After the impact event and a specified dwell time (e.g., 1 minute per SAE J175), the pressure is re-measured:

$$P_{f} = P_{0} - \Delta P$$

**Step 3.3 — Compute Percentage Loss**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \dfrac{P_{0} - P_{f}}{P_{0}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Step 3.4 — Apply the Pass/Fail Threshold**

The pass criterion from SAE J175 is:

$$\Delta P_{\%} \le 5\,\%$$

Therefore the maximum allowable final pressure is:

$$P_{f,min} = 0.95 \cdot P_{0}$$

> For $P_0 = 220$ kPa, the wheel must retain at least $P_f = 209$ kPa after impact. Any reading below 209 kPa → failure.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Test Parameters vs. Performance Outcome

| Test Variant | Striker Mass (kg) | Drop Height (mm) | Impact Energy (J) | Pass Criterion | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| SAE J175 — Cold | 12.5 | 150 | 18.4 | $\Delta P \le 5\,\%$ | Passenger car (P-metric) |
| SAE J175 — Cold | 25.0 | 230 | 56.4 | $\Delta P \le 5\,\%$ | Light truck (LT-metric) |
| ECE R30 Annex 5 | 12.0 | 200 | 23.5 | No rupture, $\Delta P \le 10\,\%/\text{min}$ | European type-approval |
| FMVSS 109 S5.2 | 12.0 | 230 | 27.1 | No air loss above 7 kPa | US passenger |
| IS 1567 | 15.0 | 200 | 29.4 | Visual integrity | Indian passenger car |

**Energy calculation verification for the SAE J175 light-truck row** (using Formula 1):

$$E = 25.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.230 = 56.4 \text{ J}$$

This matches the table entry, confirming the energy formula and the drop height/mass values cited by the standard.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Impact Test Architecture

The test rig is a mechanical-optical-pneumatic system. The block diagram below maps the functional flow from the striker release mechanism to the data-acquisition and pass/fail decision logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply and Safety Interlock] --> B[Drop Tower Frame]
    B --> C[Electromagnetic Striker Release Clamp]
    C --> D[Striker with Hemispherical Tip]
    D --> E[Pneumatic Tyre and Wheel Assembly Mounted on Test Fixture]
    E --> F[Inflation Pressure Regulator and Gauge]
    F --> G[Digital Pressure Transducer]
    G --> H[Data Acquisition System]
    E --> I[High Speed Camera for Impact Visualization]
    I --> H
    H --> J[Pass or Fail Decision Module]
    J --> K[Pass: No Visible Crack, Pressure Loss within Limit]
    J --> L[Fail: Crack, Rupture, or Pressure Loss Exceeds Threshold]
    E --> M[Test Temperature Chamber 21C or 38C]
    M --> E
    H --> N[Report Generation and Archival]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Sequential Test Procedure

The following diagram captures the **chronological execution sequence** that a laboratory technician follows. KTU students are frequently asked to list these steps in Part-A questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Receive Tyre and Wheel Assembly] --> P2[Step 2: Visual Inspection for Pre-existing Defects]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Mount Assembly on Test Fixture]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Inflate to Rated Pressure P0]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Condition at 21C for 3 Hours]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Verify Pressure and Re-adjust to P0]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Position Striker at Tread Shoulder]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Set Drop Height h per Standard]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Release Striker from Electromagnetic Clamp]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Capture Impact Event on High Speed Camera]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Hold Inflation for Specified Dwell Time 1 minute]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Re-measure Pressure Pf]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13: Inspect for Cracks and Deformation]
    P13 --> P14{Step 14: Apply Pass or Fail Criteria}
    P14 --> P15[Step 15a: PASS: No Crack, DeltaP within Limit]
    P14 --> P16[Step 15b: FAIL: Crack or DeltaP Exceeds Threshold]
    P15 --> P17[Step 16: Document and Archive Test Report]
    P16 --> P17
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Impact Test Geometry

The impact geometry is a critical concept. The diagram below is rendered as a labelled side-view block to highlight the spatial relationship between the striker, the wheel centre plane, and the impact point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Striker Release Mechanism] --> G2[Drop Height h]
    G2 --> G3[Striker Mass m]
    G3 --> G4[Impact Point at Tread Shoulder]
    G4 --> G5[Wheel Centre Plane]
    G5 --> G6[Test Fixture Base Plate]
    G4 --> G7[Tyre Bead Seat on Rim Flange]
    G4 --> G8[Critical Stress Region: Sidewall Shoulder]
    G4 --> G9[Striker Tip Radius 12.7 mm Typical]
```

**Spatial notes for the student:**

- The drop height $h$ is measured from the **lowest point of the striker tip** to the **point of first contact** on the tyre shoulder.
- The impact point is typically located **75 – 100 mm inboard** of the centre line of the tread, on the shoulder.
- The striker tip is **hemispherical with radius 12.7 mm** to avoid puncturing the tyre — the test is about *structural integrity*, not *puncture resistance* (that is the plunger energy test).

## 4.4 Cause-and-Effect Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[High Drop Height h] --> R1[Large Kinetic Energy]
    C2[Large Striker Mass m] --> R1
    R1 --> E1[High Peak Impact Force]
    E1 --> E2[High Bending Stress at Rim Flange]
    E2 --> E3[Possible Crack Initiation]
    E3 --> E4[Air Leakage through Crack]
    E4 --> E5[Pressure Loss above 5 percent]
    E5 --> F1[FAIL Outcome]
    C3[Compliant Wheel Structure] --> M1[High Static Deflection]
    M1 --> M2[Low Dynamic Load Factor]
    M2 --> M3[Reduced Peak Force]
    M3 --> P1[PASS Outcome]
    C4[High Inflation Pressure] --> M3
    C5[Warm Test Temperature 38C] --> M4[Reduced Tyre Stiffness]
    M4 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, OEAUT831]`

**Define the Road Hazard Impact Test for a pneumatic tyre-wheel assembly. Mention the relevant Indian and international standards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Road Hazard Impact Test (RHIT)** is a standardized single-event destructive test that evaluates the ability of an inflated pneumatic tyre mounted on its wheel (rim) to withstand a sudden, high-energy transverse impact at the tread shoulder, simulating a real-world collision with a road obstacle such as a kerb or pothole edge.

The test is governed by the following standards:

- **SAE J175** – Impact Test for Tubeless Pneumatic Tyres (passenger car, primary reference)
- **SAE J198** – Older version, now superseded for some categories
- **IS 1567 / IS 1100** – Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) for automotive pneumatic tyres
- **ECE Regulation 30** – European type-approval for passenger car tyres
- **FMVSS 109 / FMVSS 110** – U.S. Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards

The test outcome is a binary **pass/fail** decision based on pressure retention and absence of visible fracture. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 4, OEAUT831]`

**State the formula for impact energy of the striker and explain why the test striker is positioned to strike the tread shoulder rather than the centre of the tread.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The impact energy of the striker is given by the gravitational potential energy at the release height:

$$E = m g h$$

where $m$ is the striker mass (kg), $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²), and $h$ is the drop height (m). Equivalently, the kinetic energy at the moment of impact is:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}, \quad v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

**Why the tread shoulder is targeted (2 of 3 marks):**

1. The tread shoulder is the **stress concentration point** during a real-world kerb-strike scenario, where the sidewall and the tread meet.
2. The **rim flange and bead seat** experience the maximum bending moment at this location.
3. Striking the centre of the tread would produce a primarily radial load — a less severe and less representative loading case.
4. Real-world sidewall blowouts almost always originate at the shoulder region, so this location is the **most critical** for failure initiation. *(3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, OEAUT831, 14 Marks]`

**"With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the Road Hazard Impact Test procedure for a passenger car tyre-wheel assembly as per SAE J175. Clearly state the pass and fail criteria."**

#### (a) **Test Procedure and Schematic** (7 marks)

The procedure as per **SAE J175** is executed in the following sequence:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Select a tubeless pneumatic tyre of the specified size and mount it on its recommended wheel/rim. The assembly must be free from manufacturing defects; pre-test visual inspection is mandatory. *(1 mark)*

2. **Inflation:** Inflate the assembly to the manufacturer's recommended cold inflation pressure, typically **220 kPa** for a P-metric passenger tyre. Record this as $P_0$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Conditioning:** Place the inflated assembly in a temperature-controlled chamber at **21 °C ± 11 °C** for a minimum of **3 hours** to ensure thermal equilibrium of the air charge and the rubber compounds. *(1 mark)*

4. **Mounting on Fixture:** Clamp the wheel-tyre assembly to a rigid test fixture using the standard lug-nut pattern. The fixture must replicate the in-vehicle mounting geometry so that the rim flange restraint is realistic. *(1 mark)*

5. **Striker Positioning:** Position a **12.5 kg hemispherical-tipped striker** at a drop height of **150 mm** above the **tread shoulder**, with the impact line horizontal to the wheel centre plane. *(1 mark)*

6. **Impact Event:** Release the striker by de-energising the electromagnetic clamp. The striker falls under gravity and strikes the shoulder. A high-speed camera records the event. *(1 mark)*

7. **Post-Impact Dwell and Measurement:** Hold the inflated assembly at test temperature for **1 minute**, then re-measure the inflation pressure and record it as $P_f$. Inspect the wheel and tyre visually and dimensionally. *(1 mark)*

#### (b) **Pass and Fail Criteria** (7 marks)

**Pass Criteria (a wheel passes only if ALL of the following are satisfied):** *(3.5 marks)*

1. **Pressure retention:** The pressure loss must satisfy $\Delta P_{\%} = \dfrac{P_0 - P_f}{P_0} \times 100\,\% \le 5\,\%$.
2. **No visible crack or fracture** in the rim (disc or flange) when inspected at 5× magnification.
3. **No detachment** of the tyre from the rim bead seat.
4. **No permanent deformation** of the rim flange exceeding the dimensional tolerance (typically **1.0 mm** radial run-out increase).
5. **No rupture** of the tyre carcass visible from the exterior.

**Fail Criteria (the assembly fails if ANY of the following occur):** *(3.5 marks)*

1. Pressure loss $\Delta P_{\%} > 5\,\%$ within the 1-minute dwell.
2. Any visible crack, fracture, or split in the rim, bead seat, or tyre sidewall.
3. Complete or partial detachment of the tyre bead from the rim flange.
4. Air leakage audible (hissing) for more than **5 seconds** post-impact.
5. Permanent rim deformation that prevents the wheel from being re-balanced on a vehicle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Impact Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Impact Test with Plunger Test:** Students frequently mix up the impact test (single-event high-energy transverse load) with the plunger energy test (quasi-static puncture resistance). The impact test does **not** measure puncture resistance.
> 2. **Forgetting the Temperature Condition:** Many students omit the 21 °C ± 11 °C conditioning step. This is a **1-mark** deduction in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Writing only the pass criteria, not the fail criteria:** Both lists must be specified. The examiner allocates separate marks for each.
> 4. **Forgetting the units of drop height:** Drop height is in **mm** in the standard, not metres. A drop height of "150" without units is marked as a notational error.
> 5. **Using g = 10 m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s²:** Acceptable in KTU for integer arithmetic, but if the standard value is used in the question, retain **9.81**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – December 2022, Module 4, OEAUT831, 14 Marks]`

**"A passenger car tyre-wheel assembly is to be impact-tested as per SAE J175. The striker mass is 12.5 kg and the drop height is 150 mm. Compute: (a) the impact energy and the striker velocity at the moment of impact, and (b) the peak impact force if the contact duration is 8 ms. Comment on whether the assembly is likely to survive if the structural stiffness of the wheel is 350 kN/m."**

#### (a) **Impact Energy and Striker Velocity** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert drop height to SI units:**

$$h = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute impact energy using the potential energy formula:**

$$E_{impact} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$E_{impact} = 12.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.150$$

$$E_{impact} = 18.39 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the striker velocity using conservation of energy:**

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.150}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2.943}$$

$$v = 1.715 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-verify using the kinetic energy formula:**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 12.5 \times (1.715)^{2}$$

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 12.5 \times 2.943 = 18.39 \text{ J} \quad \checkmark$$

The values agree, confirming the calculation. *(7 marks)*

#### (b) **Peak Impact Force and Survival Assessment** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Estimate the peak impact force using impulse-momentum:**

The impulse delivered by the wheel on the striker equals the change in striker momentum:

$$F_{peak} \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot v$$

$$F_{peak} = \dfrac{m \cdot v}{\Delta t}$$

Convert $\Delta t = 8 \text{ ms} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$:

$$F_{peak} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 1.715}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$F_{peak} = \dfrac{21.44}{0.008}$$

$$F_{peak} = 2680 \text{ N} \approx 2.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Dynamic Load Factor (DLF):**

First, compute the static deflection of the wheel under the striker's weight:

$$\delta_{st} = \dfrac{m g}{k} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 9.81}{350 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \dfrac{122.6}{350{,}000} = 3.50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.350 \text{ mm}$$

Now substitute into the DLF formula:

$$DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 h}{\delta_{st}}}$$

$$DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 \times 0.150}{0.000350}}$$

$$DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 857.14}$$

$$DLF = 1 + \sqrt{858.14} = 1 + 29.29$$

$$DLF = 30.29$$

**Step 3 — Compute the dynamic peak force:**

$$F_{dyn} = DLF \cdot W_{st} = 30.29 \times (12.5 \times 9.81)$$

$$F_{dyn} = 30.29 \times 122.6$$

$$F_{dyn} = 3714 \text{ N} \approx 3.71 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the impulse method (1.43× lower estimate):**

The impulse method gave **2.68 kN**; the energy-equivalence method gave **3.71 kN**. The energy method is **more conservative** because it implicitly assumes the striker comes to a complete stop (coefficient of restitution $e = 0$). The impulse method assumed a bounce-back velocity; if we had set $e = 0$:

$$F_{peak, plastic} = \dfrac{m (v - 0)}{\Delta t} = 2680 \text{ N}$$

And if we set $e = 0.5$ (partial elastic rebound), the actual force is bracketed between these two estimates. For KTU marking purposes, the energy method answer of **3.71 kN** is the recommended one.

**Step 5 — Survival Comment:**

A peak dynamic force of **3.71 kN** acting on a 350 kN/m wheel produces a maximum dynamic deflection of:

$$\delta_{dyn} = \dfrac{F_{dyn}}{k} = \dfrac{3714}{350{,}000} = 0.0106 \text{ m} = 10.6 \text{ mm}$$

A 10.6 mm localised deflection is within the elastic range of a typical steel wheel (yield occurs at ~15–20 mm). The wheel will **likely survive** (no permanent deformation), provided there are no pre-existing stress raisers such as corrosion pits or weld defects. The corresponding maximum bending stress at the rim flange is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{F_{dyn}}{A_{flange}} + \dfrac{M c}{I_{flange}}$$

For a typical passenger rim flange ($A \approx 60 \text{ mm}^2$, $I \approx 250 \text{ mm}^4$, $c \approx 3 \text{ mm}$), the stress is on the order of **150 – 250 MPa**, which is below the yield strength of aluminium alloy rims (~200 MPa) and well below that of steel rims (~350 MPa). The assembly is therefore expected to **pass** the impact test. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Impact Test**

- **One-line definition:** A single-event, high-energy transverse impact test on a tyre-wheel assembly to validate structural integrity and pressure retention.
- **Primary standard:** **SAE J175** (current), **SAE J198** (legacy).
- **Striker:** Hemispherical tip, radius **12.7 mm**.
- **Typical passenger car parameters:** $m = 12.5$ kg, $h = 150$ mm, $E = 18.4$ J.
- **Typical light truck parameters:** $m = 25$ kg, $h = 230$ mm, $E = 56.4$ J.
- **Impact location:** **Tread shoulder** (not tread centre) — the stress concentration point.
- **Impact angle:** Horizontal striker, vertical drop; impact perpendicular to wheel centre plane.
- **Test temperatures:** Cold at **21 °C ± 11 °C**; warm at **38 °C ± 3 °C** (warm = harsher).
- **Dwell time:** **1 minute** post-impact for pressure re-measurement (SAE J175).
- **Pass threshold:** Pressure loss $\le$ **5 %** of $P_0$.
- **Other pass conditions:** No visible crack, no bead unseat, no permanent rim deformation > **1.0 mm**.
- **Impact energy formula:** $E = m g h$ (gravitational PE) and $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ (KE), with $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.
- **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF):** $DLF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}$ — increases with drop height and decreases with structural compliance.
- **Peak force estimate (impulse method):** $F_{peak} = \dfrac{m v}{\Delta t}$ — typical $\Delta t = 5$–$15$ ms.
- **Coefficient of restitution for tyre-wheel:** $e \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ (partially elastic).
- **Standard gravity:** Use **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the question explicitly states otherwise.
- **Common confusion:** Do not mix up with the **plunger energy test** (puncture), **cornering fatigue test** (cyclic), or **high-speed test** (centrifugal). Each is a separate qualification.
- **Indian equivalent:** **IS 1567** and **AIS-044** for automotive wheels.
- **Failure mechanism:** Cracks initiate at the **rim flange** and the **sidewall bead area**, propagating radially outward.
- **Why warm test is harsher:** Rubber modulus drops with temperature → tyre absorbs less energy → more energy transmitted to the rim.
- **Exam trick:** The drop height is given in **mm** in standards but must be converted to **m** for SI energy calculations. Always include the unit conversion step.
- **Numerical answer format in KTU:** Show the formula, substitute values with units, then box the final numerical answer with its unit (e.g., **$E = 18.4$ J**).
- **Diagram requirement:** Any 14-mark question on this topic **must** include a labelled schematic of the test rig. Skipping the diagram incurs a **2-mark** deduction in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bumpers (Types of Tests, Pendulum Test, Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure, Performance Criteria)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bumpers (Types of Tests, Pendulum Test, Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure, Performance Criteria) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition of a Vehicle Bumper A **bumper** is a structural energy-absorbing system fitted to the front and rear of a motor vehicle, designed to absorb impact energy during low-speed collisions (typically up to **4 km/h** to **8 km/h** for parking-type impacts and up to **15 km/h** for regulatory barrier tests), thereby minimizing damage to the vehicle's primary structure, lighting systems, and occupant safety features. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (BIS / AIS-006 / ECE-R42 Standard)** > A bumper is defined as a system comprising the bumper face-bar (reinforcement), energy absorber (foam/honeycomb), bumper fascia (cover), and the supporting brackets. Its primary function is *energy management* through controlled plastic deformation, NOT complete rebound. The bumper must prevent damage to safety-related systems such as headlamps, fuel system, hood latches, and cooling system under prescribed test conditions. The bumper subsystem is governed by the following regulatory frameworks in the Indian / Global context: - **AIS-006 (Automotive Industry Standards – India)** - **ECE R42 (Economic Commission for Europe Regulation No. 42)** - **FMVSS Part 581 (U.S. Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard)** ## 1.2 Components of a Modern Bumper System | Sub-Component | Functional Role | Typical Material | |---|---|---| | **Bumper Fascia (Cover)** | Aesthetic outer skin, UV protection | Thermoplastic olefin (TPO), Polypropylene | | **Energy Absorber** | Controlled plastic deformation | EPP foam, EPP/EPS honeycomb, PUR foam | | **Reinforcement Bar (Face Bar)** | Load distribution & stiffness | Rolled steel, Aluminium, GMT composite | | **Bumper Brackets** | Load transfer to body structure | High-strength steel stampings | | **Pedestrian Protection Pad (Lower)** | Leg impact energy dissipation | PUR foam / EPP | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Boxer's Glove and the Crash Mat** > Imagine a boxer (your car) punching a heavy bag (a wall or another car). The boxer wears a thick, padded glove (the bumper). When the punch lands, the glove *deforms permanently*, absorbing the kinetic energy of the strike. The boxer's hand and wrist (vehicle structure, headlights, cooling system) remain undamaged. A rigid steel plate bolted directly to the boxer's hand (a bumper with no absorber) would shatter his wrist on impact. This is precisely why a modern bumper is NOT designed to be rigid — it is designed to *sacrifice itself plastically* to protect expensive and safety-critical systems behind it. ### 1.4 Why Bumpers Are Critical for Vehicle Performance 1. **Low-Speed Crashworthiness:** Reduce repair costs in 4–8 km/h parking maneuvers. 2. **Compatibility with Safety Systems:** Prevent secondary damage to SRS sensors, radar, and ADAS cameras mounted in the grille. 3. **Pedestrian Safety:** Softer lower bumper reduces femur fracture risk in pedestrian impacts (compliance with AIS-100 / GTR-9). 4. **Insurance Cost Reduction:** Lower Insurance Repair Ratings (IRR) due to non-structural damage. 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory certification for vehicle type approval in India (AIS-006) and Europe (ECE R42). ## 1.5 Types of Bumper Tests (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!NOTE] > **Bumper Tests are broadly classified into three categories per KTU / AIS-006 / ECE R42:** 1. **Pendulum Impact Test** – Pendulum striker is swung to impact a stationary bumper mounted on a rigid fixture. 2. **Fixed Collision Barrier Test** – The complete vehicle is propelled (towed / driven) into a fixed rigid barrier. 3. **Hydraulic Ram / Quasi-Static Crush Test** – A hydraulic actuator pushes the bumper at constant velocity to obtain force-deflection curve. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pendulum Test — Operating Principle The pendulum test simulates a low-speed corner impact where one vehicle strikes another at an angle. A calibrated pendulum of known mass $m_p$ and effective length $L$ is raised to a prescribed height $h_1$ and released to impact the bumper at its **lowest point (cornea point)**. The pendulum swings through the bumper, and the residual swing height $h_2$ is recorded. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Pendulum Test - The pendulum mass is released from a known height → potential energy is converted to kinetic energy at the bottom of the swing. - At impact, a portion of KE is absorbed by the bumper as plastic deformation energy. - The pendulum continues forward with reduced velocity (rebound velocity if any) and rises to a residual height $h_2$. - The difference in height $(h_1 - h_2)$ represents the *energy absorbed* by the bumper. ### 2.1.2 Test Geometry (AIS-006 / ECE R42) - Pendulum mass: **$m_p$ = 1100 kg (or scaled equivalent of 1500 kg full vehicle equivalent)** - Striker head radius: **$r$ = 220 mm** for passenger cars (per ECE R42 Annex IV). - Impact angle: Pendulum is dropped such that velocity at impact = **2.5 m/s** (≈ 9 km/h) for corner impact and **4 km/h** for the central impact in some standards. - Striker shape: Spherical or cylindrical (per ECE R42 §5.1.1). ## 2.2 Fixed Collision Barrier Test — Operating Principle The complete vehicle (with dummy masses representing occupants OR curb weight) is towed or driven at a prescribed velocity into a **rigid, non-deformable barrier** (concrete block with face perpendicular to vehicle longitudinal axis, mass > 70 tonnes per AIS-006). The barrier is sometimes fitted with a deformable element (per ECE R42 §5.2) to simulate vehicle-to-vehicle compatibility. ### 2.2.1 Test Conditions - **Test speed:** 4 km/h (1.11 m/s) for AIS-006 (parking impact) - **Approach angle:** 0° (head-on) and 60° corner impact per ECE R42 - **Barrier type:** Rigid concrete or honeycomb aluminium deformable element - **Vehicle condition:** Loaded to curb weight + 75 kg dummy on driver seat ## 2.3 Performance Criteria (Pass/Fail Conditions) The bumper is considered to *PASS* the regulatory test if, after impact: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Performance Criteria per AIS-006 / ECE R42 (Mandatory for Type Approval)** > 1. No damage to headlamps, tail lamps, indicators, or reflectors such that their luminous function is impaired. > 2. No failure of hood, trunk, or tailgate latches; doors must remain operable normally. > 3. No rupture, leakage, or disconnection of the fuel system. > 4. No severance or damage to the steering, braking, or exhaust systems. > 5. Maximum bumper deflection must be within the geometric envelope defined by the manufacturer. > 6. Pedestrian protection lower legform impactor result (force ≤ **6.0 kN** for upper tibia, **2.5 kN** for knee) per AIS-100. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pendulum Impact Velocity | $v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 g (h_1 - 0)}$ | m/s | Velocity at bottom of swing | | 2 | Initial Pendulum KE | $KE_1 = m_p \cdot g \cdot h_1$ | J | Potential energy at release | | 3 | Final Pendulum KE | $KE_2 = m_p \cdot g \cdot h_2$ | J | Residual energy after impact | | 4 | Energy Absorbed by Bumper | $E_{abs} = m_p g (h_1 - h_2)$ | J | Plastic deformation energy | | 5 | Coefficient of Restitution | $e = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h_2}}{\sqrt{2 g h_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}}$ | dimensionless | Rebound ratio (ideally 0) | | 6 | Crush Energy (Quasi-Static) | $E_{crush} = \int_0^{\delta_{max}} F(\delta) \, d\delta$ | J | Area under F-d curve | | 7 | Average Crush Force | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{\delta_{max}}$ | N | Useful for material selection | | 8 | Barrier Test Kinetic Energy | $KE_{vehicle} = \dfrac{1}{2} m_v v^2$ | J | KE of vehicle at impact | | 9 | Equivalent Pendulum Mass for $m_v$ | $m_{eq} = \dfrac{m_v}{2} \cdot \sin^2\theta$ | kg | Corner impact correction | | 10 | Impact Force Estimate | $F_{avg} = \dfrac{m_p \cdot v_{imp}}{t_c}$ | N | Using contact time $t_c$ | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > - $m_p$ = pendulum mass (kg) > - $g$ = 9.81 m/s² > - $h_1, h_2$ = initial and residual swing heights (m) > - $m_v$ = vehicle mass (kg) > - $v$ = test velocity (m/s) > - $\delta_{max}$ = maximum bumper deflection (m) > - $F(\delta)$ = force-deflection function from crush test > - $\theta$ = angle between impact direction and vehicle longitudinal axis (corner test) > - $t_c$ = effective contact time (s) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **OEM Design Validation:** Force-deflection curves obtained from pendulum tests are inputs to CAE (LS-DYNA, PAM-CRASH) bumper crash simulations. - **Material Selection:** Crush energy per unit mass (specific energy absorption, SEA, kJ/kg) determines whether to use EPP foam, aluminium honeycomb, or steel reinforcement. - **Insurance Engineering:** Lower repair costs achieved by designing *modular* bumper systems with bolt-on fascias. - **Regulatory Type Approval:** Without passing AIS-006 / ECE R42, a vehicle cannot be sold in India / Europe. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pendulum Impact Velocity from Energy Conservation **Problem Statement:** A pendulum of mass $m_p$ = 1100 kg is raised to a height $h_1$ = 1.2 m above its rest position. It is released to impact a bumper. Find the impact velocity at the bottom of the swing. **Derivation:** At height $h_1$, the pendulum is momentarily at rest. All energy is potential. $$ PE_1 = m_p \cdot g \cdot h_1 $$ At the bottom of the swing (impact instant), height = 0, and all PE is converted to KE (assuming no air drag). $$ PE_1 = KE_{imp} = \frac{1}{2} m_p \cdot v_{imp}^2 $$ Equating both: $$ m_p \cdot g \cdot h_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_p \cdot v_{imp}^2 $$ Cancel $m_p$ from both sides: $$ g \cdot h_1 = \frac{1}{2} v_{imp}^2 $$ Solving for $v_{imp}$: $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 g h_1} $$ **Numerical substitution** ($g = 9.81$ m/s², $h_1 = 1.2$ m): $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2} $$ $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{23.544} $$ $$ \boxed{v_{imp} = 4.852 \text{ m/s} \quad (\approx 17.47 \text{ km/h})} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the velocity at which the striker impacts the bumper. Note that 17.47 km/h is *higher* than the 4 km/h AIS-006 standard because the pendulum equivalent test mass is much smaller than the full vehicle (1:1 mass scaling is not done; rather, kinetic energy scaling is used). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Absorbed by the Bumper **Problem Statement:** Using the same pendulum (m_p = 1100 kg, h₁ = 1.2 m), after striking the bumper, the pendulum rises to a residual height $h_2$ = 0.35 m. Find the energy absorbed by the bumper. **Derivation:** Initial total energy (just before release, at rest at $h_1$): $$ E_1 = m_p \cdot g \cdot h_1 = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2 = 12949.2 \text{ J} $$ Residual energy (just after impact, at maximum rebound height $h_2$): $$ E_2 = m_p \cdot g \cdot h_2 = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.35 = 3776.85 \text{ J} $$ By conservation of energy, the energy absorbed by the bumper equals the difference: $$ E_{abs} = E_1 - E_2 = m_p g (h_1 - h_2) $$ $$ E_{abs} = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1.2 - 0.35) $$ $$ E_{abs} = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.85 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{abs} = 9172.35 \text{ J} \approx 9.17 \text{ kJ}} $$ **Cross-verification using velocities:** $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.2} = 4.852 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ v_{rebound} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.35} = 2.621 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2} m_p (v_{imp}^2 - v_{rebound}^2) $$ $$ E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1100 \cdot (4.852^2 - 2.621^2) $$ $$ E_{abs} = 550 \cdot (23.542 - 6.870) = 550 \cdot 16.672 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{abs} = 9169.6 \text{ J}} \quad \text{(matches within rounding error)} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coefficient of Restitution for the Bumper System **Definition:** The coefficient of restitution $e$ is the ratio of relative velocity of separation to relative velocity of approach. For a bumper test, it is computed from rebound vs. impact pendulum velocity. $$ e = \frac{v_{rebound}}{v_{imp}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 g h_2}}{\sqrt{2 g h_1}} $$ Simplifying: $$ \boxed{e = \sqrt{\frac{h_2}{h_1}}} $$ **Numerical substitution:** $$ e = \sqrt{\frac{0.35}{1.2}} = \sqrt{0.2917} $$ $$ \boxed{e = 0.5401} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A perfect elastic bumper would have $e = 1$ (rebounds to same height), and a perfectly plastic bumper would have $e = 0$ (no rebound, all energy absorbed). The actual value of **0.54 indicates partial elastic recovery** — a substantial portion of energy is absorbed plastically (good), but some elastic recoil is present (typical of steel + EPP foam combinations). For a *fully optimized* energy-absorbing bumper, $e$ is targeted between 0.2 and 0.4. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalent Pendulum Mass for Corner Impact **Problem Statement:** A 1500 kg car strikes a rigid barrier at 4 km/h at an angle of 60° to its longitudinal axis (corner impact per ECE R42). Find the equivalent pendulum mass that would deliver the same impact energy normal to the bumper face. **Derivation:** The kinetic energy of the vehicle along the direction of impact (perpendicular to bumper face) is: $$ KE_{impact} = \frac{1}{2} m_v (v \sin\theta)^2 $$ For the pendulum to deliver the same energy at the same impact velocity $v$: $$ \frac{1}{2} m_{eq} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_v (v \sin\theta)^2 $$ Cancel $\frac{1}{2} v^2$: $$ m_{eq} = m_v \sin^2\theta $$ **Numerical substitution** ($m_v = 1500$ kg, $\theta = 60°$): $$ m_{eq} = 1500 \cdot \sin^2(60°) = 1500 \cdot (0.866)^2 = 1500 \cdot 0.75 $$ $$ \boxed{m_{eq} = 1125 \text{ kg}} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This is why the regulatory pendulum mass is approximately 1100 kg — it is the effective mass that delivers equivalent energy to a 1500 kg car corner-striking at 4 km/h. The sine-squared rule emerges from the projection of vehicle momentum onto the bumper normal. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Crush Energy from Force-Deflection Curve **Problem Statement:** A hydraulic ram crush test yields the following force-deflection data points for a bumper: $(0, 0), (0.05, 5), (0.10, 12), (0.15, 18), (0.20, 22), (0.25, 24), (0.30, 25)$ kN at deflection values in meters. Calculate the total crush energy absorbed up to 0.30 m using the Trapezoidal Rule. **Derivation (Trapezoidal Numerical Integration):** $$ E_{crush} = \int_0^{0.30} F(\delta) \, d\delta \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{(F_i + F_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (\delta_{i+1} - \delta_i) $$ Substituting values (force in kN, deflection in m, energy in kJ): $$ E_1 = \frac{(0 + 5)}{2} \cdot (0.05 - 0) = 0.125 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ E_2 = \frac{(5 + 12)}{2} \cdot (0.10 - 0.05) = 0.425 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ E_3 = \frac{(12 + 18)}{2} \cdot (0.15 - 0.10) = 0.750 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ E_4 = \frac{(18 + 22)}{2} \cdot (0.20 - 0.15) = 1.000 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ E_5 = \frac{(22 + 24)}{2} \cdot (0.25 - 0.20) = 1.150 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ E_6 = \frac{(24 + 25)}{2} \cdot (0.30 - 0.25) = 1.225 \text{ kJ} $$ Summing all segments: $$ E_{crush} = 0.125 + 0.425 + 0.750 + 1.000 + 1.150 + 1.225 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{crush} = 4.675 \text{ kJ} = 4675 \text{ J}} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This 4.675 kJ of crush energy must exceed the 4 km/h parking impact energy of a typical sedan (≈ 1.2 kJ) with a healthy safety margin. The *average crush force* is $E_{crush} / \delta_{max} = 4675 / 0.30 = 15{,}583$ N ≈ 15.6 kN, indicating a *soft-to-medium* bumper stiffness (low-velocity impact friendly). ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Test Methods | Parameter | Pendulum Test | Fixed Barrier Test | Hydraulic Ram Test | |---|---|---|---| | **Subject under test** | Bumper assembly (fixture-mounted) | Complete vehicle | Bumper assembly (fixture-mounted) | | **Input energy source** | Gravitational PE | Vehicle kinetic energy | Hydraulic actuator | | **Typical test speed** | 2.5–4 m/s | 1.1–4 m/s | Quasi-static (0.5 m/s) | | **Loading type** | Dynamic, point/area | Dynamic, full vehicle inertia | Quasi-static, area | | **Energy range (typical)** | 5–10 kJ | 1–20 kJ | 1–50 kJ | | **Data acquired** | $h_1, h_2$ → $E_{abs}, e$ | Damage inspection, deceleration | Force-deflection curve | | **Standard reference** | ECE R42 §5.1 | ECE R42 §5.2, AIS-006 | OEM internal / SAE J980 | | **Regulatory mandatory?** | Yes (ECE R42) | Yes (AIS-006) | No (developmental) | | **Repeatability** | High | Medium | Very high | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bumper System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Impact Event
Low-Speed Collision] --> B{Bumper System} B --> C[Bumper Fascia
Outer Cover] C --> D[Energy Absorber
EPP Foam or Honeycomb] D --> E[Reinforcement Bar
Steel or Aluminium] E --> F[Mounting Brackets
Body Side Members] F --> G[Vehicle Body-in-White
Front Longitudinal Rail] G --> H[Safety Systems
Headlamps, Fuel, Sensors] H --> I{Performance
Check} I --> J[Pass: No Damage
to Safety Systems] I --> K[Fail: Damage Observed
Repair Cost Triggered] ``` ## 4.2 Pendulum Test Setup — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Set Pendulum Mass
m_p = 1100 kg] --> S2[Raise Pendulum to
Height h_1 = 1.2 m] S2 --> S3[Release Pendulum
Under Gravity] S3 --> S4[Measure Impact
Velocity v_imp] S4 --> S5[Striker Impacts
Bumper Cornea] S5 --> S6[Record Residual
Height h_2] S6 --> S7[Calculate Energy
Absorbed E_abs] S7 --> S8[Calculate
Coefficient e] S8 --> S9[Inspect Bumper
and Safety Systems] S9 --> S10{Performance
Criteria Met?} S10 -- Yes --> S11[Pass] S10 -- No --> S12[Fail
Design Reiteration] ``` ## 4.3 Fixed Collision Barrier Test Setup — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Prepare Vehicle
Curb Weight + 75 kg Driver] --> B2[Tow Vehicle to
Test Speed 4 km/h] B2 --> B3[Impact Rigid Barrier
Mass greater than 70 tonnes] B3 --> B4[Record Impact
Duration and Deceleration] B4 --> B5[Photograph
Damage Profile] B5 --> B6[Measure Permanent
Deformation delta_max] B6 --> B7{All Safety Systems
Functional?} B7 -- Yes --> B8[Pass: Type Approval Granted] B7 -- No --> B9[Fail: Redesign Required] B8 --> B10[Issue Compliance
Certificate per AIS-006] B9 --> B11[Return to Design
Iteration Phase] ``` ## 4.4 Bumper Test Decision Tree (Pass / Fail Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Post-Impact Bumper
Inspection] --> P1{Headlamps
Functional?} P1 -- No --> P2[Fail] P1 -- Yes --> P3{Fuel System
Intact?} P3 -- No --> P2 P3 -- Yes --> P4{Hood and Doors
Operable?} P4 -- No --> P2 P4 -- Yes --> P5{Steering and
Brakes Intact?} P5 -- No --> P2 P5 -- Yes --> P6{Deflection
Within Envelope?} P6 -- No --> P2 P6 -- Yes --> P7[Pass: Bumper
Approved for Production] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a vehicle bumper. List any four components of a modern bumper system.** **Model Answer:** A vehicle bumper is a structural energy-absorbing system fitted to the front and rear of a motor vehicle. Its primary function is to absorb impact energy during low-speed collisions (typically up to 4–8 km/h), thereby protecting the vehicle's safety-critical systems such as headlamps, fuel system, and cooling system. The four main components of a modern bumper system are: 1. **Bumper Fascia (Cover)** — Outer aesthetic skin, typically made of thermoplastic olefin (TPO). 2. **Energy Absorber** — Foam or honeycomb element that deforms plastically to absorb impact energy (EPP foam, PUR foam, aluminium honeycomb). 3. **Reinforcement Bar (Face Bar)** — Stiff structural member that distributes impact load, usually made of rolled steel or aluminium. 4. **Bumper Brackets** — Mounting elements that transfer load from the bumper to the vehicle's side members (body-in-white). ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between pendulum test and fixed collision barrier test for bumpers.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Pendulum Test | Fixed Collision Barrier Test | |---|---|---| | Subject under test | Bumper assembly mounted on fixture | Complete vehicle | | Energy source | Gravitational potential energy of pendulum | Vehicle kinetic energy | | Typical speed | 2.5–4 m/s (≈ 9–14 km/h) | 1.1–4 km/h (AIS-006 standard) | | Striker geometry | Spherical head, 220 mm radius | Full vehicle front or corner | | Data obtained | Residual height, energy absorbed, $e$ | Damage profile, deceleration pulse | | Standard reference | ECE R42 §5.1 | AIS-006, ECE R42 §5.2 | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Explain in detail the **pendulum test procedure** for evaluating a vehicle bumper. Mention the test conditions, equipment, and procedure step-by-step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pendulum of mass 1100 kg is raised to a height of 1.5 m and released. After impacting the bumper, it swings to a residual height of 0.40 m. Calculate (i) the impact velocity, (ii) the energy absorbed by the bumper, and (iii) the coefficient of restitution. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a) — Pendulum Test Procedure:** **1. Equipment & Test Setup:** - A pendulum rig with a calibrated mass (typically 1100 kg equivalent). - Pendulum length such that the impact point coincides with the bumper's geometric center. - A rigid fixture to mount the bumper assembly at the same height as on the vehicle. - Striker head: spherical, 220 mm radius (per ECE R42 Annex IV). - Residual height measurement device (potentiometer, encoder, or laser sensor). **2. Test Conditions:** - Pendulum mass: 1100 kg - Release height $h_1$: 1.2 m (or such that impact velocity = 2.5 m/s for ECE R42) - Impact point: Cornea of the bumper (outermost corner) - Ambient temperature: 20 ± 4°C - Bumper must be preconditioned at this temperature for 4 hours **3. Step-by-Step Procedure:** - **Step 1:** Mount the bumper assembly on a rigid test fixture that simulates the body mounting points of the actual vehicle. - **Step 2:** Set the pendulum release height $h_1$ such that the impact velocity will be the prescribed value. - **Step 3:** Raise the pendulum to height $h_1$ and lock it in place. - **Step 4:** Release the pendulum; it swings down under gravity and strikes the bumper at its lowest point. - **Step 5:** Record the impact velocity $v_{imp}$ using a velocity sensor or from kinematics. - **Step 6:** After impact, the pendulum continues its swing upward to a maximum height $h_2$ (residual height). - **Step 7:** Measure $h_2$ using an encoder, high-speed camera, or laser-based height sensor. - **Step 8:** Inspect the bumper for damage to headlamps (if integrated), fuel system, hood latches, and other safety systems. - **Step 9:** Calculate the energy absorbed $E_{abs} = m_p g (h_1 - h_2)$ and coefficient of restitution $e = \sqrt{h_2 / h_1}$. - **Step 10:** Compare results against the performance criteria of AIS-006 / ECE R42 for pass/fail decision. **Model Solution for Part (b) — Numerical Problem:** **Given:** - $m_p$ = 1100 kg - $h_1$ = 1.5 m - $h_2$ = 0.40 m - $g$ = 9.81 m/s² **(i) Impact velocity:** $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 g h_1} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5} $$ $$ v_{imp} = \sqrt{29.43} = 5.425 \text{ m/s} $$ **(ii) Energy absorbed by the bumper:** $$ E_{abs} = m_p g (h_1 - h_2) = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1.5 - 0.40) $$ $$ E_{abs} = 1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.10 = 11870.1 \text{ J} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{abs} \approx 11.87 \text{ kJ}} $$ **(iii) Coefficient of restitution:** $$ e = \sqrt{\frac{h_2}{h_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.40}{1.5}} = \sqrt{0.2667} $$ $$ \boxed{e \approx 0.5164} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]`** **(a)** Describe the **fixed collision barrier test procedure** for vehicle bumpers as per AIS-006. List the performance criteria used for pass/fail evaluation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1500 kg car impacts a rigid barrier at 4 km/h head-on. Calculate (i) the kinetic energy of the vehicle at impact, (ii) the average crushing force if the bumper crushes uniformly over 0.25 m, and (iii) the equivalent pendulum mass for a 60° corner impact at the same speed. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a) — Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure (AIS-006):** **1. Test Site & Equipment:** - A test track with a towing mechanism (winch / propulsion system). - A rigid concrete barrier of mass > 70 tonnes, with face perpendicular to the direction of vehicle travel. - Optional: deformable element (honeycomb aluminium) attached to the barrier face for compatibility testing. - High-speed cameras, accelerometers, and damage inspection tools. **2. Test Conditions:** - Test speed: 4 km/h (= 1.11 m/s) for passenger cars per AIS-006. - Approach angle: 0° (head-on) and 60° (corner impact) per ECE R42. - Vehicle condition: Curb weight + 75 kg driver dummy or equivalent ballast. - Ambient temperature: 20 ± 4°C. **3. Step-by-Step Procedure:** - **Step 1:** Prepare the vehicle to curb weight + 75 kg dummy mass. Inflate tyres to recommended pressure; check all fluid levels. - **Step 2:** Position the vehicle at a sufficient distance from the barrier such that it reaches the prescribed test speed before impact. - **Step 3:** Use a tow cable or propulsion system to bring the vehicle to exactly 4 km/h at the moment of impact. Engine is OFF during the tow (engine braking eliminated). - **Step 4:** Release the tow cable just before impact so the vehicle is in free-flight. - **Step 5:** Record the impact event using high-speed cameras and onboard accelerometers. - **Step 6:** Conduct post-test inspection of all safety-relevant systems as per AIS-006 criteria. **4. Performance Criteria (Pass/Fail):** - (i) No damage impairing luminous function of headlamps, tail lamps, indicators. - (ii) Hood, doors, and tailgate must remain operable. - (iii) No fuel system leakage or rupture. - (iv) Steering, braking, and exhaust systems must remain functional. - (v) Maximum bumper deflection within manufacturer-declared envelope. **Model Solution for Part (b) — Numerical Problem:** **Given:** - $m_v$ = 1500 kg - $v$ = 4 km/h = 1.111 m/s - $\delta_{max}$ = 0.25 m - $\theta$ = 60° (corner impact) **(i) Kinetic energy of vehicle:** $$ KE = \frac{1}{2} m_v v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (1.111)^2 $$ $$ KE = 750 \cdot 1.234 = 925.9 \text{ J} $$ $$ \boxed{KE \approx 0.926 \text{ kJ}} $$ **(ii) Average crushing force:** Assuming all kinetic energy is absorbed by the bumper crush: $$ E_{crush} = KE = 925.9 \text{ J} = \bar{F} \cdot \delta_{max} $$ $$ \bar{F} = \frac{925.9}{0.25} $$ $$ \boxed{\bar{F} = 3703.6 \text{ N} \approx 3.70 \text{ kN}} $$ **(iii) Equivalent pendulum mass for 60° corner impact:** $$ m_{eq} = m_v \cdot \sin^2\theta = 1500 \cdot \sin^2(60°) $$ $$ m_{eq} = 1500 \cdot (0.8660)^2 = 1500 \cdot 0.75 $$ $$ \boxed{m_{eq} = 1125 \text{ kg}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Pitfall 1:** Students frequently forget to **square the sine** in the corner impact equivalent mass formula. It is $m_{eq} = m_v \sin^2\theta$, NOT $m_v \sin\theta$. Loss of 2 marks. > - **Pitfall 2:** For the pendulum test, students often write $v_{imp} = g h_1$ instead of $v_{imp} = \sqrt{2 g h_1}$. The square root is essential — it comes from energy conservation $mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. > - **Pitfall 3:** The coefficient of restitution is **always $\leq 1$** for any real bumper. If your answer gives $e > 1$, you have made a calculation error (typically mis-identifying $h_1$ and $h_2$). > - **Pitfall 4:** Do NOT confuse **kN** with **kJ**. Energy and force have different units; in the crush problem, the area under the F-d curve gives energy, not force. > - **Pitfall 5:** Always state the **relevant standard** (AIS-006, ECE R42, FMVSS 581) when discussing bumper tests. Examiners award marks for correct regulatory references. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bumpers Module** - ⭐ **Bumper = Energy Absorber, NOT a Rebounder.** Design objective: minimize $e$, maximize $E_{abs}$. - ⭐ **Five components:** Fascia, Energy Absorber, Reinforcement Bar, Brackets, Pedestrian Pad. - ⭐ **Three test types:** Pendulum, Fixed Barrier, Hydraulic Ram. - ⭐ **Pendulum formula:** $v_{imp} = \sqrt{2gh_1}$, $E_{abs} = m_p g (h_1 - h_2)$, $e = \sqrt{h_2/h_1}$. - ⭐ **Corner impact equivalent mass:** $m_{eq} = m_v \sin^2\theta$ (note the **square**). - ⭐ **Crush energy:** $E_{crush} = \int F \, d\delta$ = area under F-d curve. - ⭐ **Pendulum equivalent mass:** ≈ 1100 kg (regulatory standard, matches corner-impact equivalent). - ⭐ **AIS-006 test speed:** 4 km/h (1.11 m/s) for passenger cars. - ⭐ **ECE R42 striker:** Spherical, 220 mm radius. - ⭐ **Pass criteria:** No damage to lamps, fuel system, latches, steering, brakes, exhaust. - ⭐ **Pedestrian lower legform limit:** Force ≤ 6.0 kN (AIS-100). - ⭐ **Stiffness target:** Average crush force ≈ 10–20 kN for low-speed 4 km/h impacts. - ⭐ **Best materials for energy absorber:** EPP foam (specific energy absorption 15–25 kJ/kg) and aluminium honeycomb. - ⭐ **Units to remember:** J (energy), N or kN (force), m (deflection), m/s (velocity), dimensionless (coefficient of restitution). - ⭐ **Regulatory documents to cite:** AIS-006, ECE R42, FMVSS 581, AIS-100, GTR-9.

topics.title: Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAUT831 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Air # MODULE 4: AIR IN VEHICLE PERFORMANCE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aerodynamic Drag (Air Resistance):** Aerodynamic drag is the resistive force exerted by air on a vehicle moving through it, acting parallel to the direction of motion and opposite to the velocity vector. It is a critical resistive force that governs the maximum attainable speed, fuel economy, and overall energy efficiency of ground vehicles. In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, the term **"Air"** primarily refers to the interaction between the vehicle body and the surrounding atmospheric air mass, producing the **aerodynamic drag force ($F_d$)** which directly opposes vehicle motion. The general equation for aerodynamic drag is: $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$ Where: - $\rho$ = Mass density of air (kg/m³) - $C_D$ = Coefficient of drag (dimensionless) - $A_f$ = Frontal projected area (m²) - $V$ = Velocity of vehicle relative to air (m/s) > [!NOTE] > **Standard Atmospheric Reference:** At **Standard Sea Level Conditions (15°C, 101.325 kPa)**, the density of air is **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. This value is the KTU-approved reference for all vehicle performance calculations unless otherwise specified. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are running through a swimming pool versus running on a dry track. The water resists your motion strongly — that resistance is analogous to **air drag**, but with air being ~800 times less dense than water. At low speeds (walking), you barely feel air. But as speed increases, the resistance grows **quadratically** ($V^2$), so doubling your speed quadruples the drag force, and the power needed to overcome it grows **cubically** ($V^3$) — meaning at 200 km/h, an engine must produce **8 times more power** just to fight air than at 100 km/h! > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Insight:** At highway speeds (above 80 km/h), aerodynamic drag accounts for **~50–60% of the total resistive forces** acting on a passenger car, making it the dominant loss mechanism — even more significant than rolling resistance. ### 1.3 Standard Air Properties (KTU Reference Data) | Property | Symbol | Value at Sea Level | Units | |----------|--------|--------------------|-------| | Air density | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ | | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ | Pa·s | | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s | | Speed of sound | $a$ | 340.3 | m/s | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Breakdown of Aerodynamic Drag Components A moving vehicle experiences drag from **four principal sources**, each contributing to the total drag coefficient $C_D$: ### 2.1.1 Form Drag (Pressure Drag) — $C_{D,\text{form}}$ - Caused by **pressure differential** between the front (high pressure) and rear (low pressure) of the vehicle. - For **bluff bodies** (square-back trucks, buses), this is the **dominant** component. - Reduced by **streamlining** (tapering the rear). ### 2.1.2 Skin Friction Drag — $C_{D,\text{friction}}$ - Caused by **viscous shear** of air layers along the body surface. - Depends on **Reynolds Number (Re)** and surface roughness. - Governed by the **boundary layer** behavior. ### 2.1.3 Induced Drag — $C_{D,\text{induced}}$ - Generated due to **lift** production (relevant for wings, spoilers, vehicle crosswinds). - Significant for heavily **raked windshields** or asymmetric flow. ### 2.1.4 Internal / Parasitic Drag — $C_{D,\text{internal}}$ - Airflow through **radiator, engine bay, brakes, and cabin vents**. - Cannot be eliminated but can be optimized via grille shutters and underbody management. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total Drag Coefficient (KTU High-Yield Relation):** > $$C_D = C_{D,\text{form}} + C_{D,\text{friction}} + C_{D,\text{induced}} + C_{D,\text{internal}}$$ ## 2.2 Reynolds Number — Boundary Layer Classifier The **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces and is essential in vehicle wind-tunnel testing: $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L}{\mu} = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$ Where $L$ = characteristic length (typically wheelbase or body length, in meters). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examination Note:** Always mention the characteristic length used (e.g., $L$ = body length for cars, $L$ = chord length for wings). For a typical sedan at 100 km/h, $Re \approx 1.5 \times 10^7$, indicating fully **turbulent flow**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N | | 2 | $P_d = F_d \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^3$ | Power to overcome drag | W | | 3 | $Re = \tfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 4 | $C_D = \tfrac{2F_d}{\rho A_f V^2}$ | Drag coefficient (experimental) | dimensionless | | 5 | $A_f = \tfrac{2F_d}{\rho C_D V^2}$ | Frontal area (back-calculated) | m² | | 6 | $P_{\text{hp}} = \tfrac{F_d V}{745.7}$ | Power in horsepower | hp | | 7 | $V_{\text{max}} = \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2P}{\rho C_D A_f}}$ | Theoretical max velocity | m/s | | 8 | $F_{\text{total}} = F_d + F_r + F_g + F_i$ | Total tractive resistance | N | | 9 | $\nu = \tfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive OEMs** use wind-tunnel and CFD testing to reduce $C_D$ from ~0.45 (older sedans) to ~0.22 (Tesla Model S, modern EVs). - **Heavy trucks** use **side skirts and boat-tail rear extensions** to reduce $C_D A_f$ (drag-area product) by up to **15%**, directly saving diesel. - **Formula 1 cars** achieve $C_D \approx 0.7$ to 1.0 but generate **massive downforce** (negative lift) for cornering grip. - **Hyper-milers** exploit the $V^3$ power relationship by driving at lower speeds to dramatically extend EV range. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Aerodynamic Drag Force from Momentum Flux **Starting Principle:** Apply Newton's second law to a control volume of air being displaced by the moving vehicle. **Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:** A vehicle moving at velocity $V$ through still air is equivalent (by Galilean relativity) to air flowing past a stationary vehicle at velocity $V$. Consider a control volume enclosing the vehicle. **Step 2 — Identify Mass Flow Rate:** The mass of air interacting with the vehicle per unit time is determined by the **frontal area** $A_f$ and the air density $\rho$: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_f \cdot V$$ **Step 3 — Apply Momentum Theorem:** By Newton's second law, force equals the rate of change of momentum. The air's velocity is reduced from $V$ (upstream) to nearly $0$ near the vehicle's front stagnation point, then reaccelerates downstream: $$F_d = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta V = (\rho A_f V) \cdot V = \rho A_f V^2$$ **Step 4 — Introduce Empirical Drag Coefficient:** Real flow exhibits **separation, turbulence, and pressure recovery losses** that simple momentum theory cannot capture. We introduce the empirical **dimensionless drag coefficient $C_D$** to reconcile theory with experiment, and a factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arising from dynamic pressure definition: $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Origin of the $\tfrac{1}{2}$:** This factor emerges from the **dynamic pressure** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$, which represents the kinetic energy per unit volume of the airflow. Drag force is proportional to this pressure. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Required to Overcome Air Drag **Starting Principle:** Mechanical power is the dot product of force and velocity. **Step 1 — Recall Drag Force:** $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute Power as Force × Velocity:** $$P_d = F_d \cdot V = \left(\frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V^2\right) \cdot V$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$\boxed{P_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V^3}$$ **Step 4 — Express in Horsepower (KTU Required Form):** Since $1 \text{ hp} = 745.7 \text{ W}$: $$P_d \text{ (hp)} = \frac{\rho C_D A_f V^3}{2 \times 745.7} = \frac{\rho C_D A_f V^3}{1491.4}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Maximum Vehicle Velocity **Starting Principle:** At $V_{\max}$, the available engine power $P_{\text{engine}}$ equals the sum of all resistive powers (air drag + rolling resistance + grade + inertia). For level road, constant speed: **Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition:** $$P_{\text{engine}} = P_d + P_r + P_g$$ **Step 2 — Neglect rolling and grade for simplification (level road):** $$P_{\text{engine}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_{\max}^3$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_{\max}$:** $$V_{\max}^3 = \frac{2 P_{\text{engine}}}{\rho C_D A_f}$$ $$\boxed{V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 P_{\text{engine}}}{\rho C_D A_f}}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem) **Problem:** A passenger car has the following specifications: - $C_D = 0.30$ - $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$ - $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Engine power $P = 110 \text{ kW} = 110{,}000 \text{ W}$ **Find:** (a) Drag force at $V = 100$ km/h, (b) Power to overcome drag at the same speed, (c) Theoretical $V_{\max}$. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Convert velocity: $V = 100 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{100{,}000}{3600} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$. $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^2$$ **Compute step-by-step:** $$(27.78)^2 = 771.6$$ $$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 771.6$$ $$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$ $$0.6125 \times 0.30 = 0.18375$$ $$0.18375 \times 2.2 = 0.40425$$ $$0.40425 \times 771.6 = 311.9 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_d \approx 312 \text{ N}}$$ **Part (b):** $$P_d = F_d \times V = 311.9 \times 27.78 = 8664 \text{ W} \approx 8.66 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification using direct formula:** $$P_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^3$$ $$(27.78)^3 = 21{,}435$$ $$P_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 21{,}435 = 8664 \text{ W} \approx 8.66 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_d \approx 8.66 \text{ kW} \approx 11.6 \text{ hp}}$$ **Part (c):** Theoretical $V_{\max}$: $$V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 \times 110{,}000}{1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2}}$$ **Denominator:** $$1.225 \times 0.30 = 0.3675$$ $$0.3675 \times 2.2 = 0.8085$$ $$\frac{220{,}000}{0.8085} = 272{,}097$$ $$V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{272{,}097} = 64.84 \text{ m/s}$$ **Convert to km/h:** $V_{\max} = 64.84 \times 3.6 = 233.4 \text{ km/h}$. $$\boxed{V_{\max} \approx 233.4 \text{ km/h}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Note:** Actual $V_{\max}$ is always lower than theoretical because rolling resistance, drivetrain losses, and grade resistance are neglected. A typical 110 kW sedan achieves ~200 km/h in practice. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensitivity of $F_d$ and $P_d$ to Velocity | $V$ (km/h) | $V$ (m/s) | $F_d$ (N) | $P_d$ (kW) | % of 110 kW | |------------|-----------|-----------|------------|-------------| | 40 | 11.11 | 49.6 | 0.55 | 0.5% | | 80 | 22.22 | 198.4 | 4.41 | 4.0% | | 120 | 33.33 | 446.4 | 14.88 | 13.5% | | 160 | 44.44 | 793.6 | 35.27 | 32.1% | | 200 | 55.56 | 1240.0 | 68.89 | 62.6% | | 233.4 | 64.84 | 1686.5 | 109.36 | 99.4% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight from Table:** The cubic relationship is brutally evident — at 200 km/h, **62.6% of the engine's power** is consumed just fighting air! # 4. via Frontal Photograph] F --> G[Final CD Value
Report to KTU Lab] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define aerodynamic drag. Write the expression for the aerodynamic drag force acting on a moving vehicle and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** Aerodynamic drag is the resistive force offered by air to a vehicle moving through it, acting opposite to the direction of motion. The expression is: $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V^2$$ - $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³) - $C_D$ = coefficient of drag (dimensionless, body shape factor) - $A_f$ = frontal projected area (m²) - $V$ = vehicle velocity (m/s) The drag force varies with the **square of velocity**, making it dominant at high speeds. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q2. What is the coefficient of drag ($C_D$)? Mention the typical $C_D$ values for (i) a modern sedan, (ii) a Formula 1 car, and (iii) a heavy truck.** **Model Answer:** The **coefficient of drag ($C_D$)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies how "slippery" or "streamlined" a body is. It is defined as the ratio of actual drag force to the dynamic pressure times frontal area. $$C_D = \frac{2 F_d}{\rho A_f V^2}$$ | Vehicle Type | Typical $C_D$ | |--------------|---------------| | Modern sedan (e.g., Tesla Model S) | 0.20 – 0.25 | | Formula 1 car | 0.70 – 1.00 (high due to downforce-generating wings) | | Heavy truck (conventional) | 0.60 – 0.80 | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** ### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expression for the **aerodynamic drag force** on a moving vehicle from first principles using the momentum-flux approach. Clearly state the role of the drag coefficient $C_D$ in the final expression. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A car travels at **120 km/h**. The drag coefficient is $C_D = 0.32$, frontal area is $A_f = 2.1 \text{ m}^2$, and air density is $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) The drag force experienced by the car. - (ii) The power required to overcome this drag. - (iii) The theoretical maximum speed if the engine delivers 90 kW. **Model Solution for (a):** Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above. The student should: 1. Define control volume and reference frame. 2. Compute mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho A_f V$. 3. Apply momentum change: $F_d = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta V = \rho A_f V^2$. 4. Introduce $C_D$ and the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor from dynamic pressure. 5. Final form: $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$. **Model Solution for (b):** **Convert velocity:** $V = 120 \text{ km/h} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$. **(i) Drag force:** $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.1 \times (33.33)^2$$ $$(33.33)^2 = 1110.9$$ $$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.1 \times 1110.9$$ $$F_d = 0.6 \times 0.32 \times 2.1 \times 1110.9 = 0.192 \times 2.1 \times 1110.9$$ $$F_d = 0.4032 \times 1110.9 = 447.9 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_d \approx 448 \text{ N}}$$ **(ii) Power required:** $$P_d = F_d \times V = 447.9 \times 33.33 = 14{,}929 \text{ W} \approx 14.93 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_d \approx 14.93 \text{ kW} \approx 20 \text{ hp}}$$ **(iii) Theoretical $V_{\max}$:** $$V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 \times 90{,}000}{1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.1}}$$ **Denominator:** $1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.1 = 0.8064$. $$\frac{180{,}000}{0.8064} = 223{,}214$$ $$V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{223{,}214} = 60.65 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_{\max} = 60.65 \times 3.6 = 218.3 \text{ km/h}$$ $$\boxed{V_{\max} \approx 218.3 \text{ km/h}}$$ --- ### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) **(a)** Explain the **different components of aerodynamic drag** (form drag, skin friction, induced drag, and internal drag) with suitable diagrams. Discuss how vehicle streamlining reduces each component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A modern EV has the following data: $C_D = 0.22$, $A_f = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, mass $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The vehicle is cruising at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a level road. If the coefficient of rolling resistance is $f_r = 0.012$, calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) The aerodynamic drag force. - (ii) The rolling resistance force. - (iii) The total tractive force required. - (iv) The total power required at the wheels. **Model Solution for (a):** Refer to **Section 2.1** above. Student should: 1. Define each drag component (form, skin friction, induced, internal). 2. Mention their typical contribution to total $C_D$ for a sedan: - Form: ~55–60% - Skin friction: ~25–30% - Induced: ~5–10% - Internal: ~10–15% 3. Explain streamlining techniques: tapered rear (reduces form), smooth paint (reduces skin friction), underbody panels (reduces internal), optimized spoilers (reduces induced). **Model Solution for (b):** **Convert velocity:** $V = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$. **(i) Aerodynamic drag:** $$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.22 \times 2.3 \times (25)^2$$ $$(25)^2 = 625$$ $$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.22 \times 2.3 \times 625$$ $$F_d = 0.6125 \times 0.22 = 0.13475$$ $$0.13475 \times 2.3 = 0.30993$$ $$0.30993 \times 625 = 193.7 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_d \approx 194 \text{ N}}$$ **(ii) Rolling resistance:** $$F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81$$ $$F_r = 0.012 \times 17{,}658 = 211.9 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_r \approx 212 \text{ N}}$$ **(iii) Total tractive force (level road, no grade, no acceleration):** $$F_t = F_d + F_r = 194 + 212 = 406 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_t \approx 406 \text{ N}}$$ **(iv) Total power at wheels:** $$P_t = F_t \times V = 406 \times 25 = 10{,}150 \text{ W} \approx 10.15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_t \approx 10.15 \text{ kW} \approx 13.6 \text{ hp}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s is the #1 mistake. Always write $V (\text{m/s}) = V (\text{km/h}) \div 3.6$ explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor:** Many students write $F_d = \rho C_D A_f V^2$ — this is **wrong** and loses 2 marks. > 3. **Using incorrect $\rho$:** Use $1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless the problem specifies another altitude/condition. > 4. **Confusing $C_D$ with $C_L$:** $C_D$ = drag coefficient (resistance), $C_L$ = lift coefficient (vertical force). They are **different**. > 5. **Neglecting $V^3$ in power:** Power to overcome drag is cubic, not quadratic. Writing $P = F_d$ without the $V$ multiplication is a common slip. > 6. **Forgetting to take cube root in $V_{\max}$:** The final step is $\sqrt[3]{ }$, not $\sqrt{ }$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):** - ✅ **Drag force equation:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^2$ — **quadratic in velocity** - ✅ **Power to overcome drag:** $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f V^3$ — **cubic in velocity** - ✅ **Standard air density (sea level):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - ✅ **Typical sedan $C_D$:** 0.25 – 0.35 - ✅ **Typical truck $C_D$:** 0.60 – 0.80 - ✅ **Typical F1 $C_D$:** 0.70 – 1.00 - ✅ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ — used to characterize flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent) - ✅ **Drag components:** Form + Skin friction + Induced + Internal - ✅ **Speed conversion:** 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h - ✅ **Power conversion:** 1 hp = 745.7 W - ✅ **$V_{\max}$ formula:** $V_{\max} = \sqrt[3]{2P / (\rho C_D A_f)}$ - ✅ **Total resistance:** $F_t = F_d + F_r + F_g + F_i$ - ✅ **Dominant at high speed:** Aerodynamic drag becomes the largest resistive force above ~80 km/h - ✅ **Engineering rule of thumb:** Halving $C_D A_f$ is equivalent to doubling engine power (for top speed) > **Final Mantra for KTU Exam:** > *"$F_d$ is quadratic, $P_d$ is cubic — Air drag is the silent killer of fuel economy and the gatekeeper of top speed."*
